<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q:** *"Define the firing interval of a multi-cylinder engine. Calculate the firing interval for a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **firing interval** is the angular displacement of the crankshaft (in degrees) between two successive power strokes in a multi-cylinder engine.

For a 4-stroke engine with $n$ cylinders, the working cycle spans **720°**, and one power stroke occurs per cylinder per cycle. Therefore:

$$
\theta_{\text{firing}} = \frac{720°}{n}
$$

For a **4-cylinder** engine:

$$
\theta_{\text{firing}} = \frac{720°}{4} = 180° \text{ of crank rotation}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the calculation. Missing the unit ("° of crank rotation") may cost 0.5 marks.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q:** *"List any three advantages of a multi-cylinder engine over a single-cylinder engine."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Smoother torque delivery** — With $n$ cylinders, the firing interval is $720°/n$, giving more frequent power strokes and reduced torque fluctuations.
2. **Better mechanical balance** — Multi-cylinder arrangements (I6, V12, Flat-4) can be inherently balanced, reducing vibrations.
3. **Higher power output for the same displacement** — Distributing displacement across multiple cylinders allows better breathing (volumetric efficiency) and reduced friction per cylinder.
4. *(Bonus point)* Lower flywheel mass required for the same smoothness.
5. *(Bonus point)* Lower exhaust pulsations and better scavenging.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Pattern:** Listing 3 correct advantages with brief one-line explanations fetches full 3 marks. Skipping the explanation line costs 0.5 marks per point.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as 7 + 7 between two sub-parts (a) and (b). You are expected to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**Q:** *"With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a single-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine. List its applications."*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A single-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine consists of the following major parts:

- **Cylinder head** — Houses the intake valve, exhaust valve, and spark plug.
- **Cylinder block** — The main bore with cooling jackets.
- **Piston** — Reciprocates inside the cylinder; connected to the connecting rod via the **gudgeon (wrist) pin**.
- **Connecting rod** — Transfers reciprocating motion of the piston to the rotary motion of the crankshaft.
- **Crankshaft** — Converts linear motion into rotation; supported by main bearings.
- **Flywheel** — Stores rotational kinetic energy; smooths out torque variations.
- **Valve mechanism** — Camshaft, rocker arms, pushrods, valve springs.
- **Ignition system** — Spark plug, ignition coil, distributor (or coil-on-plug).
- **Lubrication and cooling systems** — Oil pump, radiator, water pump, thermostat.

**Working (3 marks):**
The engine completes its cycle in **two crank revolutions (720°)** through four distinct strokes:

1. **Intake Stroke (0° to 180°)** — Piston moves from TDC to BDC. Intake valve opens, exhaust valve closed. Air–fuel mixture drawn in due to partial vacuum.
2. **Compression Stroke (180° to 360°)** — Both valves closed. Piston moves BDC to TDC, compressing the mixture (typically 8:1 to 12:1).
3. **Power Stroke (360° to 540°)** — Spark plug ignites mixture just before TDC. Combustion raises pressure sharply; high-pressure gases push piston to BDC.
4. **Exhaust Stroke (540° to 720°)** — Exhaust valve opens. Piston moves BDC to TDC, expelling burnt gases.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Motorcycles, scooters, mopeds
- Small portable generators
- Lawn mowers, chainsaws
- Low-power agricultural pumps and equipment

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Q:** *"A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 75 mm and a stroke of 90 mm. The engine runs at 3000 RPM with a mean effective pressure of 7.5 bar. Calculate: (i) Swept volume per cylinder, (ii) Total engine displacement in cc, (iii) Indicated power in kW."*

**Given:**
- $n = 4$, $D = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075$ m, $L = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.090$ m
- $N = 3000$ RPM, $P_m = 7.5 \text{ bar} = 7.5 \times 10^{5}$ N/m²
- 4-stroke engine → $K = 1/2$

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Swept volume per cylinder:**

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.075)^{2} \times 0.090
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.005625 \times 0.090 = 3.978 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} = 397.8 \text{ cc}
$$

**(ii) Total engine displacement:**

$$
V_{\text{total}} = n \times V_s = 4 \times 397.8 = 1591.2 \text{ cc} \approx 1.59 \text{ L}
$$

**(iii) Indicated power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i &= \frac{P_m \times L \times A \times n \times N \times K}{60} \\[6pt]
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.075)^{2} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\[6pt]
P_i &= \frac{(7.5 \times 10^{5}) \times 0.090 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 3000 \times 0.5}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(7.5 \times 10^{5}) \times 0.090 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 6000}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1.789 \times 10^{6}}{60} \approx 29.82 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_i \approx 29.82 \text{ kW} \approx 40 \text{ hp}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]

**Q:** *"Compare Inline, V, and Flat (Boxer) engine arrangements with respect to: (i) balance characteristics, (ii) packaging, (iii) manufacturing cost, (iv) typical applications. State at least two advantages and two disadvantages for each."*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Inline (I4/I6)** | **V Engine (V6/V8/V12)** | **Flat (Boxer)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Balance** | I6 inherently balanced; I4 needs balance shaft | V12-60° balanced; V8-90° needs balance shaft | Inherently balanced (all variants) |
| **Packaging** | Long but narrow → fits transverse FWD | Short but wide | Wide but very low height |
| **Cost** | Lowest (single head, simple tooling) | Higher (two heads, V-angle machining) | Highest (two heads, complex lubrication) |
| **Centre of Gravity** | Higher than boxer | Mid-height | Lowest (cylinders horizontal) |
| **Applications** | Maruti Swift, Hyundai Creta, Honda City | Ford Mustang, Audi A8, Ferrari | Subaru WRX, Porsche 911 |

**Inline Engines — Advantages:**
- Simple, cheap, compact in width
- Easy maintenance, transverse mounting

**Inline Engines — Disadvantages:**
- Long length at higher cylinder counts
- I4 has primary imbalance

**V Engines — Advantages:**
- Compact length, high power density
- Lower centre of gravity than inline

**V Engines — Disadvantages:**
- Wider engine bay required
- Two cylinder heads → more complex

**Flat (Boxer) Engines — Advantages:**
- Inherently balanced, very low CG, exceptional handling
- Compact length, good cooling airflow

**Flat (Boxer) Engines — Disadvantages:**
- Engine is wide → packaging issues
- Oil drainage in horizontal cylinders

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** 2 marks for comparison table, 2 marks each for advantages and disadvantages of all three arrangements (combined), 1 mark for application examples.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Q:** *"A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine develops 80 kW of brake power at 2000 RPM. The engine has a bore of 100 mm and a stroke of 120 mm. The mechanical efficiency is 85%. Find: (i) Brake mean effective pressure (BMEP), (ii) Indicated power, (iii) Firing interval."*

**Given:**
- $n = 6$, $N = 2000$ RPM, $P_b = 80$ kW
- $D = 0.100$ m, $L = 0.120$ m
- $\eta_{\text{mech}} = 0.85$, 4-stroke → $K = 1/2$

**Model Solution:**

**(i) BMEP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.100)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\[6pt]
P_b &= \frac{P_{bm} \times L \times A \times n \times N \times K}{60} \\[6pt]
80 \times 10^{3} &= \frac{P_{bm} \times 0.120 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 6 \times 2000 \times 0.5}{60} \\[6pt]
80 \times 10^{3} &= \frac{P_{bm} \times 0.120 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 6000}{60} \\[6pt]
80 \times 10^{3} &= P_{bm} \times 0.120 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \\[6pt]
80 \times 10^{3} &= P_{bm} \times 0.09425 \\[6pt]
P_{bm} &= \frac{80 \times 10^{3}}{0.09425} = 8.488 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/m}^{2} = 8.49 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Indicated Power:**

$$
P_i = \frac{P_b}{\eta_{\text{mech}}} = \frac{80}{0.85} = 94.12 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Firing Interval:**

$$
\theta_{\text{firing}} = \frac{720°}{n} = \frac{720°}{6} = 120° \text{ of crank rotation}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{bm} \approx 8.49 \text{ bar}, \quad P_i \approx 94.12 \text{ kW}, \quad \theta_{\text{firing}} = 120°}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors** — Mixing mm with m. Always convert bore and stroke to **metres** before substituting into SI power formulas. Losing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgot the constant K (½ for 4-stroke)** — Half the students forget this and get nearly double the correct power. Costs **3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing MEP with BMEP/IMEP** — $P_m$ in the formula is **mean effective pressure** (either IMEP or BMEP depending on indicated or brake power). Mixing them up is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Vague answers in Part A** — Writing "multi-cylinder is better" without specifying *which trade-off* (smoothness, balance, power) loses 1 mark per incomplete point.
> 5. **Skipping the Firing Interval definition** — In Q2(b), students often jump to calculation. The **1-mark definition** must precede the math.
> 6. **Confusing "Swept Volume" with "Clearance Volume"** — Swept = $\frac{\pi}{4}D^2 L$. Clearance is the volume above the piston at TDC. **High-frequency mistake.**
> 7. **Forgetting to divide by 60 (not 60,000)** — When the answer is required in **kW**, divide by **60**. When in **Watts**, divide by **60,000**. Wrong division = **wrong numerical answer**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Cylinder Arrangements):**

- ⭐ A **single-cylinder engine** has **one** power stroke every **720°** (4-stroke); needs a **large flywheel**.
- ⭐ **Firing interval** for 4-stroke = $720°/n$. For 2-stroke = $360°/n$.
- ⭐ **Swept volume per cylinder** $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L$; **Total displacement** = $n \times V_s$.
- ⭐ **Indicated Power** $P_i = \frac{P_m L A n N K}{60}$ (kW) with $K = 1$ (2-stroke) or $K = 1/2$ (4-stroke).
- ⭐ **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_{\text{mech}} = P_b / P_i$.
- ⭐ **Inherently balanced arrangements:** **I6, V12 (60°), Flat-4, Flat-6** — memorize these for the exam.
- ⭐ **Inline engines** — simple, cheap, transverse-mountable; preferred for FWD economy cars.
- ⭐ **V engines** — compact, high power density, but wider and heavier; preferred for RWD and high-performance vehicles.
- ⭐ **Flat (Boxer) engines** — lowest centre of gravity, inherent balance, exceptional handling; used by Porsche and Subaru.
- ⭐ **Radial engines** — obsolete, used in vintage aircraft, air-cooled, asymmetric firing causes vibration.
- ⭐ **W engines** — W12, W16 for ultra-luxury (Bugatti); compact packaging, very expensive.
- ⭐ **Opposed-piston** — two pistons per cylinder, no head; emerging for commercial-vehicle efficiency.
- ⭐ **Power strokes per second** in a 4-stroke engine = $\frac{nN}{120}$.
- ⭐ **Firing frequency** (Hz) = $\frac{N}{2n}$ for 4-stroke.
- ⭐ **Total engine displacement** is **the same** for a 3.0L I6 and a 3.0L V6 — arrangement doesn't change total swept volume.
- ⭐ **BMW's** 70+ year loyalty to I6 → inherent balance, RWD packaging, smoothness.
- ⭐ **V-angle** selection is **critical** for primary and secondary force cancellation in V engines.
- ⭐ **MEP** stands for **Mean Effective Pressure**; a **theoretical constant pressure** that, if acting on the piston throughout the power stroke, would produce the same work as the actual variable pressure cycle.
- ⭐ Always carry out **dimensional consistency** — convert mm to m, bar to N/m², RPM to rev/s (divide by 60) before substitution.
- ⭐ **Transverse mounting** (engine rotated 90°, crankshaft along the car's lateral axis) is feasible only for **inline engines with ≤4 cylinders** in most FWD layouts.
- ⭐ **Longitudinal mounting** (crankshaft along the car's longitudinal axis) is used for **I6, V8, V12, and most rear-wheel-drive platforms**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** In any cylinder-arrangement question, the examiner is testing **three things** — (1) can you identify the configuration, (2) can you compare its trade-offs, and (3) can you compute power/swept volume correctly. Practice all three on past KTU papers from **Dec 2022, May 2023, Dec 2023, May 2024, Dec 2024** for the highest score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bi Fuel & Dual Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET613 - AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bi Fuel & Dual Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bi-Fuel System — Formal Definition

A **Bi-Fuel Vehicle** is an automotive powertrain equipped with **two independent fuel storage and delivery systems** that supply *one* engine, but the engine operates on **only one fuel at any given instant**. The driver (or an electronic control unit) actively switches between the two fuels using a dashboard-mounted selector switch. 

In automotive practice, the most common bi-fuel combination is **Petrol (Gasoline) + CNG (Compressed Natural Gas)** or **Petrol + LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas)**, although **Petrol + Ethanol**, **Petrol + Hydrogen**, and **Diesel + CNG** variants also exist.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Wording)**
> A bi-fuel engine is a single-fuel-at-a-time, dual-storage system in which the engine is started on one fuel (typically petrol) and can be switched to the alternative fuel (CNG/LPG) while in motion, with the inactive fuel circuit remaining pressurized and ready.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Water-Bottle" Analogy

Imagine a traveler carrying **two water bottles** — one of normal water and one of electrolyte drink. At any given moment, the traveler drinks from **only one bottle**, but both bottles are sealed, full, and ready. If the primary bottle empties or the traveler feels tired, he simply *swaps the cap* and drinks from the other. He **never mixes** the two liquids in his mouth.

This is exactly how a bi-fuel car behaves: **two storage tanks, one engine, one fuel combusted at a time**, and the driver can swap at will.

---

## 1.2 Dual-Fuel System — Formal Definition

A **Dual-Fuel Engine** is a compression-ignition (diesel) engine in which **two fuels are injected and combusted simultaneously** in the same combustion chamber during the working cycle. The **primary fuel (usually diesel)** acts as a *pilot* to provide the ignition source, while the **secondary fuel (usually a gaseous fuel such as CNG, LPG, biogas, or producer gas)** forms the bulk of the energy input.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Wording)**
> A dual-fuel engine is a simultaneous-combustion system in which a high-cetane liquid pilot fuel ignites a low-cetane gaseous fuel admitted into the cylinder, achieving partial or near-total substitution of the liquid fuel by the gaseous fuel.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lighter + Bunsen Burner" Analogy

Think of lighting a large bonfire using only a matchstick. The matchstick (diesel pilot) provides the small initial flame, which in turn ignites the massive pile of dry leaves and twigs (CNG gas). Both the matchstick and the leaves **burn together** in the same instant. The match is small, but the leaves supply almost all the energy. 

This is dual-fuel: **two fuels, one combustion event, simultaneous participation**.

---

## 1.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Snapshot

| Parameter | Bi-Fuel | Dual Fuel |
|---|---|---|
| Number of fuels active per cycle | **One** | **Two** (pilot + main) |
| Switching mechanism | Manual / ECU selector | Continuous, simultaneous injection |
| Engine type | Spark-Ignition (Otto) base | Compression-Ignition (Diesel) base |
| Common fuel pair | Petrol + CNG / LPG | Diesel + CNG / Biogas |
| Pilot fuel purpose | None — only one fuel burns | Ignition source for gaseous fuel |
| Typical application | Passenger cars, 3-wheelers | Heavy trucks, buses, gensets |
| Fuel substitution | User choice (0% or 100%) | Engineer's setting (typically **60–90 %**) |

> [!NOTE] > **Memory Anchor for the Board Exam**
> *Bi = "One of Two"* (you pick). *Dual = "Two at Once"* (engine picks).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bi-Fuel System — Operating Logic

The bi-fuel architecture is a **retrofit-friendly** topology. A petrol vehicle is fitted with an additional gas train, while the original petrol system remains fully functional.

### 2.1.1 Functional Sequence of a Bi-Fuel Engine

1. **Cold Start on Petrol** — The ECU energizes the petrol injectors. Petrol is highly volatile (Reid Vapour Pressure ≈ **60–90 kPa** at 38 °C) and atomizes easily in cold conditions, so it provides reliable cold starts.
2. **Warm-Up Phase** — Engine continues on petrol until coolant temperature crosses a calibrated threshold, typically **15–30 °C**.
3. **Driver-Initiated Switchover** — The driver presses the fuel-selector switch. The petrol injectors de-energize, the petrol pump relay cuts off, and the **gas solenoid valve** opens to pressurize the gas rail.
4. **Gaseous-Phase Combustion** — The CNG/LPG regulator reduces cylinder pressure from **~20–25 MPa (CNG tank)** to **~0.4–0.7 MPa (rail pressure)**. The gas injector meters fuel into the intake manifold.
5. **Emergency Fallback** — If gas pressure drops below a critical threshold, the ECU automatically reverts to petrol to prevent stalling — a critical safety feature.

### 2.1.2 Why Bi-Fuel Needs a Petrol Sub-System

- CNG occupies a much larger volume per unit energy than petrol (lower energy density, **~50 MJ/kg** vs petrol's **~44 MJ/kg** but much lower volumetric density).
- CNG requires gaseous injection, which makes cold-start difficult because vaporization cooling can chill the manifold.
- A small petrol reserve guarantees **startability in all climates**.

> [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight**
> Bi-fuel is therefore best understood as a *petrol engine with an alternate gaseous appendage*, not a fundamentally new engine.

### 2.1.3 Components of a Bi-Fuel System

- High-pressure **CNG cylinder(s)** with safety burst disc and excess-flow valve.
- **Pressure regulator** (multi-stage: 20 MPa → 1.0 MPa → 0.5 MPa).
- **Solenoid valve** for cut-off (acts as the "fuel cock").
- **Gas rail** with rail-pressure sensor.
- **Gas injectors** (gas-rated solenoid or piezo injectors).
- **ECU** with bi-fuel calibration map.
- **Fuel selector switch** on the dashboard.
- **Petrol system** retained *in toto* (tank, pump, injectors, ECU maps).

---

## 2.2 Dual-Fuel System — Operating Logic

The dual-fuel architecture is **fundamentally a modified diesel engine**. The diesel mechanical injection pump is retained, and an additional gas admission system is added upstream of (or directly into) the cylinder.

### 2.2.1 Functional Sequence of a Dual-Fuel Engine

1. **Intake Stroke** — Air is drawn in through the intake manifold. Near the end of the intake stroke (or during compression), the **gaseous fuel** is admitted either:
   - Into the **intake port** (PFI — Port Fuel Injection of gas), **or**
   - Directly into the **cylinder** via a gas injector.
2. **Compression Stroke** — The air–gas mixture is compressed to a high ratio (typically **14:1 to 18:1**). Compression alone is insufficient to ignite CNG (high octane rating ~ 130), so auto-ignition does **not** occur.
3. **Pilot Diesel Injection** — Near top dead centre, a small quantity of **diesel (typically 10–30 % of total energy)** is injected at high pressure through the existing diesel injector.
4. **Combustion** — The diesel pilot ignites spontaneously (high cetane, ~ 50), and the resulting flame kernel propagates through the premixed air–gas charge. Combustion is **partially premixed, partially diffusive**.
5. **Exhaust** — Combustion products exit; turbocharger recovers energy.

### 2.2.2 Why Diesel Is Retained as Pilot

- Diesel has a **high cetane number**, so it auto-ignites reliably under high compression.
- Gaseous fuels (CNG, biogas) have **low cetane numbers** (< 30) and resist auto-ignition.
- Removing diesel entirely would force conversion to a spark-ignition architecture (loss of compression-ignition advantages).

### 2.2.3 Combustion Modes of a Dual-Fuel Engine

A dual-fuel engine can be operated in **three modes**:

- **Mode 1 — Pure Diesel Mode** (Diesel Substitution Ratio = 0 %): Used as a fallback if the gas supply fails. No efficiency loss, but no fuel-saving.
- **Mode 2 — Dual-Fuel Mode** (DSR = 60–90 %): Normal operation. The bulk of energy comes from gas, ignition from diesel pilot.
- **Mode 3 — Gas-Only Mode** (DSR = 100 %): Requires auxiliary ignition (spark plug or glow plug) — not all dual-fuel engines support this.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Sub-Components of Dual-Fuel Combustion**
> 1. **Premixed flame** of air + gas (fast, partially premixed).
> 2. **Diffusion flame** of diesel pilot (slow, controlled).
> 3. **Late-burn tail** in crevice and wall-quench regions.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> **Note on Table Formatting:** The symbol `\vert` is used in place of the vertical pipe character to comply with markdown table syntax.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diesel Substitution Ratio | $DSR$ | $DSR = \dfrac{m_{diesel,baseline} - m_{diesel,actual}}{m_{diesel,baseline}} \times 100$ | **%** | Indicates how much diesel is replaced by gas |
| 2 | Energy Substitution Ratio | $ESR$ | $ESR = \dfrac{E_{gas}}{E_{gas} + E_{diesel}} \times 100$ | **%** | Energy-basis substitution |
| 3 | Mass Substitution Ratio | $MSR$ | $MSR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{gas} + \dot{m}_{diesel}} \times 100$ | **%** | Mass-basis substitution |
| 4 | Lower Heating Value (CNG) | $LHV_{CNG}$ | Look-up constant | **MJ/kg** | $\approx 50$ |
| 5 | Lower Heating Value (Diesel) | $LHV_{d}$ | Look-up constant | **MJ/kg** | $\approx 42.5$ |
| 6 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric, CNG) | $AFR_{st,CNG}$ | Mass-based | — | $\approx 17.2 : 1$ |
| 7 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric, Diesel) | $AFR_{st,d}$ | Mass-based | — | $\approx 14.5 : 1$ |
| 8 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi$ | $\phi = \dfrac{(AFR)_{st}}{(AFR)_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | $\phi < 1$ lean; $\phi > 1$ rich |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol}$ | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{\rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp}}$ | **%** | Dropped in dual-fuel due to gas displacement |
| 10 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth}$ | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{P_{brake}}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV}$ | **%** | Increases with DSR up to a point |
| 11 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC$ | $SEC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV}{P_{brake}}$ | **kJ/kWh** | Lower is better |
| 12 | CNG Tank Pressure | $p_{tank}$ | Look-up constant | **MPa** | $20 - 25$ |
| 13 | Octane Number (CNG) | $ON_{CNG}$ | Property | — | $\approx 130$ (high knock resistance) |
| 14 | Cetane Number (CNG) | $CN_{CNG}$ | Property | — | $\approx 10 - 15$ (poor CI) |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility of these Equations**
> 1. **DSR** is the *first* quantity a KTU examiner will quote in a dual-fuel problem.
> 2. **ESR vs MSR distinction** catches many students — always convert using LHV.
> 3. **Equivalence ratio** determines whether combustion will be *knock-limited* (rich) or *misfire-limited* (lean) in dual-fuel mode.

---

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Fleet Operators (KSRTC, BEST, DTC buses)** buy dual-fuel buses to claim **20–30 % diesel replacement**; the bill of materials hinges on the actual *DSR achieved at part-load*.
- **CNG-kit installers in Kerala** must size the regulator based on the *highest expected gas mass-flow* computed from peak engine torque and AFR.
- **Emissions testing** at ARAI uses *ESR* (not MSR) to certify that CO₂ reductions are real.
- **Cold-start calibration** of bi-fuel vehicles uses *η_vol* to estimate manifold filling during cranking.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR) from Mass Flows

In a dual-fuel engine, two fuels enter the cylinder simultaneously. The total energy release per cycle is the sum of the energies of each fuel. We wish to find what *fraction* of that energy comes from the gaseous fuel.

### Step 1 — Define the instantaneous energy rates

Let $\dot{m}_{g}$ = mass flow rate of the gaseous fuel (kg/s) and $\dot{m}_{d}$ = mass flow rate of the diesel pilot (kg/s). Their lower heating values are $LHV_{g}$ and $LHV_{d}$ respectively, in J/kg.

The total power released as chemical energy in the cylinder is:

$$\dot{E}_{total} = \dot{m}_{g} \cdot LHV_{g} + \dot{m}_{d} \cdot LHV_{d}$$

### Step 2 — Express the gas-only energy share

The energy contribution of the gas alone is:

$$\dot{E}_{g} = \dot{m}_{g} \cdot LHV_{g}$$

### Step 3 — Form the ratio

By definition:

$$ESR = \dfrac{\dot{E}_{g}}{\dot{E}_{total}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{g} \cdot LHV_{g}}{\dot{m}_{g} \cdot LHV_{g} + \dot{m}_{d} \cdot LHV_{d}}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by 100 to obtain percentage

$$ESR \,\vert\, \% = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{g} \cdot LHV_{g}}{\dot{m}_{g} \cdot LHV_{g} + \dot{m}_{d} \cdot LHV_{d}} \times 100$$

This is the energy substitution ratio. It is the **correct metric for emissions and efficiency analysis** because it accounts for the energy density difference between the two fuels.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — ESR and DSR Computation

> **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> A dual-fuel engine consumes $0.4$ kg of CNG and $0.2$ kg of diesel per minute. The lower heating values are $LHV_{CNG} = 50$ MJ/kg and $LHV_{diesel} = 42.5$ MJ/kg. The same engine, running on pure diesel at the same load, would have consumed $1.0$ kg of diesel per minute. Compute (a) the Energy Substitution Ratio, (b) the Mass Substitution Ratio, and (c) the Diesel Substitution Ratio.

### Given Data

$$\dot{m}_{g} = 0.4 \;\; \text{kg/min}, \quad \dot{m}_{d} = 0.2 \;\; \text{kg/min}, \quad LHV_{g} = 50 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}, \quad LHV_{d} = 42.5 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{m}_{diesel,baseline} = 1.0 \;\; \text{kg/min}$$

### (a) Energy Substitution Ratio

Step 1 — Compute gas energy rate:

$$\dot{E}_{g} = 0.4 \times 50 = 20.0 \;\; \text{MJ/min}$$

Step 2 — Compute diesel energy rate (in dual mode):

$$\dot{E}_{d} = 0.2 \times 42.5 = 8.5 \;\; \text{MJ/min}$$

Step 3 — Total energy rate:

$$\dot{E}_{total} = 20.0 + 8.5 = 28.5 \;\; \text{MJ/min}$$

Step 4 — Form the ESR:

$$ESR = \dfrac{20.0}{28.5} \times 100 = 70.18 \;\%$$

### (b) Mass Substitution Ratio

Step 1 — Total mass flow:

$$\dot{m}_{total} = 0.4 + 0.2 = 0.6 \;\; \text{kg/min}$$

Step 2 — Form the MSR:

$$MSR = \dfrac{0.4}{0.6} \times 100 = 66.67 \;\%$$

### (c) Diesel Substitution Ratio

$$DSR = \dfrac{1.0 - 0.2}{1.0} \times 100 = 80 \;\%$$

> [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check & Interpretation**
> Notice that $DSR = 80\%$ but $ESR = 70.18\%$. The DSR overstates the *energy* replacement because it does not account for the higher LHV of CNG. The ESR is the more *honest* number to quote in a KTU answer script.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bi-Fuel vs Dual-Fuel Decision Grid

A frequently-tested KTU question is "When would you choose bi-fuel and when dual-fuel?" The decision matrix below is the model answer the examiner expects.

| Decision Criterion | Choose **Bi-Fuel** | Choose **Dual-Fuel** |
|---|---|---|
| Engine base available | Spark-ignition (petrol) | Compression-ignition (diesel) |
| Required range on a single tank-fill | Medium (taxi, family car) | Long-haul (bus, truck) |
| Driver must switch fuels? | **Yes**, by design | **No**, continuous dual injection |
| Cold-start ability on gas alone | Poor — petrol back-up | Diesel pilot handles cold-start |
| Diesel replacement achieved | 0 % (when on gas only) | **60 – 90 %** (continuous) |
| Refuelling infrastructure maturity | Wide (CNG pumps in Kerala) | Limited (fleet operators only) |
| Cost of conversion | Low (₹ 40,000 – 70,000) | High (₹ 4 – 8 lakh for full dual-fuel kit) |
| Typical Indian application | Maruti Wagon-R, Hyundai Santro, auto-rickshaws | Tata Star-bus EV series, Ashok-Leyland buses on CNG-diesel |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy & Emissions Outlook (2024–2030)**
> Kerala's *Kerala State Road Transport Corporation* (KSRTC) is expanding its CNG-diesel dual-fuel fleet as part of its carbon-neutrality roadmap. **Bi-fuel** will continue to dominate the *private passenger-car* segment, while **dual-fuel** will dominate *heavy commercial transport*.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Brake Thermal Efficiency Gain in Dual-Fuel Mode

The brake thermal efficiency in any engine is:

$$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{P_{b}}{\dot{m}_{f,total} \cdot LHV_{mix}}$$

For the dual-fuel mixture, an effective $LHV_{mix}$ is defined using mass-fraction-weighted averaging:

$$LHV_{mix} = x_{g} \cdot LHV_{g} + x_{d} \cdot LHV_{d}, \quad \text{where} \quad x_{g} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{g}}{\dot{m}_{g} + \dot{m}_{d}}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{bth,DF} = \dfrac{P_{b}}{(\dot{m}_{g} + \dot{m}_{d}) \, (x_{g} LHV_{g} + x_{d} LHV_{d})}$$

Compared to a pure-diesel baseline operating at the same load, the gain in $\eta_{bth}$ arises from:

1. **Higher octane gaseous fuel** allowing leaner combustion → reduced heat loss.
2. **Premixed gas-air charge** burning faster → closer to constant-volume cycle.
3. **Lower flame temperature in lean regions** → reduced dissociation losses.

The penalty arises from:

1. Reduced $\eta_{vol}$ due to gas displacing intake air.
2. **Knock-limited** operation at high loads.
3. **Misfire-limited** operation at very low loads (gas may fail to ignite).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bi-Fuel System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the functional interactions between the petrol sub-system and the CNG sub-system. The driver, ECU, and selector switch coordinate the routing of fuel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Fuel Selector Switch] --> B[Bi-Fuel ECU]
    B --> C{Petrol Mode Active?}
    C -- YES --> D[Petrol Pump Relay]
    C -- NO --> E[CNG Solenoid Valve]
    D --> F[Petrol Rail]
    F --> G[Petrol Injectors]
    G --> H[Intake Manifold]
    E --> I[CNG High Pressure Regulator]
    I --> J[CNG Low Pressure Regulator]
    J --> K[CNG Rail]
    K --> L[CNG Injectors]
    L --> H
    H --> M[Engine Cylinder]
    M --> N[Combustion and Power]
    B --> O[Rail Pressure Sensor CNG]
    O --> B
    B --> P[Coolant Temperature Sensor]
    P --> B
    B --> Q[Lambda Oxygen Sensor]
    Q --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Path `B → C → D → F → G → H` represents the **petrol route**.
- Path `B → C → E → I → J → K → L → H` represents the **CNG route**.
- Only one path is active at a time, controlled by the ECU branch `C`.
- The three feedback sensors `O, P, Q` continuously inform the ECU about rail pressure, coolant temperature, and exhaust oxygen content.

---

## 4.2 Dual-Fuel System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This diagram shows how air, gas, and diesel pilot converge inside the cylinder of a dual-fuel engine. Note the **simultaneous** participation of both fuels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Intake Filter] --> B[Turbocharger Compressor]
    B --> C[Charge Air Cooler]
    C --> D[Intake Manifold]
    D --> E[Engine Cylinder]
    F[CNG Tank High Pressure] --> G[CNG Pressure Regulator]
    G --> H[CNG Gas Injector Port]
    H --> E
    I[Diesel Fuel Tank] --> J[Diesel Transfer Pump]
    J --> K[Diesel Injection Pump]
    K --> L[Common Rail Diesel]
    L --> M[Diesel Injector]
    M --> E
    E --> N[Pilot Diesel Ignites Premixed Gas]
    N --> O[Combustion Pressure Rise]
    O --> P[Piston Downstroke Work Output]
    P --> Q[Crankshaft and Flywheel]
    E --> R[Exhaust Manifold]
    R --> S[Turbocharger Turbine]
    S --> B
    R --> T[Aftertreatment DOC DPF SCR]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Air path: `A → B → C → D → E`.
- CNG path: `F → G → H → E`.
- Diesel pilot path: `I → J → K → L → M → E`.
- All three streams converge at `E (Engine Cylinder)`.
- The combustion event at `N` is initiated by diesel and consumes both fuels.
- Exhaust energy at `S` drives the turbocharger, completing a closed loop back to the air intake.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Mode-Switching State Diagram

The state diagram below makes explicit the three operational states of a dual-fuel engine: pure diesel, dual fuel, and gas-only (spark-assisted).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ColdStart
    ColdStart --> PureDiesel : Pilot Diesel Only
    PureDiesel --> DualFuel : Load Increases and Gas Available
    DualFuel --> PureDiesel : Gas Pressure Lost
    DualFuel --> GasOnly : Spark Plug Enabled
    GasOnly --> DualFuel : Driver Override
    PureDiesel --> GasOnly : Emergency Mode
    GasOnly --> PureDiesel : Spark Plug Fault
```

> [!NOTE] > **Why this diagram is high-yield for KTU**
> Examiners often frame a 7-mark question as: *"Explain with a block diagram the various operating modes of a dual-fuel engine."* The state diagram above is a board-exam-ready response that scores full marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Distinguish between bi-fuel and dual-fuel engine systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bi-fuel engine** has two separate fuel storage systems but uses **only one fuel at a time**. The driver switches between them, typically between petrol and CNG. The two fuels **never burn together** in the same cycle.

A **dual-fuel engine** uses **two fuels simultaneously**. A small pilot quantity of diesel provides ignition for a bulk charge of gaseous fuel (CNG, biogas, LPG). Both fuels **burn together** in the same combustion event, achieving 60–90 % diesel substitution.

The fundamental difference is *time-multiplexing* (bi-fuel) versus *frequency-multiplexing* (dual-fuel) of the two energy sources.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Define Diesel Substitution Ratio (DSR) and state its typical range in a dual-fuel engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Diesel Substitution Ratio (DSR)** is defined as the mass fraction of baseline diesel consumption that has been replaced by the secondary gaseous fuel, expressed as a percentage:

$$DSR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{d,baseline} - \dot{m}_{d,actual}}{\dot{m}_{d,baseline}} \times 100 \;\%$$

In a properly calibrated dual-fuel engine, the DSR typically lies in the range of **60 % to 90 %**, with the upper limit governed by knock and misfire limits and the lower limit set by the need to maintain reliable pilot ignition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Question]**

> **(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a bi-fuel engine system. List the major components and explain the role of the fuel selector switch. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

A bi-fuel engine is a spark-ignition engine equipped with **two independent fuel systems** — a primary petrol system and a secondary gaseous (CNG/LPG) system. The driver selects the active fuel using a dashboard-mounted **fuel selector switch** that signals the **bi-fuel ECU**.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Petrol Tank] --> [Petrol Pump] --> [Petrol Filter] --> [Petrol Rail] --> [Petrol Injectors]
                                                                          |
                                                                          v
[CNG Cylinder] --> [CNG Regulator] --> [Solenoid Valve] --> [CNG Rail] --> [CNG Injectors]
                                                                          |
                                                                          v
                                                                    [Intake Manifold]
                                                                          |
                                                                          v
                                                                    [Engine Cylinder]
```

**Major Components and Roles:**

1. **CNG Cylinder** — Stores CNG at **20–25 MPa**. Equipped with a **burst disc** (ruptures at ~ 1.5× design pressure) and an **excess-flow valve** (closes automatically if flow rate exceeds a threshold, e.g., during a hose rupture).
2. **Pressure Regulator** (typically two-stage) — Reduces tank pressure (20 MPa) to a manageable rail pressure (0.4–0.7 MPa). Also includes a heat-exchange coil that uses engine coolant to prevent regulator freeze due to Joule–Thomson expansion cooling.
3. **Solenoid Valve** — Acts as the master fuel cock. Energized to allow gas flow, de-energized to cut it off completely.
4. **CNG Rail with Pressure Sensor** — Maintains constant rail pressure using an internal regulator diaphragm.
5. **CNG Injectors** — Gas-rated solenoid injectors with low dynamic flow (about **5–25 mg per pulse** per injector).
6. **Bi-Fuel ECU** — Hosts two separate fuel maps. Switches between them based on the selector-switch position and sensor inputs.
7. **Fuel Selector Switch** — A two-position rocker switch on the dashboard. In **Petrol** position, the ECU enables petrol injectors; in **CNG** position, it energizes the gas solenoid and disables petrol injectors. The switch position is read by the ECU as a digital input.
8. **Petrol System (retained)** — Tank, fuel pump, filter, rail, injectors remain operational as a fall-back.

**Working Sequence:**

- **Cold start** occurs on **petrol** because CNG has poor vaporization in cold intake manifolds.
- After coolant temperature reaches the **calibration threshold** (typically 20–30 °C), the driver can switch to **CNG**.
- On switching, the petrol injectors de-energize within one cycle, the gas solenoid opens, and gas injection begins on the next intake stroke.
- A small quantity of petrol is retained in the petrol rail to permit instant fallback in case of gas starvation.

**Role of the Fuel Selector Switch (1.5 marks):**

The selector switch is the **human–machine interface (HMI)** that allows the driver to consciously decide which fuel to use. It sends a digital high/low signal to the ECU, which then energizes the appropriate fuel rail and injectors. Modern vehicles augment the switch with **automatic fallback logic** that reverts to petrol if gas pressure falls below a critical threshold.

---

> **(b) Explain why petrol is retained as the back-up fuel in a bi-fuel system. Discuss the cold-start limitation of CNG. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Reasons for Retaining Petrol as Back-Up Fuel (3.5 marks):**

1. **Cold-Start Reliability** — Petrol atomizes readily at low temperatures due to its high volatility (RVP ≈ **60–90 kPa**). CNG, being already gaseous, releases very little vaporization energy to mix with intake air, and the Joule–Thomson cooling effect at the regulator can drop gas temperature by **40–60 °C**, leading to manifold ice formation and poor air-fuel mixing.
2. **Energy Density Compromise** — CNG occupies about **4× the volume** of petrol for the same energy. Without a petrol reserve, range in winter or in remote areas is severely limited.
3. **Safety Fallback** — If the CNG pressure drops, hose leaks develop, or the regulator ices up, the vehicle must still be drivable. Petrol provides a *guaranteed* secondary energy source.
4. **Regulatory Compliance** — Indian Motor Vehicle Rules and AIS standards implicitly require a back-up fuel for any retrofitted gas system, primarily to ensure vehicles are not stranded in case of gas-system failure.
5. **Calibration Simplicity** — A bi-fuel ECU can re-use the original petrol maps almost unchanged, reducing development cost.

**Cold-Start Limitation of CNG (3.5 marks):**

1. **Low Vaporization Energy** — Petrol releases about **350 kJ/kg** of latent heat during vaporization, which warms the incoming air-fuel mixture. CNG, being a pre-vaporized gas, contributes virtually **zero** vaporization energy, so the intake manifold remains cold.
2. **Joule–Thomson Effect** — When CNG expands from 20 MPa to ~ 0.5 MPa at the regulator, its temperature can drop by **40–60 °C**, potentially freezing any residual moisture in the regulator and blocking gas flow. This is the well-known *"regulator freeze"* problem.
3. **Poor Atomization on Cold Manifold** — Even with port injection, a cold manifold wall can cause partial condensation of the lubricating oil and create uneven cylinder-to-cylinder distribution.
4. **Lean Misfire at Cranking Speed** — At cranking RPM (200–300 rpm), the time available for mixture preparation is short, and any residual exhaust gas dilutes the charge further, leading to **misfire** if the engine is started on CNG alone.
5. **Mitigation Strategies**:
   - Electrically heated regulators.
   - Coolant-heated gas lines.
   - Twin-stage pressure reduction to limit Joule–Thomson drop.
   - ECU-enforced cold-start on petrol with **automatic switchover** only after warm-up.

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> In 7-mark sub-questions on bi-fuel, students often write about CNG storage and forget to discuss the **regulator freeze** and **vaporization energy** aspects. Mentioning these two technical points typically fetches the extra 1–2 marks that separate a 5 from a 7. Always conclude with a *mitigation* statement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

> **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a dual-fuel engine. Compare its combustion with that of a pure diesel engine. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

A **dual-fuel engine** is a compression-ignition (diesel) engine in which a **gaseous fuel** (CNG, LPG, biogas, or producer gas) is admitted along with intake air, and a **small pilot quantity of diesel** is injected near top dead centre to provide ignition.

**Block Diagram (text rendition):**

```
[Intake Air] --> [Turbocharger] --> [Charge Cooler] --> [Intake Manifold]
                                                                |
                                                  [CNG Regulator] --> [CNG Injector]
                                                                |
                                                                v
                                                         [Engine Cylinder]
                                                                ^
                                                                |
                                              [Diesel Pump] --> [Diesel Injector]

[Engine Cylinder] --> [Exhaust Manifold] --> [Turbocharger Turbine] --> [Exhaust]
```

**Working Sequence:**

1. **Intake Stroke** — Air (and sometimes CNG if port-injected) enter the cylinder.
2. **Compression Stroke** — The mixture is compressed to a pressure of about **35–45 bar** and a temperature of about **700–900 K**. This temperature is sufficient to ignite the diesel pilot, but **not** the gaseous fuel.
3. **Pilot Injection** — Near TDC, diesel (10–30 % of energy) is injected at high pressure (**600–2000 bar** through the common rail). It atomizes and **auto-ignites** due to its high cetane number (~ 50).
4. **Combustion** — The diesel flame kernel ignites the surrounding premixed air–gas mixture. Combustion proceeds as a **combined premixed + diffusion flame** for about **30–60 crank-angle degrees**.
5. **Expansion and Exhaust** — Piston descends, exhaust valve opens, gases are expelled.

**Comparison with Pure Diesel Combustion:**

| Aspect | Pure Diesel | Dual Fuel (Diesel + CNG) |
|---|---|---|
| Ignition source | Compression auto-ignition | Pilot diesel auto-ignition |
| Charge preparation | Fully diffusive | Partially premixed (gas) + diffusive (diesel) |
| Combustion duration | Long (controlled by injection rate) | Shorter (faster premixed burn) |
| Peak pressure location | 10–15° aTDC | 5–10° aTDC (more advanced) |
| Knock tendency | Low (only at start of injection) | High at rich conditions |
| NOx formation | High (high T, diffusive) | Lower at lean conditions; can rise if rich |
| Smoke (PM) | High (diffusion soot) | Lower (less diesel) |
| HC and CO | Low | Higher (misfire at low load) |
| Efficiency | High | Slightly higher at part load, lower at full load |

> **Key Insight (0.5 mark bonus):**
> Dual-fuel combustion is *partially premixed* and therefore has **higher peak pressure rise rates (dP/dθ)**, which can produce audible *diesel-knock*. This is why dual-fuel engines are often limited to **moderate loads** in commercial service.

---

> **(b) Define and derive the expressions for Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR) and Diesel Substitution Ratio (DSR). A dual-fuel engine consumes 0.5 kg of CNG and 0.15 kg of diesel per minute. The same engine on pure diesel would consume 1.2 kg of diesel per minute. Given LHV of CNG = 50 MJ/kg and LHV of diesel = 42.5 MJ/kg, calculate the ESR and DSR. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Definitions and Derivations (4 marks):**

**Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR):**
It is the ratio of the chemical energy supplied by the gaseous fuel to the total chemical energy supplied by both fuels, expressed as a percentage.

Let $\dot{m}_{g}$, $\dot{m}_{d}$ be mass flow rates (kg/s) of gas and diesel, with lower heating values $LHV_{g}$ and $LHV_{d}$ (J/kg).

Energy rate of gas: $\dot{E}_{g} = \dot{m}_{g} \cdot LHV_{g}$

Energy rate of diesel: $\dot{E}_{d} = \dot{m}_{d} \cdot LHV_{d}$

Total energy rate: $\dot{E}_{total} = \dot{E}_{g} + \dot{E}_{d}$

Therefore:

$$ESR = \dfrac{\dot{E}_{g}}{\dot{E}_{total}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{g} \, LHV_{g}}{\dot{m}_{g} \, LHV_{g} + \dot{m}_{d} \, LHV_{d}} \times 100 \;\%$$

**Diesel Substitution Ratio (DSR):**
It is the ratio of the mass of diesel *saved* (compared to a pure-diesel baseline at the same load) to the mass of diesel the engine *would have consumed* on pure diesel.

$$DSR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{d,baseline} - \dot{m}_{d,actual}}{\dot{m}_{d,baseline}} \times 100 \;\%$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

**Given:**

$$\dot{m}_{g} = 0.5 \;\; \text{kg/min}, \quad \dot{m}_{d} = 0.15 \;\; \text{kg/min}, \quad \dot{m}_{d,baseline} = 1.2 \;\; \text{kg/min}$$

$$LHV_{g} = 50 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}, \quad LHV_{d} = 42.5 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 1 — Energy rate of CNG:**

$$\dot{E}_{g} = 0.5 \times 50 = 25.0 \;\; \text{MJ/min}$$

**Step 2 — Energy rate of diesel (in dual mode):**

$$\dot{E}_{d} = 0.15 \times 42.5 = 6.375 \;\; \text{MJ/min}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy rate:**

$$\dot{E}_{total} = 25.0 + 6.375 = 31.375 \;\; \text{MJ/min}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ESR:**

$$ESR = \dfrac{25.0}{31.375} \times 100 = 79.68 \;\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute DSR:**

$$DSR = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.15}{1.2} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.05}{1.2} \times 100 = 87.5 \;\%$$

**Interpretation (0.5 mark):**
The DSR (87.5 %) is *higher* than the ESR (79.68 %) because the baseline mass-based comparison does not credit the higher energy content of CNG. A well-calibrated dual-fuel engine in this configuration saves about **79.7 % of the chemical energy** that would otherwise come from diesel — a very strong result for a heavy-duty bus or truck.

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> Two common errors in numerical dual-fuel problems:
> 1. Students use *Mass Substitution Ratio* (MSR) instead of ESR. Read the question carefully.
> 2. Students forget to multiply by 100 and write the answer as a *decimal*. KTU board examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for a missing "%" symbol.
> 3. Students do not write the *units* of LHV. Always quote **MJ/kg** or **kJ/kg** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Board-Exam Ready)**

- **Bi-Fuel = One of Two, Dual-Fuel = Two at Once.** This single sentence resolves 80 % of confusion in viva and KTU exams.
- Bi-fuel is a **petrol engine with a gaseous appendage**; dual-fuel is a **diesel engine with a gaseous supplement**.
- Bi-fuel: **manual or ECU-assisted switchover**, only one fuel active at a time.
- Dual-fuel: **simultaneous injection**, diesel pilot (10–30 %) ignites premixed gaseous fuel.
- **Pilot fuel purpose in dual-fuel** = provide ignition; pilot is *not* optional in basic dual-fuel.
- **Cold-start is always on petrol in bi-fuel** because of CNG's poor vaporization and Joule–Thomson cooling at the regulator.
- **Diesel Substitution Ratio (DSR)** is mass-based and overstates the actual energy replacement.
- **Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR)** is energy-based and is the *honest* metric for emissions and efficiency reporting.
- Typical dual-fuel DSR range: **60 % to 90 %**.
- Typical bi-fuel applications: **passenger cars, auto-rickshaws** in Indian cities.
- Typical dual-fuel applications: **buses, trucks, gensets**.
- **Components of a bi-fuel gas train:** CNG cylinder, regulator, solenoid, gas rail, gas injectors, ECU, selector switch.
- **Components of a dual-fuel gas train:** Gas regulator, gas injector (port or direct), and a **retained diesel injection system**.
- **Key combustions phases in dual-fuel:** pilot ignition → premixed burn → diffusion burn → late burn.
- **Major drawback of dual-fuel:** *Knock-limited* at high load, *misfire-limited* at low load.
- **Major drawback of bi-fuel:** Limited range on gas; reduced boot space due to gas cylinder.
- **Efficiency gain mechanism in dual-fuel:** leaner combustion, less dissociation loss, more premixed burning.
- **Efficiency loss mechanism in dual-fuel:** reduced volumetric efficiency, gas displaces air.
- **Always quote units** in numerical answers: kg/min, MJ/kg, %, MPa.
- **Drawing a block diagram** in the KTU answer script is worth **at least 1–2 marks** in any bi-fuel or dual-fuel question — never skip it.

> **End of Topic — Bi-Fuel & Dual-Fuel. Module 1, OEMET613.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latest Technologies in ICEs - Flex Fuel Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET613 - AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Latest Technologies in ICEs - Flex Fuel Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Latest Technologies in ICEs
## Topic: Flex Fuel Vehicles (FFVs)

---

### 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (As per 2024 Scheme OEMET613 syllabus):**
> A **Flex Fuel Vehicle (FFV)** is an alternate-fuel vehicle equipped with an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) that has been specifically calibrated to operate on more than one fuel source — typically **gasoline**, **ethanol**, or any **intermediate gasoline–ethanol blend** (ranging from pure gasoline E0 to pure hydrous ethanol E100) — within a single, shared fuel system, **without any manual switching** by the driver.

The vehicle detects the **volumetric percentage of ethanol** in the fuel through an on-board **fuel composition sensor** and dynamically recalibrates the **engine control parameters** — primarily injection pulse width, ignition timing, and idle speed — to maintain **stoichiometric combustion** across the entire blend range.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Bilingual Chef":**
> Imagine a chef who is fluent in two cuisines: North Indian and South Indian. When the customer orders any dish from either cuisine, the chef doesn't ask for switching time — he instantly reads the order, picks the right spice proportions, adjusts cooking temperature, and serves it perfectly.
>
> In the same way, an FFV "reads" the fuel coming in (using the composition sensor), instantly decides how much ethanol is mixed in, and "cooks" the mixture optimally in the combustion chamber by adjusting injector timing, fuel quantity, and spark advance — **all in real time, automatically, blend-by-blend**.

---

### 1.3 Why FFVs Matter in the 2024 Automotive Industry

| Driver | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| **Energy Security** | Reduces dependence on imported crude oil |
| **Renewable Fuel Integration** | Ethanol is bio-derived; closes the **carbon cycle** |
| **Emission Compliance** | Meets **BS-VI Phase 2** and future **Euro 7** norms |
| **BS-VI / CAFÉ Norms** | India targeting **20% ethanol blending (E20)** by 2025–26 under the **Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme** |
| **CO₂ Reduction** | Ethanol molecule carries **35% oxygen**, enabling leaner combustion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant to Remember:**
> The **stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** shifts dramatically with ethanol content: pure gasoline = **14.7 : 1** by mass, pure ethanol = **9.0 : 1** by mass. An FFV's job is to manage this entire **9.0 to 14.7** range continuously.

---

### 1.4 FFV vs Conventional Bi-Fuel Vehicle

| Parameter | Conventional Bi-Fuel (CNG/Petrol) | Flex Fuel Vehicle (FFV) |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel storage | **Two separate tanks** | **Single tank** for blended fuel |
| Switching | **Manual or solenoid** | **Automatic, blend-aware** |
| Fuel range | Discrete (Petrol OR CNG) | Continuous (E0 → E100) |
| Sensor needed | Pressure regulators, solenoid valves | **Fuel composition sensor** |
| ECU strategy | Two fixed maps | **Adaptive multi-fuel map** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

### 2.1 Architecture of a Flex Fuel System — Subsystem Breakdown

The FFV powertrain has **six coordinated sub-systems**:

#### (a) Fuel Storage & Delivery Subsystem
- **Single pressurized fuel tank** made of **corrosion-resistant stainless steel or polymer-coated mild steel** (since ethanol is hygroscopic and mildly corrosive).
- **Higher-capacity in-tank fuel pump** delivers up to **30–50% more volumetric flow** because ethanol has lower volumetric energy density than gasoline.
- Ethanol-compatible **fuel lines, seals, and O-rings** made of **Viton (FKM) or Teflon (PTFE)** instead of nitrile rubber.

#### (b) Fuel Composition Detection Subsystem
- **Fuel composition sensor (FCS)** is the **heart of the FFV**.
- It works on the principle of **dielectric constant (permittivity) measurement**:
  - Gasoline: ε_r ≈ 2.0–2.3
  - Pure ethanol: ε_r ≈ 24.3
- The sensor measures the **capacitance** of the fuel passing between two electrodes; capacitance is directly proportional to the ethanol fraction.
- The measured voltage is fed to the **ECU** as a **PWM signal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The dielectric constant difference (≈22 units between gasoline and ethanol) is what makes reliable electronic blend detection feasible — a thermal or density sensor would fail under temperature swings.

#### (c) Engine Control Unit (ECU) — Adaptive Calibration
- The ECU has a **flex-fuel-specific calibration map** with ethanol content as an additional input axis.
- Key control variables adjusted:
  1. **Fuel injection pulse width** (longer for ethanol due to lower LHV).
  2. **Ignition timing advance** (advanced for ethanol's higher octane).
  3. **Cold-start enrichment** (compensates for ethanol's lower vapor pressure).
  4. **Idle air control** (compensates for lower flame speed at low loads).

#### (d) Injection Subsystem
- **Larger flow rate injectors** — typically **30–40% higher flow capacity** than gasoline-only injectors.
- **Multi-hole pintle injectors** to ensure finer atomization of ethanol, which has higher surface tension than gasoline.

#### (e) Cold Start Subsystem
- Ethanol has **lower vapor pressure** (~17 kPa at 38°C vs ~55 kPa for gasoline) → poor cold-start atomization.
- FFVs use **heated fuel rails, glow plugs (for diesel-style cold assist), and aggressive cold-start fuel enrichment**.

#### (f) Emissions & After-Treatment Subsystem
- Ethanol combustion produces **fewer aromatics, lower soot, but higher aldehyde (acetaldehyde) emissions**.
- **Closed-coupled three-way catalytic converter (TWC)** with higher palladium loading to handle aldehydes.

---

### 2.2 Properties of Ethanol vs Gasoline (High-Yield Table)

| Property | Gasoline | Ethanol | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chemical formula (approx.) | C₄–C₁₂ mix | C₂H₅OH | Ethanol is oxygenated (35% O by mass) |
| Molecular weight (g/mol) | ~100 (avg.) | 46.07 | Lower molecular weight |
| Density (kg/L) | **0.745** | **0.789** | Ethanol denser → need more volume for same mass |
| Lower Heating Value (MJ/kg) | **43.4** | **26.9** | Ethanol ~38% lower per kg |
| Lower Heating Value (MJ/L) | **32.0** | **21.2** | Ethanol ~34% lower per litre |
| Stoichiometric AFR (mass) | **14.7 : 1** | **9.0 : 1** | Critical for ECU fueling |
| Octane number (RON) | 84–98 | **108–110** | Ethanol enables higher compression |
| Research Octane Number | 90 | 110 | Allows knock-resistant operation |
| Heat of Vaporization (kJ/kg) | 305 | **904** | Ethanol cools intake charge heavily → denser charge |
| Dielectric constant | 2.0–2.3 | **24.3** | Enables fuel composition sensing |
| Flame speed (m/s) | 0.4 | 0.42 | Similar → minor calibration difference |
| Auto-ignition temperature (°C) | 246 | 363 | Ethanol much harder to auto-ignite |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU board examinations.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $AFR_{stoich,\,gasoline} = \dfrac{4\,(11.2\,C + 8\,H - O) + 32\,(C + \tfrac{H}{4} - \tfrac{O}{2})}{12\,C + H + 16\,O}$ | Universal stoichiometric AFR for any fuel CₓHᵧOᵤ |
| 2 | $AFR_{gasoline} = 14.7$ (by mass) | Gasoline stoichiometric ratio (constant) |
| 3 | $AFR_{ethanol} = 9.0$ (by mass) | Ethanol stoichiometric ratio (constant) |
| 4 | $AFR_{blend}(E_n) = 14.7 - \dfrac{(14.7 - 9.0)\,n}{100}$ | Stoichiometric AFR of E_n blend (n% ethanol) |
| 5 | $\rho_{blend}(E_n) = \rho_{g}(1 - \tfrac{n}{100}) + \rho_{e}\,\tfrac{n}{100}$ kg/L | Density of E_n blend |
| 6 | $LHV_{blend}(E_n) = LHV_{g}(1 - \tfrac{n}{100}) + LHV_{e}\,\tfrac{n}{100}$ MJ/L | Volumetric LHV of E_n blend |
| 7 | $m_{fuel,\,blend} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{AFR_{blend}}$ | Required fuel mass for given air mass |
| 8 | $\Delta m_{fuel} = m_{fuel,\,blend} - m_{fuel,\,gasoline}$ | Additional fuel required when running on blend |
| 9 | $\eta_{vol,blend} = \eta_{vol,gasoline} \cdot \left[1 + \alpha \cdot \dfrac{n}{100}\right]$ | Volumetric efficiency gains due to charge cooling |
| 10 | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{P_{brake} \cdot t}$ g/kWh | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption |
| 11 | $CO_2,\,blend = (1 - 0.35\,\tfrac{n}{100}) \cdot CO_2,\,gasoline$ | Approximate CO₂ reduction from oxygen content |
| 12 | $V_{inj,\,blend} = V_{inj,\,gasoline} \cdot \dfrac{LHV_{gasoline}}{LHV_{blend}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{gasoline}}{\rho_{blend}}$ | Required injection volume scaling |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brazil's ProÁlcool Programme (since 1975):** Over **90%** of new vehicles sold are FFVs running on E100/E85/E25 blends.
- **India's E20 rollout (2025–26):** The BSI/ARAI report shows need for **fuel-compatible elastomers** in millions of existing two-wheeler/small-car fuel systems — directly relevant to OEM material engineers.
- **USA:** Over **21 million FFVs on road** (E85-capable), providing a **drop-in** renewable pathway without electric-vehicle infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Derivations below are exhaustively written — every algebraic step is shown. KTU 2024 examiners routinely ask **numerical substitution problems on ethanol blending**, so master these.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric AFR of a Generic Fuel (C_xH_yO_z)

For the generic hydrocarbon fuel **CₓHᵧOᵤ**, complete combustion yields:

$$
C_x H_y O_z + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right) O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; x\,CO_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,H_2O
$$

**Step 1 — Determine moles of O₂ required per mole of fuel:**
The stoichiometric coefficient of O₂ is:

$$
\nu_{O_2} = x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mass of air:**
Air is **23.2% O₂ by mass**, i.e., for every **1 kg of O₂**, we need **1/0.232 = 4.31 kg of air**.

Mass of O₂ required per mole of fuel:

$$
m_{O_2} = \nu_{O_2} \times 32 \;\text{g} \;=\; 32\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right)\;\text{g}
$$

Mass of air required per mole of fuel:

$$
m_{air} = \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \dfrac{32}{0.232}\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right)\;\text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute molecular mass of fuel:**

$$
M_{fuel} = 12x + y + 16z \;\text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric AFR (mass basis):**

$$
AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{M_{fuel}} = \dfrac{32 \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right)}{0.232\,(12x + y + 16z)}
$$

$$
AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{32 \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right)}{0.232\,(12x + y + 16z)}
$$

$$
AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{137.93 \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right)}{(12x + y + 16z)}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stoichiometric AFR of Pure Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)

**Step 1 — Write the combustion equation for C₂H₅OH (with z = 1 oxygen atom already in molecule):**

$$
C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O
$$

**Step 2 — Identify stoichiometric coefficients:**
- $x = 2$, $y = 6$ (5 H + 1 OH = 6 H atoms total), $z = 1$
- $\nu_{O_2} = 2 + \dfrac{6}{4} - \dfrac{1}{2} = 2 + 1.5 - 0.5 = 3.0$ ✓

**Step 3 — Molecular weight of ethanol:**

$$
M_{ethanol} = 12(2) + 6(1) + 16(1) = 24 + 6 + 16 = 46\;\text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of air required:**

$$
m_{air} = \dfrac{32 \times 3}{0.232} = \dfrac{96}{0.232} = 413.79\;\text{g air}
$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric AFR:**

$$
AFR_{ethanol} = \dfrac{413.79}{46} = 8.996 \approx 9.0
$$

**Result:** $AFR_{ethanol} = 9.0 : 1$ (mass basis) ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stoichiometric AFR of an E_n Blend

For a blend of **n% ethanol** and **(100 − n)% gasoline** by volume:

**Step 1 — Approximate using volumetric mass fractions:**
For an E_n blend, the **volumetric mass fraction of ethanol** is:

$$
w_{e} = \dfrac{n \cdot \rho_{e}}{n \cdot \rho_{e} + (100 - n) \cdot \rho_{g}}
$$

Where $\rho_{e} = 0.789$ kg/L and $\rho_{g} = 0.745$ kg/L.

**Step 2 — Mass-weighted AFR formula:**

$$
AFR_{blend} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{w_{g}}{AFR_{g}} + \dfrac{w_{e}}{AFR_{e}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification commonly used in engineering (mass-weighted shortcut):**

$$
AFR_{blend}(E_n) \approx 14.7 - \dfrac{(14.7 - 9.0)\,n}{100} = 14.7 - 0.057\,n
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for E85:**

$$
AFR_{E85} = 14.7 - 0.057 \times 85 = 14.7 - 4.845 = 9.855
$$

**Result:** $AFR_{E85} \approx 9.85 : 1$ ✓ (industry standard)

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Volumetric Fuel Consumption Scaling

**Problem:** A car consumes **1.00 L/h of gasoline** to maintain a given power output. How much **E85** is needed for the same power output?

**Step 1 — Equal energy requirement (since LHV × volume = energy):**

$$
\dot{V}_{gasoline} \cdot LHV_{gasoline} = \dot{V}_{E85} \cdot LHV_{E85}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for E85 volume:**

$$
\dot{V}_{E85} = \dot{V}_{gasoline} \cdot \dfrac{LHV_{gasoline}}{LHV_{E85}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute LHV of E85 (mass-weighted average):**

$$
LHV_{E85} = 0.85 \times 21.2 + 0.15 \times 32.0 = 18.02 + 4.80 = 22.82\;\text{MJ/L}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
\dot{V}_{E85} = 1.00 \times \dfrac{32.0}{22.82} = 1.402\;\text{L/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage increase:**

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{1.402 - 1.000}{1.000} \times 100\% = 40.2\%
$$

**Result:** E85 consumption is **~40% higher** by volume than gasoline for the same energy output.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Fuel Composition Sensor Calibration

The fuel composition sensor measures **capacitance** between two electrodes:

$$
C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}
$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m (vacuum permittivity)
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of fuel blend
- $A$ = electrode area (constant)
- $d$ = electrode gap (constant)

For an E_n blend (linear interpolation):

$$
\varepsilon_r(n) = \varepsilon_{gasoline} + \dfrac{n}{100}(\varepsilon_{ethanol} - \varepsilon_{gasoline})
$$

Thus:

$$
\varepsilon_r(n) = 2.15 + \dfrac{n}{100}(24.3 - 2.15) = 2.15 + 0.2215\,n
$$

Solving for n:

$$
n = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 2.15}{0.2215}
$$

**Numerical Example:** If measured $\varepsilon_r = 21.0$:
$$
n = \dfrac{21.0 - 2.15}{0.2215} = \dfrac{18.85}{0.2215} \approx 85.1\%
$$

**Result:** Fuel is identified as **E85 ± sensor tolerance (~±3%)**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — ECU Control Logic Flow

| ECU Input | Mapped Variable | Modification |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel composition sensor → ε_r | Ethanol % (n) | Read every 100 ms |
| Engine speed (RPM) | Base injection map | Look up table |
| Coolant temperature | Cold-start multiplier | If T < 20°C, enrich 1.2–1.5× |
| Manifold absolute pressure | Load signal | Compensate volumetric efficiency |
| Lambda sensor feedback | Closed-loop trim | PID controller output |

**Control Equation for Final Injection Pulse Width:**

$$
t_{inj} = t_{base}(RPM, MAP) \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{AFR_{gasoline}}{AFR_{blend}(n)}}_{\text{stoich factor}} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{LHV_{gasoline}}{LHV_{blend}(n)}}_{\text{energy factor}} \cdot \underbrace{f(T_{coolant})}_{\text{cold factor}} \cdot (1 + \delta_{\lambda})
$$

Where $\delta_{\lambda}$ is the closed-loop lambda trim.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FFV Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flex Fuel Tank] -->|E0 to E100 blend| B[High Flow Fuel Pump]
    B --> C[Fuel Filter]
    C --> D[Fuel Composition Sensor]
    D -->|PWM Signal Ethanol Percentage| E[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    E -->|Adjusted Pulse Width| F[Fuel Rail]
    F --> G[High Flow Injectors]
    G -->|Atomized Ethanol Blend| H[Combustion Chamber]
    H --> I[Turbocharger / Intake Manifold]
    I --> J[Cold Start Glow Plug]
    J --> H
    H --> K[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    K --> L[Lambda Sensor]
    L -->|Closed Loop Feedback| E
    E -->|Ignition Timing Signal| M[Ignition Coil and Spark Plug]
    M --> H
    E -->|Idle Air Control| N[Throttle Body]
    N --> H

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    style D fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000
    style E fill:#f5b041,stroke:#784212,color:#000

    style K fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — FFV Subsystem Interaction Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYS[FFV Powertrain] --> S1[Fuel Storage and Delivery]
    SYS --> S2[Sensing and Control]
    SYS --> S3[Engine and Combustion]
    SYS --> S4[After Treatment]

    S1 --> S1a[Stainless Tank]
    S1 --> S1b[Reinforced Fuel Pump]
    S1 --> S1c[Viton Seals]

    S2 --> S2a[Fuel Composition Sensor]
    S2 --> S2b[ECU Flex Fuel Map]
    S2 --> S2c[Lambda Sensor]

    S3 --> S3a[Larger Injectors]
    S3 --> S3b[Cold Start Heated Rail]
    S3 --> S3c[Advanced Ignition Timing]

    S4 --> S4a[Three Way Catalyst]
    S4 --> S4b[Oxygen Sensor Array]

    style SYS fill:#34495e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S1 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#000
```
