```

---

### 4.3 Signal Flow — Fuel Composition Sensor to Injector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Fuel Blend Enters Sensor] --> P2[Dielectric Capacitor Plates]
    P2 -->|Capacitance Change| P3[Oscillator Circuit]
    P3 -->|Frequency to Voltage Converter| P4[Analog Voltage Signal]
    P4 -->|ADC Conversion| P5[ECU Microcontroller]
    P5 -->|Lookup Table for Ethanol Percentage| P6[Calculate AFR and LHV]
    P6 -->|New Injection Pulse Width| P7[Driver Circuit]
    P7 -->|Solenoid Actuation| P8[Fuel Injector]
    P8 -->|Optimized Spray| P9[Engine Combustion]

    style P1 fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#000
    style P5 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P9 fill:#cb4335,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.4 Cold Start Strategy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Engine Cranking Initiated] --> Q2{Coolant Temperature}
    Q2 -->|Above 20 Degree Celsius| Q3[Normal Start Sequence]
    Q2 -->|Between 0 and 20 Degree| Q4[Activate Heated Fuel Rail]
    Q2 -->|Below 0 Degree Celsius| Q5[Activate Glow Plug Plus Heated Rail]
    Q4 --> Q6[Enrich Mixture by Factor 1.2]
    Q5 --> Q7[Enrich Mixture by Factor 1.5]
    Q6 --> Q8[Extended Cranking Time]
    Q7 --> Q8
    Q3 --> Q9[Standard Operation]
    Q8 --> Q9

    style Q5 fill:#cb4335,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q9 fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Flex Fuel Vehicle. List any four components that distinguish an FFV from a conventional petrol car.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Flex Fuel Vehicle (FFV) is an automobile capable of operating on **gasoline, ethanol, or any intermediate gasoline–ethanol blend** (E0–E100) within the **same fuel tank and engine**, without manual fuel switching.

Four distinguishing components:
1. **Fuel composition sensor** (dielectric-based) — detects ethanol %.
2. **High-flow injectors** — deliver 30–40% more volume per stroke.
3. **Corrosion-resistant fuel system** (stainless tank, Viton seals).
4. **Adaptive ECU** with multi-fuel calibration maps.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is a fuel composition sensor necessary in an FFV? State the physical principle on which it operates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A fuel composition sensor is required because **stoichiometric AFR changes from 14.7:1 (gasoline) to 9.0:1 (pure ethanol)**, and the **volumetric heating value differs by ~34%**. The ECU must continuously know the ethanol fraction to correctly compute **injection pulse width** and **ignition timing**.

**Principle:** It works on the **dielectric permittivity (capacitance) measurement** — the capacitance between two electrodes immersed in the fuel is directly proportional to the ethanol fraction, since $\varepsilon_{ethanol} \approx 24.3$ vs $\varepsilon_{gasoline} \approx 2.15$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write $AFR_{ethanol} = 14.7$ (incorrect — should be **9.0**). One mark lost per such error.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert LHV from **MJ/kg to MJ/L** when comparing fuels — density correction is mandatory.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "FFV runs on petrol and CNG" in the answer — this is **wrong**; FFVs are gasoline–ethanol systems, not CNG bi-fuel systems.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to mention the **cold-start enrichment strategy** in subsystem explanations — examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Sub-part (a) = 7 marks, Sub-part (b) = 7 marks. Each numerical problem step carries 1 mark; the final boxed answer carries 1 mark.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a Flex Fuel Vehicle powertrain**. Discuss in detail the role of the **fuel composition sensor** and the **adaptive ECU strategy** for multi-fuel operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car is calibrated to run on gasoline with stoichiometric AFR = 14.7 and BSFC = 280 g/kWh. Compute:
   - (i) The stoichiometric AFR when running on **E85** blend.
   - (ii) The new BSFC (in g/kWh) on E85, assuming **equal brake power output** and equal volumetric efficiency.
   - (iii) The percentage **increase in volumetric fuel consumption** when switching from gasoline to E85.
   Use: $\rho_{gasoline} = 0.745$ kg/L, $\rho_{ethanol} = 0.789$ kg/L, $LHV_{gasoline} = 43.4$ MJ/kg, $LHV_{ethanol} = 26.9$ MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A:**

#### **Part (a) — Functional Architecture (7 marks)**

The FFV powertrain consists of four primary subsystems working in coordination:

**1. Fuel Storage & Delivery (1 mark):**
A single stainless-steel tank stores any blend from E0 to E100. A high-flow electric fuel pump (capable of 30–40% greater volumetric output) supplies the blend through Viton-sealed Viton lines to the fuel rail.

**2. Fuel Composition Sensing (2 marks):**
A capacitive dielectric sensor placed **after the fuel filter and before the fuel rail** continuously measures the **relative permittivity (ε_r)** of the blend. The measured capacitance is converted to a voltage via an oscillator circuit, and the ECU's ADC interprets this to determine the ethanol percentage (n).

**3. Adaptive ECU Strategy (3 marks):**
The ECU uses a **three-dimensional lookup table** with axes (RPM, MAP, ethanol fraction). For each input combination, it determines:
- **Injection pulse width** $t_{inj} = t_{base} \times \dfrac{AFR_{gasoline}}{AFR_{blend}} \times \dfrac{LHV_{gasoline}}{LHV_{blend}} \times f(T)$
- **Ignition timing advance** (typically advanced by 2°–5° on ethanol due to its **110 RON**)
- **Cold-start enrichment factor** (1.2× to 1.5× below 20°C)
- **Closed-loop lambda trim** based on post-catalyst O₂ sensor.

**4. After-Treatment (1 mark):**
A palladium-loaded three-way catalyst handles ethanol's specific emissions profile, which includes higher **acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)** and lower **aromatic HC** emissions compared to gasoline.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Stoichiometric AFR of E85 (2 marks):**

Using the linear blend formula:

$$
AFR_{E85} = 14.7 - \dfrac{(14.7 - 9.0) \times 85}{100}
$$

$$
AFR_{E85} = 14.7 - \dfrac{5.7 \times 85}{100} = 14.7 - 4.845 = 9.855
$$

$$
\boxed{AFR_{E85} = 9.855 : 1}
$$

**(ii) New BSFC on E85 (3 marks):**

For equal brake power, energy input must be equal:
$$
\dot{m}_{fuel,E85} \cdot LHV_{E85} = \dot{m}_{fuel,gasoline} \cdot LHV_{gasoline}
$$

First, compute **LHV of E85** (mass-weighted):
$$
LHV_{E85} = 0.85 \times 26.9 + 0.15 \times 43.4 = 22.865 + 6.51 = 29.375\;\text{MJ/kg}
$$

New BSFC ratio:
$$
\dfrac{BSFC_{E85}}{BSFC_{gasoline}} = \dfrac{LHV_{gasoline}}{LHV_{E85}} = \dfrac{43.4}{29.375} = 1.4774
$$

$$
BSFC_{E85} = 280 \times 1.4774 = 413.7\;\text{g/kWh}
$$

$$
\boxed{BSFC_{E85} \approx 413.7\;\text{g/kWh}}
$$

**(iii) Percentage increase in volumetric fuel consumption (2 marks):**

Volumetric consumption = mass consumption / density.

$$
\dot{V}_{gasoline} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{gasoline}}{\rho_{gasoline}} \;\text{ and }\; \dot{V}_{E85} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{E85}}{\rho_{E85}}
$$

Compute the density of E85:
$$
\rho_{E85} = 0.85 \times 0.789 + 0.15 \times 0.745 = 0.67065 + 0.11175 = 0.7824\;\text{kg/L}
$$

Compute volume ratio:
$$
\dfrac{\dot{V}_{E85}}{\dot{V}_{gasoline}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{E85}}{\dot{m}_{gasoline}} \times \dfrac{\rho_{gasoline}}{\rho_{E85}} = 1.4774 \times \dfrac{0.745}{0.7824}
$$

$$
\dfrac{\dot{V}_{E85}}{\dot{V}_{gasoline}} = 1.4774 \times 0.9523 = 1.4070
$$

Percentage increase:
$$
\Delta V\% = (1.4070 - 1) \times 100 = 40.70\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta V \approx 40.7\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** Switching to E85 requires about **41% more fuel by volume** for the same brake power — this is the central engineering trade-off of FFVs.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With suitable equations, derive the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** for **pure ethanol (C₂H₅OH)**. Compare the AFR, density, and Lower Heating Value of pure ethanol with those of gasoline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle, construction, and signal conditioning** of the fuel composition sensor used in FFVs. A sensor reads a capacitance of **9.5 pF** in pure gasoline and **78.2 pF** in pure ethanol for the same electrode geometry. If the measured capacitance is **66.0 pF**, determine the ethanol percentage in the blend. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B:**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of AFR for Pure Ethanol (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Combustion Equation (2 marks):**

$$
C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O
$$

**Step 2 — Molecular weight of ethanol (1 mark):**

$$
M_{ethanol} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = 24 + 6 + 16 = 46\;\text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of O₂ required (1 mark):**

$$
m_{O_2} = 3 \times 32 = 96\;\text{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of air required (using 23.2% O₂ by mass) (1 mark):**

$$
m_{air} = \dfrac{96}{0.232} = 413.79\;\text{g}
$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric AFR (1 mark):**

$$
AFR_{ethanol} = \dfrac{413.79}{46} = 8.996 \approx 9.0
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Gasoline | Ethanol |
|---|---|---|
| AFR (stoich) | 14.7 | **9.0** (38.8% lower) |
| Density (kg/L) | 0.745 | 0.789 (5.9% higher) |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 43.4 | 26.9 (38% lower) |
| LHV (MJ/L) | 32.0 | 21.2 (33.8% lower) |

---

#### **Part (b) — Fuel Composition Sensor (7 marks)**

**Principle & Construction (3 marks):**
The sensor consists of **two concentric cylindrical electrodes** separated by a fixed gap through which fuel flows. The capacitance between the electrodes is:

$$
C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}
$$

The dielectric constant of the fuel blend is linearly interpolated:

$$
\varepsilon_r(n) = 2.15 + 0.2215\,n
$$

Since $C \propto \varepsilon_r$, the capacitance is also linearly proportional to ethanol content.

**Signal Conditioning (2 marks):**
The capacitor forms part of an **LC oscillator** whose frequency varies with $\varepsilon_r$. A frequency-to-voltage converter (FVC) outputs an analog voltage proportional to the ethanol fraction. This signal is digitized by the ECU's **ADC** and processed against the lookup table.

**Numerical Solution (2 marks):**

Given:
- $C_{gasoline} = 9.5$ pF
- $C_{ethanol} = 78.2$ pF
- $C_{measured} = 66.0$ pF

Using linear interpolation:

$$
n = \dfrac{C_{measured} - C_{gasoline}}{C_{ethanol} - C_{gasoline}} \times 100
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{66.0 - 9.5}{78.2 - 9.5} \times 100 = \dfrac{56.5}{68.7} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 82.2\%}
$$

**Result:** The fuel is approximately **E82**, i.e., 82% ethanol + 18% gasoline — classified as an E85-grade blend within sensor tolerance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Flex Fuel Vehicles (FFV)**

- [ ] **FFV Definition:** Single-tank vehicle running on any gasoline–ethanol blend (E0–E100) without manual switching.
- [ ] **Core Components:** Fuel composition sensor, adaptive ECU, larger injectors, corrosion-resistant fuel system.
- [ ] **Fuel Composition Sensor Principle:** Capacitance measurement based on dielectric permittivity difference ($\varepsilon_r$ gasoline = 2.15, ethanol = 24.3).
- [ ] **Key AFR Values:** Gasoline = **14.7 : 1**, Ethanol = **9.0 : 1**, E85 ≈ **9.85 : 1**, E20 ≈ **13.56 : 1**.
- [ ] **LHV Values (MJ/kg):** Gasoline = **43.4**, Ethanol = **26.9**.
- [ ] **Density (kg/L):** Gasoline = **0.745**, Ethanol = **0.789**.
- [ ] **Ethanol's LHV is ~38% lower per kg → ~34% lower per litre → ~40% more volume required for same energy.**
- [ ] **Ethanol Advantages:** Octane 110 RON (enables higher CR), 35% oxygen by mass (cleaner combustion), renewable.
- [ ] **Ethanol Disadvantages:** Lower vapor pressure (cold-start issue), hygroscopic (corrosion), lower flame visibility, attacks nitrile rubber.
- [ ] **ECU Adjustments:** Injection pulse width ↑, ignition timing advance ↑, cold-start enrichment ↑.
- [ ] **Emission Profile:** Lower CO₂, lower aromatics, lower soot, but higher **acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)**.
- [ ] **India Context:** Government target — **E20 by 2025–26** (Ethanol Blended Petrol Programme).
- [ ] **Brazil Context:** World's largest FFV fleet (>90% of new cars); supports E100.
- [ ] **Formula Must-Memorize:** $AFR_{blend}(E_n) = 14.7 - 0.057n$ and $LHV_{blend}(E_n) = 32.0 \cdot (1 - n/100) + 21.2 \cdot (n/100)$ MJ/L.
- [ ] **Cold-Start Strategy:** Heated fuel rails + glow plug assist + enrichment factor of 1.2×–1.5×.
- [ ] **Catalyst:** Palladium-loaded TWC required for aldehyde oxidation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SEEDS-E":**
> **S**ensor (composition) → **E**CU (adaptive) → **E**thanol-resistant seals → **D**elivery (high-flow) → **S**tart (cold assist) → **E**missions catalyst.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: H2-ICE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET613 - AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** H2-ICE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engine (H2-ICE)

A **Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engine (H2-ICE)** is a reciprocating or rotary heat engine that derives its motive power from the combustion of hydrogen (H₂) gas with an oxidizer (typically air) inside the combustion chamber of the working cylinder. The chemical energy released during the exothermic reaction between hydrogen and oxygen is converted into mechanical work via the standard Otto or Diesel thermodynamic cycles.

The stoichiometric chemical reaction governing H2-ICE operation is:

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Energy } (\approx 242 \text{ kJ/mol of } H_2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** H2-ICE falls under *Alternative Fuels and Advanced Powertrain Technologies*. Unlike a Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV) which converts H₂ into electricity electrochemically, the H2-ICE **directly combusts** hydrogen, making it a drop-in alternative to the conventional gasoline SI engine with minimal structural modification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure Definition (KTU Board Standard):** "H2-ICE is a spark-ignited or dual-fueled internal combustion engine in which hydrogen is the primary combustible fuel, admitted either by port fuel injection (PFI) or direct injection (DI), combusted in the presence of an oxidizer, and whose exhaust consists primarily of water vapor (H₂O) with negligible CO₂ and very low carbonaceous emissions."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Clean Steam" Engine

Imagine a regular petrol engine in your motorcycle. The petrol (a hydrocarbon) is a "dirty" fuel — when it burns, it produces **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, **carbon monoxide (CO)**, and **unburnt hydrocarbons (HC)** because of the carbon content in the fuel.

Now, **replace that petrol with pure hydrogen gas**. Hydrogen has *no carbon* in its molecule. So when it burns, the only "smoke" it produces is **water vapor (H₂O)** — literally, the engine "breathes out" clean steam.

**The Analogy:**
- 🔥 **Petrol engine** = burning a candle (leaves soot/carbon)
- 💧 **Hydrogen engine** = burning pure gas (leaves only water)

However, the catch is that hydrogen is **highly flammable**, has a **very wide flammability range**, and burns **much faster** than petrol — which creates new engineering challenges (backfire, pre-ignition, NOx) that we will study in Section 2.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Properties of Hydrogen (as Fuel)

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline (Reference) | Methane (CH₄) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight (kg/kmol) | **2.016** | ~100 – 110 | 16.04 |
| Density at STP (kg/m³) | **0.0899** | ~750 | 0.717 |
| Lower Heating Value (MJ/kg) | **120** | ~44 | 50 |
| Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric) | **34.3 : 1** | 14.7 : 1 | 17.2 : 1 |
| Flammability Limits (vol % in air) | **4 – 75 %** | 1.0 – 7.6 % | 5 – 15 % |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature (°C) | **585** | 257 | 540 |
| Minimum Ignition Energy (mJ) | **0.02** | 0.25 | 0.30 |
| Flame Speed (m/s) | **2.65 – 3.25** | 0.37 – 0.43 | 0.4 |
| Octane Number (RON) | **> 130** | 90 – 98 | 120 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter for KTU:**
> - **Octane Number > 130** → Hydrogen resists knocking, allowing very high compression ratios (CR up to 15:1 or more) without knock.
> - **Flame Speed 8× gasoline** → Enables lean-burn operation and faster, more complete combustion.
> - **Flammability 4 – 75%** → Extremely wide range enables ultra-lean operation, but also causes backfire.
> - **Energy per kg = 3× gasoline**, but energy per **volume** is much lower → Requires large storage tanks (≈ 4× volume for same energy).

---

## 1.4 Classification of H2-ICE Systems

Hydrogen ICEs are broadly classified as:

1. **Hydrogen-Only Spark Ignition (SI) Engine**
   - Pure H₂ inducted + spark-ignited.
   - May be stoichiometric or lean-burn.

2. **Hydrogen-Diesel Dual-Fuel Engine** (More common in heavy-duty applications)
   - Diesel acts as the pilot fuel (compression ignition).
   - Hydrogen is inducted into the intake manifold.
   - Compression ratio remains similar to diesel (~16–18:1).

3. **Direct Injection (DI) Hydrogen Engine**
   - H₂ injected directly into the cylinder at high pressure.
   - Avoids backfire (no hydrogen in intake manifold).
   - Higher power density.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Operating Principle

A hydrogen ICE operates on the **Otto Cycle** (theoretical) for SI versions, and a **Modified Diesel Cycle** for dual-fuel versions.

### The Otto Cycle for H2-ICE consists of four processes:

1. **Process 1 → 2: Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression**
   - Air (or air + H₂ mixture) is compressed in the cylinder.
   - Work input → Increase in temperature and pressure.

2. **Process 2 → 3: Constant Volume Heat Addition**
   - Combustion of H₂ at (approximately) constant volume.
   - Heat added: $Q_{in} = m \cdot LHV \cdot \eta_{combustion}$

3. **Process 3 → 4: Adiabatic (Isentropic) Expansion**
   - High-pressure hot gases expand, doing work on the piston.

4. **Process 4 → 1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection**
   - Exhaust valve opens → blowdown → exhaust stroke.

---

## 2.2 Stoichiometry of Hydrogen Combustion

The ideal stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (by mass) is:

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = 34.3 : 1$$

Derivation logic (KTU Board Favourite):
- Molecular weight of H₂ = 2 kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of O₂ = 32 kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of air ≈ 28.97 kg/kmol
- Air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass.

$$AFR = \frac{1 \text{ kmol } O_2 \times \frac{1}{0.232} \text{ kmol air} \times 28.97}{2 \text{ kmol } H_2 \times 2.016}$$

$$AFR = \frac{4.32 \times 28.97}{4.032} \approx 34.3$$

### Equivalence Ratio (Φ) and Lambda (λ)

$$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} \quad ; \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\phi}$$

- **Lean Burn (λ > 1):** H2-ICE typically operates at λ = 1.5 to 3.0 for emissions & efficiency.
- **Stoichiometric (λ = 1):** Maximum power.
- **Rich (λ < 1):** Rare, causes backfire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** H2-ICE **cannot operate rich** (Φ > 1) because of severe backfire and abnormal combustion. Lean operation is preferred.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | Stoichiometric reaction | — |
| $AFR_{stoich} = 34.3$ | Stoichiometric A/F ratio by mass | — |
| $LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Lower Heating Value of H₂ | MJ/kg |
| $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency | dimensionless |
| $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | Net Work per cycle | J |
| $\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Thermal Efficiency definition | dimensionless |
| $\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{a}}{(A/F)_{s}}$ | Equivalence Ratio | dimensionless |
| $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{BP \cdot bsfc}{3600}$ | Hydrogen mass flow rate | kg/s |
| $P_{mi} = \frac{W_{cycle}}{V_{d} \cdot N/2 \cdot K}$ | Mean Effective Pressure | bar |
| $NO_x \propto e^{(-E_a / RT)}$ | Arrhenius (Zeldovich) NOx formation | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latex Note for KTU Board Sheets:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` if absolute value signs appear in formulas to keep markdown table syntax safe.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of H2-ICE |
|---|---|
| **Heavy-Duty Trucks & Buses** | Cummins, Toyota, BMW, MAN have demonstrated H2-ICE trucks. Useful where FCEV cost is prohibitive. |
| **Stationary Power Generation** | Remote power plants using hydrogen produced from surplus renewable electricity. |
| **Aviation** | Hydrogen-combustion turbine/jet prototypes (Airbus ZEROe, hydrogen combustion engines). |
| **Off-Road / Mining** | Underground machinery needing zero-emission (water-vapor exhaust). |
| **Maritime** | Hybrid systems: H2-ICE + battery for ships. |
| **Grid Stabilization** | Backup generators using on-site electrolyzed H₂. |

---

## 2.5 Key Engineering Challenges — The "Dark Side" of H2-ICE

### Challenge 1: Abnormal Combustion — *Pre-Ignition and Backfire*
- **Pre-ignition:** H₂ ignites spontaneously before the spark due to hot spots in the cylinder (deposits, exhaust valves, spark plug electrodes).
- **Backfire:** H₂-air mixture in the intake manifold ignites back through the throttle body (because H₂ has very low ignition energy of **0.02 mJ**).
- **Mitigation:** Direct Injection (DI), water injection, exhaust gas recirculation (EGR), and special spark plugs.

### Challenge 2: Low Power Density
- Hydrogen displaces air in the cylinder (since H₂ is gaseous and takes up volume).
- At stoichiometric, the **energy content per cylinder volume is about 15–20% less** than gasoline.
- **Mitigation:** Supercharging, turbocharging, cryogenic liquid hydrogen storage.

### Challenge 3: NOx Formation
- Adiabatic flame temperature of H₂ in air ≈ **2400 K** (high).
- NOx formed via Zeldovich mechanism: $O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N$
- **Mitigation:** Lean burn (lower flame temp), EGR, Three-Way Catalyst (TWC) with rich excursion.

### Challenge 4: On-Board Storage
- H₂ has very low density → Needs:
  - **350 bar** or **700 bar** Compressed Gas tanks (CHG)
  - **Liquid H₂ (LH₂)** at -253 °C
  - **Metal Hydrides** or **Carbon Nanotube Adsorption** (research stage)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always mention **NOx is the major pollutant** from H2-ICE. CO, CO₂, and HC are practically zero (because there is no carbon in the fuel). Many students mistakenly write "CO₂ is high" — this is **wrong** for pure H2-ICE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency of Otto Cycle H2-ICE

The Otto cycle thermal efficiency governs the ideal performance ceiling of an H2-ICE operating in spark-ignition mode.

### Starting Point — Ideal Gas Law during Compression (Process 1 → 2):

$$P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$$

### Step 1: Compression Ratio Definition

Let $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ be the compression ratio.

### Step 2: Temperature Ratio After Adiabatic Compression

For an isentropic compression of an ideal gas:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$

### Step 3: Heat Addition at Constant Volume (Process 2 → 3)

Heat added by hydrogen combustion:

$$Q_{in} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$$

### Step 4: Heat Rejection at Constant Volume (Process 4 → 1)

$$Q_{out} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)$$

### Step 5: Net Work of the Otto Cycle

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m \cdot C_v \cdot [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$

### Step 6: Apply Isentropic Relations for Process 3 → 4

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

(Since $V_3 = V_2$ and $V_4 = V_1$ in the Otto cycle.)

Similarly for Process 1 → 2:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

### Step 7: Substitute and Simplify

Combining:

$$T_3 = T_2 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \cdot r^{2(\gamma - 1)}$$

$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot r^{-(\gamma - 1)} = T_1 \cdot r^{(\gamma - 1)}$$

### Step 8: Final Expression for Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

After algebraic simplification using the isentropic relations:

$$\boxed{\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A 4-stroke hydrogen SI engine has a compression ratio $r = 12:1$. The cylinder contains 0.0005 kg of air-hydrogen mixture. Initial temperature $T_1 = 350$ K. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.717$ kJ/kg·K, and heat added $Q_{in} = 1.2$ kJ. Find:
(a) The temperature after compression $T_2$.
(b) The temperature after heat addition $T_3$.
(c) The thermal efficiency.
(d) The net work output.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Temperature after compression**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} = 350 \cdot (12)^{0.4}$$

Computing $12^{0.4}$:

$$12^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 12} = e^{0.4 \times 2.4849} = e^{0.99396} \approx 2.7019$$

$$T_2 = 350 \times 2.7019 = 945.66 \text{ K}$$

**Part (b): Temperature after heat addition**

$$Q_{in} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$$

$$1.2 = 0.0005 \times 0.717 \times (T_3 - 945.66)$$

$$1.2 = 0.0003585 \times (T_3 - 945.66)$$

$$T_3 - 945.66 = \frac{1.2}{0.0003585} = 3347.28 \text{ K}$$

$$T_3 = 945.66 + 3347.28 = 4292.94 \text{ K}$$

**Part (c): Thermal Efficiency**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.7019} = 1 - 0.3701 = 0.6299$$

$$\eta_{th} \approx 63\%$$

**Part (d): Net Work Output**

For Otto cycle, heat rejected is:

$$Q_{out} = \frac{Q_{in}}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot \text{correction factor}$$

Simplifying using cycle algebra:

$$W_{net} = \eta_{th} \times Q_{in} = 0.6299 \times 1.2 = 0.7559 \text{ kJ}$$

$$W_{net} \approx 0.756 \text{ kJ per cycle}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Parameter Effects on H2-ICE

| Parameter Increase | Effect on Power | Effect on Efficiency | Effect on NOx | Effect on Backfire |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compression Ratio (r) | ↑ | ↑↑ (Otto formula) | ↑ | ↑ |
| Equivalence Ratio (Φ → 1) | ↑ (max at stoich) | ↓ (slightly) | ↑↑ (max at stoich) | ↑↑ |
| Equivalence Ratio (Φ < 0.5, lean) | ↓ | ↑ (lean burn) | ↓↓ (low temp) | ↓ |
| Spark Advance | ↑ initially, then ↓ | Variable | ↑ | ↑ |
| EGR Rate | ↓ | ↑ (slightly) | ↓↓ | ↓ |
| Intake Air Temperature | ↓ (density drops) | ↓ | ↑ (slightly) | ↑ |
| Injection Timing (DI) | Variable | ↑ if optimized | ↓ if retarded | ↓ (no backfire) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio (Re-derived for clarity)

For complete combustion of hydrogen:

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$

- Mass of 2 kmol H₂ = $2 \times 2.016 = 4.032$ kg
- Mass of 1 kmol O₂ = 32 kg
- Mass of air required = $32 / 0.232 = 137.93$ kg (since air is 23.2% O₂ by mass)

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{137.93}{4.032} = 34.21 \approx 34.3$$

$$\boxed{AFR_{stoich} \approx 34.3 : 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **2.33 times** the gasoline A/F ratio. The volumetric penalty is the reason H2-ICE suffers reduced volumetric efficiency at full load.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any three advantages and two disadvantages of Hydrogen as a fuel for IC engines.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Wide flammability range (4 – 75% in air):** Allows ultra-lean operation improving fuel economy.
2. **High octane number (> 130 RON):** Permits high compression ratios (up to 15:1) without knock, raising thermal efficiency.
3. **High flame speed (2.65 – 3.25 m/s):** Enables rapid, complete combustion; lower cycle-to-cycle variation.
4. **Zero carbon emissions:** Combustion produces only H₂O; no CO₂, CO, or unburnt HC.
5. **Renewable production pathway:** Can be generated via electrolysis of water using solar/wind energy.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **On-board storage problem:** Very low density requires 700 bar tanks or cryogenic LH₂ at -253 °C; bulky and expensive.
2. **Pre-ignition and backfire:** Low ignition energy (0.02 mJ) and wide flammability cause abnormal combustion.
3. **High NOx formation:** High adiabatic flame temperature (~2400 K) produces NOx via Zeldovich mechanism.
4. **Low volumetric energy density:** Displaces intake air, reducing power output by 15 – 20% at stoichiometric.
5. **Hydrogen embrittlement:** Causes degradation of metallic engine components over time.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Explain the principle of operation of a hydrogen-diesel dual-fuel engine.**

**Model Answer:**

A hydrogen-diesel dual-fuel engine is a **modified compression-ignition (CI) engine** in which:

1. **Hydrogen is inducted** into the intake manifold along with intake air, either through port injection (PFI) or low-pressure mixing.
2. The mixture of air + H₂ is **compressed to a high ratio** (16:1 to 18:1), just as in a normal diesel engine.
3. A **small quantity of diesel (pilot fuel)** is injected directly into the hot compressed charge near TDC. The diesel auto-ignites due to compression heating.
4. The diesel pilot flame acts as the **ignition source** for the surrounding hydrogen-air mixture, which burns and releases energy.
5. The resulting high-pressure gases drive the **power stroke**, producing work on the piston.

**Key advantages:**
- Retains diesel's high compression ratio → high thermal efficiency.
- Displaces a portion of diesel → reduced fuel cost, lower carbon footprint.
- NOx can be controlled via EGR and lean operation.

**Key challenges:**
- Hydrogen substitution ratio (energy share replaced by H₂) limited by knock and backfire.
- Diesel auto-ignition quality must remain reliable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
>
> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a hydrogen-fueled spark-ignition engine. Discuss the role of **direct injection (DI)** in overcoming backfire. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hydrogen SI engine operates on the Otto cycle with a compression ratio of 14:1. The initial conditions are $P_1 = 1$ bar and $T_1 = 350$ K. The heat added is 1200 kJ/kg. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. Calculate the **maximum temperature** in the cycle and the **thermal efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working of H2 SI Engine with DI:**

A hydrogen-fueled spark-ignition engine operates identically to a conventional gasoline SI engine **in principle**, with modifications to handle hydrogen's unique combustion properties.

**Schematic Layout:**
- Hydrogen storage tank (700 bar CHG) → Pressure regulators (two-stage) → High-pressure injector → Combustion chamber (direct injection near TDC).
- Air intake through throttle → Cylinder → Spark ignition → Power stroke → Exhaust.

**Cycle of Operation (4-stroke):**
1. **Intake stroke:** Only air is inducted (in DI mode). Hydrogen is not present in the intake manifold.
2. **Compression stroke:** Air is compressed to high pressure and temperature.
3. **Power stroke (combustion):** Hydrogen is injected directly into the cylinder near the end of compression stroke. The high-pressure H₂ mixes with hot compressed air and is ignited by the spark plug. Combustion releases energy; piston descends.
4. **Exhaust stroke:** Combustion products (H₂O vapor, excess air, and NOx) expelled.

**Role of Direct Injection in Eliminating Backfire:**
- In **Port Fuel Injection (PFI)**, hydrogen is mixed with air in the intake manifold. If the intake valve is hot, or there is residual exhaust gas, the hydrogen-air mixture can ignite prematurely and propagate backward — causing **backfire** into the intake manifold.
- In **Direct Injection (DI)**, hydrogen is injected **directly into the combustion chamber at the end of the compression stroke**. The intake manifold contains only air.
- Therefore, there is **no flammable mixture in the intake manifold**, eliminating the possibility of backfire.
- Additional benefits of DI: better control of stratification, higher power density, and lower NOx via stratified lean combustion.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem:**

Given:
- $r = 14$, $P_1 = 1$ bar, $T_1 = 350$ K
- $q_{in} = 1200$ kJ/kg
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1: Find $T_2$ (Temperature after compression)**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} = 350 \times 14^{0.4}$$

$$14^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 14} = e^{0.4 \times 2.6391} = e^{1.0556} = 2.8733$$

$$T_2 = 350 \times 2.8733 = 1005.66 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Find $T_3$ (Maximum temperature after heat addition)**

$$q_{in} = C_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$$

$C_v$ for the gas:

$$C_v = \frac{R}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{0.287}{0.4} = 0.7175 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

$$1200 = 0.7175 \times (T_3 - 1005.66)$$

$$T_3 - 1005.66 = \frac{1200}{0.7175} = 1672.47 \text{ K}$$

$$T_3 = 1005.66 + 1672.47 = 2678.13 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_3 \approx 2678 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 3: Thermal Efficiency**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.8733} = 1 - 0.3480 = 0.6520$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 65.2\%}$$

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
>
> **(a)** Discuss the major **challenges** in using hydrogen as a fuel in IC engines. Compare PFI and DI strategies for hydrogen delivery. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** for hydrogen combustion and the **mass of air** required to completely burn 2 kg of hydrogen. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Challenges and PFI vs DI:**

**Major Challenges of H2-ICE:**
1. **Abnormal combustion (pre-ignition & backfire):** Due to low ignition energy (0.02 mJ) and wide flammability range (4 – 75%).
2. **High NOx emissions:** High adiabatic flame temperature (2400 K) causes thermal NOx.
3. **Power loss at stoichiometric:** Hydrogen displaces air → 15 – 20% lower volumetric efficiency.
4. **Storage and distribution:** Low density requires high-pressure (700 bar) or cryogenic storage; infrastructure absent.
5. **Material degradation:** Hydrogen embrittlement of steel components.
6. **Lubrication issues:** H₂ may dilute lubricating oil film, increasing wear.

**Comparison of PFI and DI:**

| Parameter | Port Fuel Injection (PFI) | Direct Injection (DI) |
|---|---|---|
| Injection Location | Intake manifold | Combustion chamber |
| Injection Pressure | 3 – 10 bar | 100 – 300 bar |
| Backfire Risk | High (H₂ in manifold) | Negligible |
| Power Density | Lower (premixed charge) | Higher |
| NOx Emissions | Higher (more uniform mixture) | Lower (stratified charge) |
| Cold Start | Poor (H₂ displaces air) | Better |
| Pre-Ignition Risk | Higher | Lower |
| Cost & Complexity | Lower | Higher |

**Part (b) — Stoichiometric A/F Ratio Calculation:**

The balanced chemical equation:

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$

**Step 1: Molecular masses**

- $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ kg/kmol
- $M_{O_2} = 32$ kg/kmol
- $M_{air} = 28.97$ kg/kmol (with 23.2% O₂ by mass)

**Step 2: Per kmol of H₂ basis**

For 1 kmol H₂ burned, we need 0.5 kmol O₂.

Mass of H₂ burned:

$$m_{H_2} = 1 \times 2.016 = 2.016 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of O₂ required:

$$m_{O_2} = 0.5 \times 32 = 16 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Mass of air required**

Since O₂ is 23.2% of air by mass:

$$m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{16}{0.232} = 68.97 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio**

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{68.97}{2.016} = 34.21$$

$$\boxed{AFR_{stoich} \approx 34.3 : 1}$$

**Step 5: Air required for 2 kg of H₂**

$$m_{air} = 2 \times 34.21 = 68.42 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{air} \approx 68.4 \text{ kg}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to show ALL steps** in the stoichiometric A/F derivation (the molar mass calculation is mandatory).
> 2. **Using 23.2% O₂ as volume basis** by mistake (it is **mass basis**; volume basis is 21%).
> 3. **Writing "H₂-ICE produces high CO₂ emissions"** — INCORRECT. H₂ has no carbon, so CO₂ = 0.
> 4. **Not specifying SI vs CI** when comparing with conventional engines — always clarify.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention lean burn** as a NOx mitigation strategy.
> 6. **Confusing the LHV of H₂ (120 MJ/kg) with HHV (142 MJ/kg)** — engine cycles use LHV.
> 7. **Not labelling Mermaid diagrams properly** in descriptive answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Definition:** H2-ICE = Internal combustion engine using hydrogen as primary fuel, combusted with air in cylinder.

✅ **Stoichiometric Reaction:** $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + 242 \text{ kJ/mol}$

✅ **Stoichiometric A/F Ratio:** **34.3 : 1** (mass basis) — memorize this number.

✅ **LHV of Hydrogen:** **120 MJ/kg** (≈ 2.7× gasoline).

✅ **Octane Number:** > **130 RON** → allows very high CR.

✅ **Flammability Range:** **4 – 75%** (extremely wide → causes backfire).

✅ **Minimum Ignition Energy:** **0.02 mJ** (very low → easy to ignite accidentally).

✅ **Flame Speed:** **2.65 – 3.25 m/s** (≈ 8× gasoline).

✅ **Adiabatic Flame Temperature:** ≈ **2400 K** → high NOx.

✅ **Otto Cycle Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$

✅ **Two Hydrogen Delivery Methods:**
- **PFI** (Port Fuel Injection): Low cost, but backfire risk.
- **DI** (Direct Injection): High cost, eliminates backfire, higher power.

✅ **Two Major H2-ICE Types:**
- **Pure H₂ SI Engine** (SI mode, spark ignited).
- **H₂-Diesel Dual-Fuel Engine** (diesel pilot, H₂ main).

✅ **Emissions Profile of Pure H2-ICE:**
- **CO₂ = 0**, CO ≈ 0, HC ≈ 0 (no carbon).
- **NOx is the only significant pollutant** → control via lean burn, EGR, TWC, SCR.

✅ **Key Challenges:** Backfire, pre-ignition, NOx, low power density at stoich, storage.

✅ **Mitigation Strategies:** DI, lean burn (λ > 1), EGR, water injection, supercharging, cryogenic LH₂ storage.

✅ **Storage Methods:** 700 bar CHG, LH₂ (-253 °C), metal hydrides (research).

✅ **KTU Board Rule:** Always state **NOx is the main emission** of H2-ICE; **not CO₂**.

✅ **Formula to Remember:** $AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{32 / 0.232}{2.016} \approx 34.3$

✅ **Real-World Examples:** BMW Hydrogen 7, Toyota Mirai (FCEV, not H2-ICE — do not confuse), Cummins H2-ICE trucks, MAN hydrogen combustion truck.

✅ **Comparison with FCEV:** H2-ICE **combusts** hydrogen (thermal); FCEV **electrolyzes** hydrogen (electrochemical). H2-ICE retains the existing engine ecosystem; FCEV is a fully new powertrain.

✅ **Typical Operating Lambda:** λ = 1.5 to 3.0 (lean) for best NOx/efficiency tradeoff.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Internal Combustion (IC) Engine?

An **Internal Combustion Engine** is a prime mover that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (petrol, diesel, CNG, etc.) into mechanical work (rotational energy on the crankshaft) by **burning the fuel inside the engine cylinder itself**. The combustion gases expand and exert pressure on a piston, which is converted to rotary motion through a crank-slider mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An IC engine is a heat engine in which the combustion of fuel occurs with an oxidizer (air) inside a combustion chamber that is an integral part of the working fluid flow circuit. (Ref: Course Outcome CO2 – Identify and describe the constructional features of major IC engine sub-systems.)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lung-and-Skeleton" Engine

Imagine the human body:
- The **Engine Block** is like the **skeleton** — it holds everything together.
- The **Cylinders** are the **lungs** — where air-fuel mixes and "breathes" (intake + exhaust).
- The **Piston** is the **heart muscle** — it pumps up and down.
- The **Connecting Rod** is the **tendon** — it transmits force.
- The **Crankshaft** is the **joint / pivot** — converting linear motion to rotary motion.
- The **Cylinder Head** is the **skull cap** — sealing the top and housing valves (mouth & nose).
- The **Valve Train** is the **voice box + lips** — controls when air-fuel comes in and exhaust goes out.
- The **Oil Sump** is the **stomach** — stores lubricant for digestion (lubrication).

Just like a body cannot function without a heart or skeleton, an IC engine cannot run without these components working in perfect synchrony.

---

## 1.3 Master Classification of IC Engine Components

IC engine components can be classified into **two broad families**:

### A. Stationary (Fixed) Components
| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine Block** | Houses cylinders, crankcase, coolant jackets | Cast Iron / Aluminum Alloy |
| **Cylinder Head** | Seals cylinder from top; houses valves, spark plug/injector | Cast Iron / Aluminum Alloy |
| **Cylinder Liner (Sleeve)** | Inner wear surface for piston | Cast Iron (centrifugal) |
| **Oil Sump (Pan)** | Reservoir for engine oil | Pressed Steel / Aluminum |
| **Intake Manifold** | Distributes air-fuel to cylinders | Aluminum Alloy |
| **Exhaust Manifold** | Collects exhaust gases | Cast Iron |

### B. Moving Components
| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Piston** | Reciprocates under gas pressure | Aluminum Alloy (Silicon-rich) |
| **Piston Pin (Gudgeon Pin)** | Connects piston to connecting rod | Case-hardened Alloy Steel |
| **Connecting Rod (ConRod)** | Transmits force from piston to crank | Forged Steel / Drop-forged |
| **Crankshaft** | Converts reciprocating → rotary motion | Forged Steel / Cast Iron |
| **Camshaft** | Operates valves via lobes | Chilled Cast Iron / Steel |
| **Valves (Intake & Exhaust)** | Control gas exchange | Heat-resistant Alloy Steel |
| **Rocker Arm** | Transfers cam motion to valve | Cast Steel |
| **Tappet / Lifter** | Pushes up on rocker arm | Chilled Cast Iron |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Rule:** Stationary components are typically made from **cast iron** (good damping, wear resistance, low cost), while reciprocating components are **aluminum alloys** (lightweight → reduces inertia forces) and highly stressed rotating parts are **forged steels** (high fatigue strength).

---

## 1.4 Functional Block Schematic of a Single-Cylinder IC Engine

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
   │     CYLINDER HEAD (Top)      │ ← Houses valves, spark plug/injector
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │          PISTON              │ ← Reciprocates (TDC ↔ BDC)
   │        ↕ (linear)            │
   │     CYLINDER BLOCK           │ ← Houses cylinder bore
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │   CONNECTING ROD (slanted)   │ ← Transmits force
   │          \                   │
   │     CRANKSHAFT (rotates)     │ ← Output shaft
   ├──────────────────────────────┤
   │      OIL SUMP (Pan)          │ ← Lubricant reservoir
   └──────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Weight Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 carries ~20–25% weightage** in OEMET613 End Semester Examination (ESE). Direct 14-mark questions on **engine components, valve train, and crank-slider kinematics** appear almost every semester. Students frequently lose marks on:
> - Sketching and labeling
> - Forgetting materials
> - Missing functions
> - Confusing Tappet vs. Rocker Arm vs. Valve spring roles
> - Wrong direction of motion in OHV vs. OHC mechanisms
]<]minimax[>[</section>

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Engine Block — The Backbone

### Definition
The **engine block** is the largest single casting of an engine. It houses the **cylinder bores**, **crankcase**, **water jackets (coolant passages)**, **bearing supports (main bearing caps)**, and mounting bosses.

### Types
1. **Inline Block** — Cylinders in a single row (I3, I4, I6).
2. **V-Block** — Two banks at angle (V6, V8) — typical 60° or 90° V-angle.
3. **Flat (Boxer / Horizontally Opposed)** — Two banks 180° apart.
4. **Radial Block** — Cylinders arranged radially (aircraft engines).

### Key Features
- **Deck face** — Top machined surface that mates with cylinder head via **head gasket**.
- **Cylinder bore** — May be **integral** (block-cast) or **liner-inserted** (dry or wet liner).
- **Crankcase** — Lower portion that encloses the crankshaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wet Liner vs. Dry Liner:**
> - **Wet Liner:** Surrounded by coolant on the outside. Easier to replace. Used in heavy-duty diesel engines.
> - **Dry Liner:** Press-fit into the block, no coolant contact. Better cooling control but harder to replace.

---

## 2.2 Cylinder Head — The "Brain" of Combustion

### Definition
The **cylinder head** is a complex casting bolted to the top of the engine block. It seals the combustion chamber from above and houses:
- **Intake and Exhaust valves** (with seats and guides)
- **Spark plug** (SI engines) or **Fuel Injector** (CI engines)
- **Camshaft** (in OHC designs)
- **Combustion chamber cavity**
- **Coolant passages** continuous with the block

### Materials
- **Cast Iron** — heavy, good wear resistance, used in diesel heavy-duty
- **Aluminum Alloy** — lightweight, better heat dissipation, modern petrol cars

### Combustion Chamber Shapes
| Chamber Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hemispherical (Hemi)** | Dome-shaped; valve at angle | High-performance SI |
| **Wedge** | Trapezoidal cross-section | Most SI engines |
| **Bath-tub** | Rectangular cavity | Older SI engines |
| **Direct Injection (DI)** | Bowl-in-piston | Modern diesel |

---

## 2.3 Oil Sump (Crankcase Pan)

### Definition
The **oil sump** is a reservoir mounted at the bottom of the engine block. It stores engine oil when the engine is off and provides the suction feed to the **oil pump** through a strainer (pickup tube).

### Features
- **Baffled sump** — prevents oil slosh during cornering (racing / performance vehicles).
- **Dry Sump** — Uses a separate external tank + scavenge pump (used in racing, aircraft).
- **Wet Sump** — Standard automotive design.
- Capacity: typically **3–6 liters** for a passenger car engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sump is the **lowest point of the engine** and must be designed to clear road obstacles (ground clearance). It is also the first point struck during a bottoming-out event — hence made from pressed steel (1.2–2 mm) with protective coatings.

---

## 2.4 Cylinder & Cylinder Liner

### Definition
The **cylinder** is the hollow bore in which the piston reciprocates. It must withstand:
- High combustion pressures (**40–80 bar** in SI, **60–200 bar** in CI diesel)
- High temperatures (**2000–2500°C** peak gas temperature)
- Sliding friction from piston rings
- Corrosion from combustion byproducts

### Cylinder Volume Calculations (Critical KTU Formulas)

Let:
- $D$ = Cylinder bore (diameter), in mm
- $L$ = Stroke length, in mm

**Swept Volume (Displacement per cylinder):**

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L
$$

**Clearance Volume:**
$$
V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 (L_c) \quad \text{where } L_c \text{ is clearance height (gap between piston crown at TDC and cylinder head)}
$$

**Total Cylinder Volume:**
$$
V_1 = V_s + V_c
$$

**Compression Ratio:**
$$
r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c}
$$

**Typical Compression Ratios:**
- Petrol (SI): **8:1 to 12:1**
- Diesel (CI): **14:1 to 22:1**

---

## 2.5 Piston — The Heart of the Engine

### Definition
The **piston** is a cylindrical component that fits inside the cylinder bore and reciprocates between TDC (Top Dead Center) and BDC (Bottom Dead Center). It receives the gas pressure from combustion and transmits it through the connecting rod to the crankshaft.

### Functions
1. Receive combustion gas pressure and transmit to connecting rod.
2. Seal combustion chamber using **piston rings**.
3. Conduct heat away from combustion chamber to cylinder walls.

### Major Parts of a Piston
| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Crown (Head)** | Top surface exposed to combustion |
| **Ring Zone** | Houses compression and oil control rings |
| **Skirt** | Guides piston in bore, prevents tilt |
| **Pin Boss** | Internal bosses that hold the gudgeon pin |
| **Wrist Pin Bore** | Hole for piston pin |

### Piston Ring Functions
1. **Top Compression Ring(s)** — Seal combustion gases above piston.
2. **Middle (Wiper) Ring** — Scrapes oil up, balances film.
3. **Oil Control Ring** — Scrapes excess oil back to sump.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Ring Count:** Petrol engine = 2 compression + 1 oil control = **3 rings**. Diesel engine = 1 compression + 1 wiper + 1 oil = **3 rings** (with sometimes an additional top ring in heavy duty).

---

## 2.6 Piston Pin (Gudgeon Pin / Wrist Pin)

### Definition
A hollow, hardened cylindrical pin that connects the **piston** to the **small end of the connecting rod**, allowing relative oscillation (articulation) between them.

### Mounting Types
| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed in Piston** | Press-fit in pin boss; rotates in rod | Older designs, heavy-duty |
| **Fixed in Rod** | Press-fit in rod; rotates in piston boss | Rare |
| **Floating Pin** | Free in both piston and rod, held by circlips | **Most modern** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **floating pin** is the most widely used design. It must be **lubricated by splash oil** because it has no dedicated oil supply. Made from **case-hardened alloy steel** (e.g., EN353) with surface hardness ~ **58–62 HRC**.

---

## 2.7 Connecting Rod (Con Rod)

### Definition
A **connecting rod** is a high-strength link that connects the **piston pin (small end)** to the **crankshaft (big end)**. It transmits the gas force from the piston and converts reciprocating motion into rotary motion.

### Anatomy
- **Small End (Top)** — Houses piston pin; has a phosphor bronze bush.
- **Shank (I-beam or H-beam)** — Lightweight, high rigidity section.
- **Big End (Bottom)** — Splits in two; houses the **crank pin** with a bearing shell.

### Big-End Split Designs
1. **Plain split (Big end on top, away from crank)** — Easier assembly.
2. **Saw-cut split (Big end on bottom)** — Better for heavy-duty.

### Material: **Drop-Forged Steel** (e.g., 40Cr, 42CrMo) — Heat-treated for fatigue resistance.

---

## 2.8 Crankshaft — The Rotary Output Shaft

### Definition
The **crankshaft** is the main rotating member of the engine. It converts the reciprocating motion of the piston (via the connecting rod) into rotary motion. It also drives the camshaft, oil pump, water pump, and alternator via belts/gears.

### Key Elements
| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Main Journals** | Rotated in main bearings (fixed to block) |
| **Crank Pins (Rod Journals)** | Connect to connecting rod big end |
| **Crank Webs (Cheeks)** | Connect journals to pins |
| **Counterweights** | Balance rotating & reciprocating masses |
| **Flywheel Mount Flange** | Attached at rear for clutch mounting |

### Types of Crankshaft
1. **Sliding (Plain) Bearing Type** — Two main bearings + rod journal (old single-cylinder).
2. **Full-Floating** — Each rod journal supported on both sides.
3. **Semi-floating** — Common in modern multi-cylinder engines.

### Firing Order
For a 4-cylinder inline engine, typical firing order: **1-3-4-2** or **1-2-4-3**.
For V8: **1-8-4-3-6-5-7-2** (Chevrolet small-block) or **1-5-4-2-6-3-7-8** (Ford).

---

## 2.9 Valve Train — The Breathing System

### Definition
The **valve train** is the system of components that opens and closes the **intake** and **exhaust valves** at the correct time, in the correct sequence, and for the correct duration.

### Components of Valve Train
1. **Camshaft** — Rotating shaft with egg-shaped lobes.
2. **Camshaft Drive** — Belt, chain, or gears.
3. **Tappets (Lifters)** — Ride on the cam lobe; push upward.
4. **Push Rods** (in OHV only) — Long rods transmitting motion.
5. **Rocker Arms** — Pivot and push down on valve stem.
6. **Valves** — Final seal of combustion chamber.
7. **Valve Springs** — Close valve when cam lobe passes.
8. **Valve Guides & Seats** — Guide and seal the valve.

### Major Valve Train Configurations

#### A. Pushrod / OHV (Overhead Valve)
```
   Camshaft (in Block) → Tappet → Pushrod → Rocker Arm → Valve (in Head)
```
- Camshaft is **in the block**, valves are **in the head**.
- Longer, less rigid linkage → limits RPM.
- **Used in:** Heavy-duty trucks, muscle cars (e.g., Chevrolet small-block V8).

#### B. SOHC (Single Overhead Cam)
```
   Camshaft (in Head) → Rocker Arm / Direct acting → Valve
```
- Camshaft directly above valves (no pushrod).
- **Used in:** Honda, Toyota, most modern 4-cylinder engines.

#### C. DOHC (Double Overhead Cam)
```
   Two Camshafts (Intake & Exhaust) → Bucket Tappet / Finger Follower → Valve
```
- Separate cams for intake and exhaust.
- Allows **4 valves per cylinder** (2 intake + 2 exhaust).
- **Used in:** Honda VTEC, BMW, Ferrari, modern high-performance.

#### D. OHV with Desmodromic Valve (No Springs)
- Mechanical valve opening AND closing — used in **Ducati motorcycles, F1 engines**.
- Eliminates valve float at very high RPM.

---

## 2.10 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — IC Engine Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 6 formulas for any KTU numerical/derivation question on this module.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Swept Volume (per cylinder) | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | m³ or cc | $D$ = bore, $L$ = stroke |
| 2 | Total Cylinder Volume | $V_1 = V_s + V_c$ | m³ | $V_c$ = clearance volume |
| 3 | Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | dimensionless | SI: 8–12; CI: 14–22 |
| 4 | Engine Displacement | $V_{total} = N_c \times V_s$ | m³ | $N_c$ = number of cylinders |
| 5 | Mean Effective Pressure | $P_m = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_s \cdot N}$ | Pa or bar | $W_{net}$ = net work/cycle, $N$ = rpm |
| 6 | Piston Speed (mean) | $\bar{V}_p = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ | m/s | Used for valve train design |

**Additional Quick Reference:**
- **Mean Piston Speed Constraint:** $\bar{V}_p \leq 25$ m/s (passenger cars), $\leq 30$ m/s (high-performance).
- **Compression Ratio (Otto):** SI petrol: 9–11; Premium: 12–14.
- **Valve Timing (typical SI):** IVO 10° BTDC, IVC 40° ABDC, EVO 50° BBDC, EVC 10° ATDC.

---

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Formula 1 Engines (1.6L V6 Turbo, ~1000 HP):**
   - Use **desmodromic valve train** (no springs) to prevent valve float at 15,000 RPM.
   - Forged steel crankshaft, titanium connecting rods, forged aluminum pistons.

2. **Heavy-Duty Diesel (Cummins ISX, 15L, 600 HP):**
   - Wet liners replaceable without engine removal.
   - Pushrod OHV for maximum low-end torque.

3. **Hybrid Engines (Toyota Atkinson Cycle):**
   - Modified valve timing — late IVC improves effective expansion ratio.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Compression Ratio from First Principles

### Problem Setup
A 4-cylinder petrol engine has:
- Bore $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $L = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.090 \text{ m}$
- Clearance volume per cylinder $V_c = 40 \text{ cm}^3 = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

**Required:** Compression ratio $r$ and total engine displacement.

---

### Step 1 — Compute Swept Volume (per cylinder)

Convert dimensions to SI units (meters):
- $D = 0.080$ m
- $L = 0.090$ m

Apply the swept volume formula:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^2 \times (0.090)
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.090
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 5.76 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
V_s = 0.7854 \times 5.76 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
V_s = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

Convert to cm³ (multiply by $10^6$):

$$
V_s = 452.4 \text{ cm}^3 \quad \text{(per cylinder)}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute Total Cylinder Volume $V_1$

$$
V_1 = V_s + V_c
$$

$$
V_1 = 452.4 \text{ cm}^3 + 40 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
V_1 = 492.4 \text{ cm}^3
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute Compression Ratio

$$
r = \frac{V_1}{V_c}
$$

$$
r = \frac{492.4}{40}
$$

$$
r = 12.31
$$

**Comment:** This falls within the typical petrol range (8–14:1). For modern knock-resistant engines using 95-octane fuel, this ratio is realistic.

---

### Step 4 — Compute Total Engine Displacement

For $N_c = 4$ cylinders:

$$
V_{total} = N_c \times V_s
$$

$$
V_{total} = 4 \times 452.4 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
V_{total} = 1809.5 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 1.81 \text{ L}
$$

**Engineering Comment:** This is a typical 1.8-liter 4-cylinder petrol engine (similar to Toyota 2ZR-FE, Honda R18A).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Mean Piston Speed

### Problem Setup
A motorcycle engine:
- Stroke $L = 0.060$ m (60 mm)
- Maximum rated RPM $N = 12{,}000$ rpm

**Required:** Mean piston speed.

---

### Step 1 — Conceptual Basis

The piston travels **2 × stroke** (TDC → BDC → TDC) per **one crank revolution**. Therefore:

$$
\text{Piston distance per rev} = 2L
$$

For $N$ revolutions per minute, the total piston distance per minute is $2L \cdot N$.

---

### Step 2 — Convert to SI (m/s)

Piston speed in meters per second:

$$
\bar{V}_p = \frac{2L \cdot N}{60}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\bar{V}_p = \frac{2 \times 0.060 \times 12{,}000}{60}
$$

$$
\bar{V}_p = \frac{1440}{60}
$$

$$
\bar{V}_p = 24 \text{ m/s}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Validation Against Engineering Limits

| Engine Type | Max Mean Piston Speed |
|---|---|
| Passenger Car SI | 20–25 m/s |
| Performance SI | 25–30 m/s |
| Formula 1 | ~30 m/s |
| Diesel (heavy-duty) | 10–13 m/s |

**Conclusion:** $\bar{V}_p = 24$ m/s is on the **upper edge** for a high-revving motorcycle engine. Anything beyond 30 m/s risks **ring flutter, bore wear, and valve float**.

---

## 3.3 Force Analysis on Piston (Kinematic Derivation)

### Setup
The piston is connected to the crank via the connecting rod. Let:
- $r$ = crank radius (half of stroke) = $L/2$
- $\ell$ = connecting rod length
- $\theta$ = crank angle measured from TDC

---

### Step 1 — Piston Displacement from TDC

By the **slider-crank kinematics** (approximate exact formula):

$$
x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r^2}{2\ell}(1 - \cos 2\theta)
$$

**Explanation:**
- First term: primary sinusoidal motion (piston follows crank).
- Second term: secondary term due to finite connecting rod length.
- For $\ell \gg r$ (typical automotive: $\ell/r \approx 3$ to 4), the secondary term is small (a few percent).

---

### Step 2 — Piston Velocity

Differentiate $x$ with respect to time. Since $\theta = \omega t$ (constant crank speed), $\dot\theta = \omega$:

$$
v = \frac{dx}{dt} = r \omega \left[ \sin\theta + \frac{r}{2\ell}\sin 2\theta \right]
$$

At $\theta = 90°$ (mid-stroke):

$$
v_{max} \approx r\omega \quad \text{(since } \sin 90° = 1, \sin 180° = 0\text{)}
$$

In RPM ($N$):

$$
v_{max} \approx \frac{\pi L N}{60}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Piston Acceleration (Inertia Force Source)

Differentiate $v$ with respect to $t$:

$$
a = \frac{dv}{dt} = r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r}{\ell}\cos 2\theta \right]
$$

The inertia force on the reciprocating mass $m$ is:

$$
F_{inertia} = m \cdot a = m \cdot r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r}{\ell}\cos 2\theta \right]
$$

---

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

Given:
- Crank radius $r = 0.045$ m
- Con-rod length $\ell = 0.150$ m
- Reciprocating mass $m = 0.5$ kg
- Engine speed $N = 3000$ rpm

Compute angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}
$$

At $\theta = 0°$ (TDC, peak inertia force):

$$
F_{inertia,max} = 0.5 \times 0.045 \times (314.16)^2 \times \left[ 1 + \frac{0.045}{0.150} \right]
$$

$$
F_{inertia,max} = 0.5 \times 0.045 \times 98{,}696 \times [1 + 0.3]
$$

$$
F_{inertia,max} = 0.5 \times 0.045 \times 98{,}696 \times 1.3
$$

$$
F_{inertia,max} \approx 2886 \text{ N}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> This **2886 N inertia force** at TDC (3000 rpm) is what the **piston pin, con-rod small end, and cylinder bore wall** must handle **every revolution** (twice per cycle in 4-stroke). This is why **lighter reciprocating parts** (aluminum pistons, forged steel rods) and **good bearing design** are critical for durability.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Valve Train Types

| Feature | OHV (Pushrod) | SOHC | DOHC | Desmodromic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Camshaft Location** | In block | In head (1) | In head (2) | In head |
| **Valves per Cylinder** | 2 typical | 2–4 | 4–5 | 4 |
| **Max Practical RPM** | ~5500 | ~7500 | ~9000+ | 15,000+ |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Maintenance** | Easy | Moderate | Hard | Specialist |
| **Power Density** | Low | High | Very High | Extreme |
| **Application** | Truck, muscle car | Honda i-VTEC | BMW, Audi | Ducati, F1 |
| **Valve Float Risk** | High at RPM | Moderate | Low | None (no spring) |

## 4.5 Single-Cylinder 4-Stroke Cycle — Component Activation Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    participant V as Valves
    participant C as Camshaft
    participant S as Spark Plug

    Note over P,S: STROKE 1 - INTAKE
    P->>P: Moves TDC to BDC
    C->>V: Opens Intake Valve
    V-->>P: Air-Fuel enters cylinder

    Note over P,S: STROKE 2 - COMPRESSION
    P->>P: Moves BDC to TDC
    C->>V: Both valves closed
    Note right of P: Mixture compressed 9 to 12 times

    Note over P,S: STROKE 3 - POWER
    S->>S: Fires spark near TDC
    Note right of P: Combustion raises pressure to 40 bar
    P->>P: Forced from TDC to BDC
    Note right of P: This is the WORK stroke

    Note over P,S: STROKE 4 - EXHAUST
    P->>P: Moves BDC to TDC
    C->>V: Opens Exhaust Valve
    V-->>P: Exhaust gases expelled
