# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six major components of an IC engine and state their functions briefly.**

#### Model Answer (6 points):

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Engine Block** | Main structural casting housing the cylinders and crankcase |
| 2 | **Cylinder Head** | Seals the combustion chamber from the top; houses valves and spark plug/injector |
| 3 | **Piston** | Reciprocates to receive combustion pressure and transmit force to the connecting rod |
| 4 | **Connecting Rod** | Links piston to crankshaft; converts reciprocating to rotary motion |
| 5 | **Crankshaft** | Output shaft; converts piston motion into rotation and delivers power |
| 6 | **Valve Train** | Controls timing and opening/closing of intake and exhaust valves |
| 7 | **Oil Sump** | Reservoir for engine lubricating oil |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner Tip: 6 components × 0.5 = 3 marks. Naming alone gets partial credit; **function must accompany each**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Differentiate between OHV and DOHC valve trains. (Any 3 points)**

#### Model Answer:

| Parameter | OHV (Overhead Valve) | DOHC (Double Overhead Cam) |
|---|---|---|
| Camshaft location | In the engine block | In the cylinder head (two camshafts) |
| Drive mechanism | Pushrods + rocker arms | Direct acting bucket tappets |
| Valves per cylinder | Usually 2 | Usually 4 (better breathing) |
| Maximum RPM | Limited (~5500) | Higher (~8000–9000+) |
| Complexity & Cost | Simple, cheap | Complex, expensive |
| Power output | Lower | Higher (better volumetric efficiency) |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and function of the **cylinder block** and **cylinder head** of an IC engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **piston assembly** of an IC engine with a labeled diagram. Explain the functions of **piston rings**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Cylinder Block & Cylinder Head

**Cylinder Block (3.5 marks):**
- Largest single casting of the engine; made of **cast iron or aluminum alloy**.
- Houses **cylinder bores** (where pistons reciprocate), **crankcase** (where crankshaft rotates), and **water jackets** (coolant passages).
- Contains **main bearing caps** that support the crankshaft.
- Top surface = **deck face** — machined for head gasket sealing.
- Bottom surface mates with **oil sump** via gasket.

**Cylinder Head (3.5 marks):**
- Bolted to the top of the block using **head bolts** (torqued in a star sequence) and sealed with a **multi-layer steel (MLS) head gasket**.
- Houses **intake and exhaust valves**, **valve guides**, **valve seats**, **valve springs**, **spark plug** (SI) or **fuel injector** (CI), and in OHC designs, the **camshaft(s)**.
- Contains the **combustion chamber cavity** — geometry affects flame propagation and knock resistance.
- Material: cast iron (heavy duty) or aluminum alloy (light duty).

**Sketch Reference:** Use the Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 as a labeled guide.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Piston Assembly

**Piston Anatomy (3.5 marks):**
- **Crown (Head)** — top surface; may be flat, domed, or dished depending on combustion chamber shape.
- **Ring Zone** — contains grooves for piston rings (typically 3: 2 compression + 1 oil control).
- **Skirt** — lower cylindrical portion that guides the piston in the bore. Often slightly **oval** (barrel-shaped) to compensate for thermal expansion and piston "rocking".
- **Pin Boss** — internal reinforced bosses containing the **gudgeon pin bore**.

**Material:** Aluminum-silicon alloy (e.g., A390, A4032) for light weight and good heat conduction. For high-performance, **forged aluminum** is used.

**Piston Ring Functions (3.5 marks):**

| Ring | Function |
|---|---|
| **Top Compression Ring** | Primary seal against combustion gas leakage (blow-by) into crankcase |
| **Second Compression / Wiper Ring** | Secondary gas seal + scrapes oil from cylinder wall upward |
| **Oil Control Ring** | Scrapes excess oil from bore back into sump, controls oil consumption |

**Additional functions of rings:**
- Transfer heat from piston to cylinder wall (cooling effect — up to **30–40%** of piston heat is shed this way).
- Maintain proper oil film thickness between piston and bore.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **valve train** of an IC engine with a neat sketch. Compare **OHV, SOHC, and DOHC** configurations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **connecting rod** and **crankshaft** with diagrams. Discuss materials used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Valve Train

**Components (3 marks):**
1. **Camshaft** — shaft with eccentric lobes; one lobe per valve.
2. **Drive mechanism** — belt (timing belt), chain, or gears (1:2 ratio to crankshaft).
3. **Tappets / Lifters** — ride on cam lobe, translate rotation to vertical motion.
4. **Pushrods** — long rods (OHV only) carrying motion up to head.
5. **Rocker Arms** — pivot to convert pushrod motion into downward valve motion.
6. **Valves** — poppet valves with stems, heads, and spring retainers.
7. **Valve Springs** — helical springs that close valves.
8. **Valve Guides & Seats** — guide valve stem and provide sealing surface.

**OHV vs. SOHC vs. DOHC Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | OHV | SOHC | DOHC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cam location | Block | Head | Head (×2) |
| Linkage | Pushrod + Rocker | Rocker / Direct | Direct bucket |
| Valves/cyl | 2 | 2–4 | 4–5 |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| RPM limit | ~5500 | ~7500 | ~9000+ |
| Application | Trucks, V8 muscle | Family sedans | Sports, premium |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Connecting Rod & Crankshaft

**Connecting Rod (3.5 marks):**
- **Small End** — Bronze-bushed eye connected to gudgeon pin.
- **Shank** — I-section or H-section beam (high rigidity, low mass).
- **Big End** — Splits in two halves (cap + rod) fastened by two **big-end bolts**; houses crank pin via bearing shell.
- Material: **drop-forged steel** (e.g., EN8, 40Cr, 42CrMo4); heat-treated to 28–32 HRC for fatigue life.
- Function: transmits gas force from piston to crank, converting reciprocating to rotary motion.

**Crankshaft (3.5 marks):**
- **Main Journals** — supported in main bearings (fixed to block).
- **Crank Pins** — offset from axis; connect to con-rod big ends.
- **Crank Webs / Cheeks** — connect main journals to crank pins; carry **counterweights**.
- **Front Pulley** — drives timing belt/chain, water pump, alternator.
- **Rear Flange** — mounts flywheel.
- Material: **forged steel** (most common) or **nodular cast iron** for moderate-duty engines.
- Counterweights balance rotating inertia and partially reciprocating inertia.

**Working:** As piston pushes down via con-rod, the crank rotates around the main journal axis, converting linear force into rotary torque output.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Module 2 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to label the sketch** — Examiner expects at least **6 labels** on any diagram. Unlabeled sketch = **0 marks** for that component.
> 2. **Confusing "Block" with "Head"** — Block houses cylinders; Head seals the top. Many students mix up.
> 3. **Missing materials** — Examiner expects **cast iron, aluminum alloy, forged steel** as standard answers. Omitting material = loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong ring function** — Oil control ring does NOT seal gases; it controls oil. Compression rings seal gases.
> 5. **Forgetting firing order** — A 14-mark question on crankshaft without firing order is incomplete.
> 6. **Skipping pushrods** — In OHV sketch, pushrod MUST be present. Students often omit it.
> 7. **Compression ratio = wrong formula** — Use $(V_s + V_c)/V_c$, not $V_s/V_c$.
> 8. **No units in numericals** — Always state **mm, cm³, m³, bar, N, m/s** explicitly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 An IC engine has **stationary parts** (block, head, sump, manifolds) and **moving parts** (piston, rod, crank, valves, cam).
- 🔹 **Engine block** is the main casting; **cylinder head** seals the top and houses valves.
- 🔹 **Cylinder bore × stroke** gives swept volume; **swept + clearance = total volume**; ratio = compression ratio.
- 🔹 **Piston** transmits force via **gudgeon pin → connecting rod → crankshaft**.
- 🔹 **Crankshaft** has **main journals, crank pins, webs, and counterweights**.
- 🔹 **Valve train** controls gas exchange. **OHV, SOHC, DOHC** are the 3 main configurations.
- 🔹 **Piston rings** = 2 compression + 1 oil control; functions = gas seal, oil control, heat transfer.
- 🔹 **Firing order** for inline 4 = 1-3-4-2 (or 1-2-4-3).
- 🔹 **Compression ratio:** SI 8–12, CI 14–22. **Mean piston speed:** ≤ 25 m/s (passenger), ≤ 30 m/s (racing).
- 🔹 **Materials:** Block = cast iron; Head = Al alloy; Piston = Al-Si; Con-rod & Crank = forged steel.
- 🔹 **Wet sump** = oil inside pan; **Dry sump** = external reservoir (used in racing).
- 🔹 **Tappet** rides cam; **Pushrod** transmits to rocker (OHV only); **Rocker arm** pushes valve.
- 🔹 **Valve spring** closes valve; **Bucket tappet** (DOHC) directly actuates valve.
- 🔹 Always draw a **labeled diagram** — even a simple line drawing with 6 labels scores full marks.
- 🔹 Master the **6 KTU formulas** in the cheat sheet (Section 2.10).
- 🔹 **Floating gudgeon pin** is the modern standard, retained by circlips, splash-lubricated.
]<]minimax[>[</section>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combustion Process - A/F Ratio, Self-Ignition Temperature, Octane and Cetane Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET613 - AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Combustion Process - A/F Ratio, Self-Ignition Temperature, Octane and Cetane Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Combustion Process in IC Engines

**Combustion** is the rapid exothermic chemical reaction between a hydrocarbon fuel and an oxidizer (air) that releases thermal energy, which is then partially converted into mechanical work inside the cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):** *Combustion in an IC engine is the chemical oxidation of a hydrocarbon fuel mixed with air, releasing heat at a controlled rate to produce high-pressure gases that drive the piston.*

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of combustion like **lighting a Bunsen burner in a closed, sealed pressure cooker that has a movable lid (the piston)**. When the air-fuel mixture is ignited:
- A small **spark** (SI engine) or **heat of compression** (CI engine) initiates the reaction.
- The hot expanding gases push the lid (piston) down — this is your **mechanical work**.
- For a smooth ride, this "push" must be steady, not explosive.

---

## 1.2 Air-Fuel (A/F) Ratio

The **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)** is the mass ratio of air to fuel supplied to the engine cylinder for combustion.

$$A/F = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric A/F Ratio:** The chemically exact ratio at which *all* the fuel and *all* the oxygen are completely consumed with no excess of either. For gasoline: **14.7 : 1** (≈ **15 : 1** for pure octane).

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine making **maggi noodles** — the recipe says "1 packet : 2 cups of water." If you use too much water, the noodles are **watery (lean)**; too little water, they are **dry and burnt (rich)**. The A/F ratio is exactly this — the perfect "recipe" for complete combustion.

### Categories of A/F Mixtures

| Mixture Type | A/F Value | Condition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stoichiometric** | 14.7 : 1 | Theoretical perfect mix | Lambda sensors target this |
| **Lean** | > 14.7 : 1 | Excess air | SI cruise, lean-burn engines |
| **Rich** | < 14.7 : 1 | Excess fuel | SI WOT, CI idle |

---

## 1.3 Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT)

**Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT)**, also called **auto-ignition temperature**, is the **minimum temperature** at which a fuel-air mixture spontaneously ignites **without any external spark or flame**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** SIT is the *fundamental* property that distinguishes **SI (Spark Ignition)** from **CI (Compression Ignition)** engines. In SI engines, the mixture must NOT self-ignite before the spark (avoid knock). In CI engines, the mixture MUST self-ignite due to compression heat.

### Intuitive Analogy
SIT is like the temperature at which **paper catches fire on its own** when left on a hot pan. There is no matchstick — just heat. Diesel has a **lower** SIT than petrol, which is why a diesel engine can "self-light" from compression heat, but petrol cannot.

### Typical SIT Values
- **Petrol (iso-octane):** ~ **415 °C**
- **Diesel (n-cetane):** ~ **210 °C**
- **Methane:** ~ **540 °C**
- **Hydrogen:** ~ **500 °C**

---

## 1.4 Octane Number (ON)

**Octane Number** is a measure of a fuel's **resistance to knock (uncontrolled auto-ignition)** in a **Spark Ignition (SI)** engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher Octane Number = Greater resistance to knock = Safer for high-compression SI engines.**

### Intuitive Analogy
Octane number is like the **"discipline rating" of a fuel**. A high-octane fuel is a *disciplined student* — it waits patiently for the spark. A low-octane fuel is *impatient* — it self-ignites prematurely, causing the engine to "knock" like a student tapping the desk before being called.

### Reference Fuels
- **Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane):** ON = **100** (excellent knock resistance)
- **n-Heptane:** ON = **0** (very poor knock resistance — knocks violently)

A fuel with **ON = 90** behaves like a blend of 90% iso-octane and 10% n-heptane.

---

## 1.5 Cetane Number (CN)

**Cetane Number** is a measure of a fuel's **ignition quality (ignition delay)** in a **Compression Ignition (CI)** engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher Cetane Number = Shorter ignition delay = Quick, smooth ignition in CI engines.**

### Intuitive Analogy
Cetane number is like the **"responsiveness rating"** of a fuel. A high-cetane fuel is an *alert student* — when the teacher calls (compression heat reaches it), it answers immediately. A low-cetane fuel is *slow to react*, causing rough engine running (diesel knock).

### Reference Fuels
- **n-Cetane (n-Hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄):** CN = **100** (excellent ignition quality)
- **α-Methylnaphthalene:** CN = **0** (very poor ignition quality)
- **Heptamethylnonane (HMN):** CN = 15 (modern reference)

A fuel with **CN = 50** behaves like a blend of 50% n-cetane and 50% α-methylnaphthalene.

---

## 1.6 Quick Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Octane Number (ON) | Cetane Number (CN) |
|---|---|---|
| Engine type | SI (Petrol) | CI (Diesel) |
| What it measures | Resistance to **knock** | Quality of **ignition** |
| High value means | Less knock (good for SI) | Quick ignition (good for CI) |
| Reference 100 fuel | Iso-octane | n-Cetane |
| Reference 0 fuel | n-Heptane | α-Methylnaphthalene |
| Typical fuel range | 80 – 100 (petrol) | 40 – 60 (diesel) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stoichiometric Combustion — General Reaction

For a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_y$:

$$C_xH_y + a(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O + d\,N_2$$

**Stoichiometric coefficient of O₂ (a):**
$$a = x + \frac{y}{4}$$

**Coefficient of N₂ (d):**
$$d = 3.76\,a = 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)$$

**Coefficient of CO₂ (b):**
$$b = x$$

**Coefficient of H₂O (c):**
$$c = \frac{y}{2}$$

---

## 2.2 A/F Ratio — Theoretical Formulation

The mass of air required per kg of fuel (stoichiometric):

$$m_{air,\,stoich} = \frac{a \times (32 + 3.76 \times 28)}{x \times 12 + y \times 1}$$

The mass of fuel per mole:

$$m_{fuel} = 12x + y \quad \text{[kg/kmol fuel]}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{32 + 105.28}{12x + y}\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) = \frac{137.28\,(4x + y)}{4(12x + y)}}$$

---

## 2.3 Equivalence Ratio (φ) and Excess Air

### Equivalence Ratio (φ)
$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}} = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$$

### Lambda (λ) — Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\phi} = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **λ = 1** → Stoichiometric  
> **λ > 1** → Lean mixture (excess air)  
> **λ < 1** → Rich mixture (excess fuel)

### Percentage of Excess Air
$$\%\,Excess\,Air = (\lambda - 1) \times 100$$

### Percentage of Excess Air (Direct)
$$\%\,Excess\,Air = \left(\frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} - 1\right) \times 100$$

### Air-Fuel Ratio from Excess Air
$$(A/F)_{actual} = (A/F)_{stoich} \times (1 + \frac{\%\,Excess\,Air}{100})$$

---

## 2.4 Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT) — Key Engineering Insights

### Factors Affecting SIT
1. **Fuel chemistry** — Longer hydrocarbon chains → lower SIT
2. **Pressure** — Higher pressure → lower SIT
3. **A/F ratio** — Stoichiometric mixtures ignite more easily than very lean/rich
4. **Mixture homogeneity** — Better mixing → lower effective SIT
5. **Physical state** — Vapours ignite at lower temperatures than liquids
6. **Combustion chamber geometry** — Surface-to-volume ratio affects heat retention

### Significance in Engine Design

| Engine | Required SIT Behaviour |
|---|---|
| **SI Engine** | Fuel must have **HIGH** SIT (resist auto-ignition) → uses **Octane Number** |
| **CI Engine** | Fuel must have **LOW** SIT (easy to ignite under compression) → uses **Cetane Number** |

---

## 2.5 Octane Number — Detailed Classification

### Two Test Standards
| Test | Standard | Conditions | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RON** (Research Octane Number) | ASTM D2699 / ISO 5164 | 600 rpm, 52 °C intake | **Mild** |
| **MON** (Motor Octane Number) | ASTM D2700 / ISO 5163 | 900 rpm, 149 °C intake, variable ignition | **Severe** |

### Anti-Knock Index (AKI) / Posted Octane Number
$$AKI = \frac{RON + MON}{2}$$

### Sensitivity
$$Sensitivity = RON - MON$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity** indicates how a fuel's knock resistance changes with engine severity. Modern gasolines have sensitivity ≈ 7–12.

### Common Indian Fuel Ratings
| Fuel | RON | MON | AKI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Regular Petrol (BS-VI) | 91 | 81 | 86 |
| Premium Petrol (XP95) | 95 | 85 | 90 |
| Super Premium (XP100) | 100 | 88 | 94 |

---

## 2.6 Cetane Number — Detailed Analysis

### Ignition Delay Period
The time (in crank angle degrees) between fuel injection start and the start of combustion:

$$ID = \theta_{ignition} - \theta_{injection\,start}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Relationship:** CN ↑ → Ignition delay ↓ → Smoother CI combustion → Higher CN fuels are preferred.

### Required Cetane Number for CI Engines
- **Low-speed, large engines:** CN = 30–45
- **High-speed automotive diesels:** CN = 45–55
- **Modern common-rail diesels:** CN = 50–60

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A/F = m_{air}/m_{fuel}$ | Mass A/F ratio | kg/kg |
| 2 | $a = x + y/4$ | Stoichiometric O₂ coefficient | kmol O₂/kmol fuel |
| 3 | $m_{fuel} = 12x + y$ | Mass per kmol of fuel $C_xH_y$ | kg/kmol |
| 4 | $m_{air} = 137.28\,a$ | Mass of air per kmol of fuel | kg |
| 5 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = 137.28\,(4x+y)/[4(12x+y)]$ | Stoichiometric A/F ratio | kg/kg |
| 6 | $\phi = (F/A)_{actual}/(F/A)_{stoich}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ | Air-fuel equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\%\,EA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100$ | Excess air percentage | % |
| 9 | $AKI = (RON + MON)/2$ | Anti-Knock Index | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Sensitivity = RON - MON$ | Octane sensitivity | dimensionless |
| 11 | $ID \propto 1/CN$ | Ignition delay vs. cetane number | crank ° |
| 12 | $\theta_{comb,\,duration} = f(CN, P, T)$ | Combustion duration | crank ° |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Refineries:** Octane and Cetane numbers dictate refining processes (catalytic cracking, isomerization, hydrocracking).
- **Engine Calibration:** ECU maps A/F ratio across the entire operating range using lambda sensors.
- **Emission Control:** Three-way catalytic converters operate optimally only at λ = 1 ± 0.01.
- **Cold Start:** Rich mixtures (λ < 1) are needed; this is when HC emissions peak.
- **Alt-Fuel Selection:** CNG has very high ON (~120) but needs special SI engines; Biodiesel typically has higher CN (~50–55) than mineral diesel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio for Iso-Octane (C₈H₁₈)

This is the **fundamental derivation** the KTU examiner loves to test.

**Step 1: Write the unbalanced combustion reaction**

$$C_8H_{18} + a(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O + d\,N_2$$

**Step 2: Balance Carbon (C) atoms**

Number of C atoms on LHS = 8 → on RHS = b
$$\therefore b = 8$$

**Step 3: Balance Hydrogen (H) atoms**

Number of H atoms on LHS = 18 → on RHS = 2c
$$2c = 18 \implies c = 9$$

**Step 4: Balance Oxygen (O) atoms**

Number of O atoms on LHS = 2a → on RHS = 2b + c
$$2a = 2(8) + 9 = 25 \implies a = 12.5$$

**Step 5: Balance Nitrogen (N) atoms**

Number of N atoms on LHS = 2 × 3.76a → on RHS = 2d
$$2d = 2(3.76)(12.5) \implies d = 47$$

**Step 6: Write the balanced equation**

$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47\,N_2$$

**Step 7: Calculate mass of 1 kmol of fuel**

$$m_{fuel} = 8(12) + 18(1) = 96 + 18 = 114 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 8: Calculate mass of air required per kmol of fuel**

$$m_{air} = a \times M_{air} = 12.5 \times (32 + 3.76 \times 28)$$

$$m_{air} = 12.5 \times (32 + 105.28) = 12.5 \times 137.28$$

$$m_{air} = 1716 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 9: Calculate Stoichiometric A/F Ratio**

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{1716}{114}$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} = 15.053 : 1 \approx 15 : 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **real gasoline** (mixture of hydrocarbons, not pure C₈H₁₈), the accepted value is **14.7 : 1**, because gasoline has an effective composition closer to **C₇H₁₇** or a mixture average.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: A/F Ratio for Methane (CH₄) — Sample Calculation

**Step 1: Write unbalanced reaction**

$$CH_4 + a(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O + d\,N_2$$

**Step 2: Balance C, H, O, N**

- Carbon: $b = 1$
- Hydrogen: $2c = 4 \implies c = 2$
- Oxygen: $2a = 2(1) + 2 = 4 \implies a = 2$
- Nitrogen: $d = 3.76 \times 2 = 7.52$

$$CH_4 + 2(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2$$

**Step 3: Mass of fuel and air**

$$m_{fuel} = 12 + 4(1) = 16 \text{ kg/kmol}$$
$$m_{air} = 2 \times 137.28 = 274.56 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Step 4: A/F Stoichiometric**

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{274.56}{16} = 17.16 : 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matches the well-known stoichiometric A/F for methane: **17.16 : 1**. CNG vehicles require more air per kg of fuel.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalence Ratio and Excess Air Problem

**Given:** A SI engine operates on propane (C₃H₈) with an actual A/F = 16.
**Find:** Equivalence ratio (φ), Lambda (λ), and % excess air.

**Step 1: Stoichiometric A/F for C₃H₈**

$$C_3H_8 + a(O_2 + 3.76N_2) \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O + 3.76a\,N_2$$

$$2a = 6 + 4 = 10 \implies a = 5$$
$$m_{fuel} = 3(12) + 8(1) = 44 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{air} = 5 \times 137.28 = 686.4 \text{ kg}$$
$$(A/F)_{stoich} = 686.4 / 44 = 15.6 : 1$$

**Step 2: Calculate Lambda (λ)**

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} = \frac{16}{15.6} = 1.0256$$

**Step 3: Calculate Equivalence Ratio (φ)**

$$\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.0256} = 0.975$$

**Step 4: Calculate % Excess Air**

$$\%\,EA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100 = (1.0256 - 1) \times 100 = 2.56\,\%$$

**Conclusion:** The mixture is **lean** (λ > 1) with **2.56% excess air** and equivalence ratio of 0.975.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Relative A/F Ratio Using Carbon and Hydrogen

For any hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$:

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{137.28\,(4x + y)}{4(12x + y)}$$

For **C₈H₁₈ (octane)**:
$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{137.28 \times (32 + 18)}{4 \times (96 + 18)} = \frac{137.28 \times 50}{456} = \frac{6864}{456} = 15.05$$

For **C₁₆H₃₄ (cetane)**:
$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{137.28 \times (64 + 34)}{4 \times (192 + 34)} = \frac{137.28 \times 98}{904} = \frac{13453.44}{904} = 14.88$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Heavier hydrocarbons (longer carbon chains) have *lower* stoichiometric A/F ratios. This is why diesel (C₁₆H₃₄ average) needs less air per kg of fuel than petrol (C₈H₁₈ average).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: A/F, ON, and CN for Common Fuels

| Fuel | Formula | $(A/F)_{stoich}$ | SIT (°C) | ON (RON) | CN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Methane** | CH₄ | 17.16 | 540 | 120 | 0 |
| **Propane** | C₃H₈ | 15.60 | 450 | 112 | — |
| **Iso-octane** | C₈H₁₈ | 15.05 | 415 | 100 | — |
| **n-Heptane** | C₇H₁₆ | 15.13 | 215 | 0 | — |
| **Gasoline (avg.)** | ~C₈H₁₈ | 14.70 | 280 | 88–98 | 5–20 |
| **Diesel (avg.)** | ~C₁₂H₂₃ | 14.50 | 210 | 15–25 | 45–55 |
| **n-Cetane** | C₁₆H₃₄ | 14.88 | 205 | — | 100 |
| **Biodiesel (FAME)** | C₁₉H₃₆O₂ | 12.50 | 150 | — | 50–60 |
| **Methanol** | CH₃OH | 6.43 | 385 | 114 | 5 |
| **Ethanol** | C₂H₅OH | 9.00 | 363 | 108 | 8 |

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Ignition Delay vs Cetane Number

Empirical relation (Wolfer's formula, modified):

$$ID\,(\text{crank}^\circ) = \frac{0.00309}{CN} \times e^{(1900/T_a)}$$

Where $T_a$ is the absolute temperature of air at the end of compression (in Kelvin).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As **CN increases**, ignition delay **decreases exponentially**. Doubling CN roughly halves the delay — this is why modern high-speed direct-injection diesels need CN ≥ 50.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Combustion Process — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Intake Air Induction] --> B1[Mixing with Fuel]
    B1 --> C1{A/F Ratio Check}
    C1 -->|lambda greater than 1| D1[Lean Mixture]
    C1 -->|lambda equals 1| E1[Stoichiometric Mixture]
    C1 -->|lambda less than 1| F1[Rich Mixture]
    D1 --> G1[Combustion Chamber]
    E1 --> G1
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1{Ignition Source}
    H1 -->|Spark Plug| I1[SI Engine Combustion]
    H1 -->|Compression Heat| I2[CI Engine Combustion]
    I1 --> J1{Self Ignition Check}
    J1 -->|SIT high enough| K1[Controlled Combustion]
    J1 -->|SIT too low| L1[Engine Knock]
    I2 --> M1{Cetane Quality Check}
    M1 -->|CN high enough| N1[Smooth Ignition]
    M1 -->|CN too low| O1[Diesel Knock]
    K1 --> P1[Mechanical Work Output]
    N1 --> P1
    L1 --> Q1[Power Loss and Damage]
    O1 --> Q1
```

## 4.2 A/F Ratio and Engine Performance Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEAN[Lean Region]
        L1[lambda greater than 1.1] --> L2[Lower BSFC]
        L1 --> L3[Higher NOX]
        L1 --> L4[Lower Power]
    end
    subgraph STOI[Stoichiometric]
        S1[lambda equals 1.0] --> S2[Maximum TWC Efficiency]
        S1 --> S3[Best Compromise]
        S1 --> S4[Reference A/F 14.7:1]
    end
    subgraph RICH[Rich Region]
        R1[lambda less than 0.9] --> R2[Higher Power]
        R1 --> R3[Higher HC and CO]
        R1 --> R4[Lower NOX]
    end
    STOI --> PERF[Optimal Engine Performance]
    LEAN --> PERF
    RICH --> PERF
```

## 4.3 Octane vs Cetane Number — Comparative Reference Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Fuel Property Reference Scale] --> R2{Octane Number}
    R1 --> R3{Cetane Number}
    R2 --> R2A[Reference 100: Iso Octane]
    R2 --> R2B[Reference 0: n Heptane]
    R2A --> R2C[Measures Knock Resistance in SI]
    R2B --> R2C
    R3 --> R3A[Reference 100: n Cetane]
    R3 --> R3B[Reference 0: alpha Methylnaphthalene]
    R3A --> R3C[Measures Ignition Quality in CI]
    R3B --> R3C
    R2C --> R2D[HIGH ON = SAFE for SI]
    R3C --> R3D[HIGH CN = GOOD for CI]
    R2D --> ENG1[SI Engine Fuel Selection]
    R3D --> ENG2[CI Engine Fuel Selection]
```

## 4.4 Self-Ignition Temperature — Engine Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SIT[Self Ignition Temperature Concept] --> F1{Fuel Type}
    F1 -->|Gasoline| G1[SIT around 280 degrees C]
    F1 -->|Diesel| D1[SIT around 210 degrees C]
    F1 -->|CNG| C1[SIT around 540 degrees C]
    G1 --> SI1[SI Engine Risk: Premature Ignition equals KNOCK]
    D1 --> CI1[CI Engine Benefit: Easy Compression Ignition]
    C1 --> CNG1[SI Engine with CNG: Very High Resistance to Knock]
    SI1 --> ACT1[Action: Use HIGH Octane Fuel]
    CI1 --> ACT2[Action: Use HIGH Cetane Fuel]
    CNG1 --> ACT1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Air-Fuel Ratio. What is the stoichiometric A/F ratio for gasoline?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) is the ratio of the mass of air to the mass of fuel supplied to the engine cylinder for combustion. Mathematically, $A/F = m_{air}/m_{fuel}$.

**Stoichiometric A/F for gasoline (2 marks):**  
The stoichiometric A/F ratio is the chemically exact mass ratio at which complete combustion occurs with no excess air or fuel. For gasoline (assumed C₈H₁₈), the stoichiometric A/F ratio is:

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{12.5 \times 137.28}{114} \approx 15.05 : 1$$

For commercial gasoline, the standard value is **14.7 : 1**.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Octane Number and Cetane Number.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Octane Number (ON) | Cetane Number (CN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine Type** | Spark Ignition (SI / Petrol) | Compression Ignition (CI / Diesel) |
| **Property Measured** | Resistance to knock (uncontrolled auto-ignition) | Ignition quality (ignition delay) |
| **Higher value means** | Less knock (safer at high compression) | Shorter ignition delay (better cold start) |
| **Reference 100 fuel** | Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) | n-Cetane (n-Hexadecane) |
| **Reference 0 fuel** | n-Heptane | α-Methylnaphthalene |
| **Typical Range** | Petrol: 80–100 | Diesel: 40–60 |

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**
`[KTU University Exam - Module 2 Internal Choice, May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for octane (C₈H₁₈). Assume air contains 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diesel engine operates with an actual A/F ratio of 18. The fuel used is C₁₂H₂₃. Calculate: (i) Stoichiometric A/F ratio, (ii) Equivalence ratio (φ), (iii) Lambda (λ), (iv) % excess air. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the unbalanced reaction for combustion of octane:

$$C_8H_{18} + a(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O + d\,N_2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Balance elements:
- Carbon: $b = 8$
- Hydrogen: $2c = 18 \implies c = 9$
- Oxygen: $2a = 2(8) + 9 = 25 \implies a = 12.5$
- Nitrogen: $d = 3.76 \times 12.5 = 47$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Balanced equation:

$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + 47\,N_2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Mass of 1 kmol of fuel:

$$m_{fuel} = 8(12) + 18(1) = 114 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Mass of air per kmol of fuel (M_air per kmol O₂ = 32 + 3.76 × 28 = 137.28 kg):

$$m_{air} = 12.5 \times 137.28 = 1716 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Stoichiometric A/F:

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{1716}{114} = 15.05 : 1$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Stoichiometric A/F for C₁₂H₂₃:

- $a = 12 + 23/4 = 12 + 5.75 = 17.75$
- $m_{fuel} = 12(12) + 23(1) = 144 + 23 = 167 \text{ kg}$
- $m_{air} = 17.75 \times 137.28 = 2436.72 \text{ kg}$
- $(A/F)_{stoich} = 2436.72/167 = 14.59 : 1$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Lambda (λ):

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} = \frac{18}{14.59} = 1.234$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Equivalence ratio (φ):

$$\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.234} = 0.810$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** % Excess air:

$$\%\,EA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100 = (1.234 - 1) \times 100 = 23.4\%$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Interpretation:
- Since λ > 1, the mixture is **lean** (excess air).
- A real-world CI engine typically operates lean (λ = 1.2 to 2.0) to ensure complete combustion of the injected fuel and to manage exhaust emissions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**
`[KTU University Exam - Module 2 Internal Choice, May 2024]`

**(a)** Define Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT). Discuss its significance in the selection of fuels for SI and CI engines, with typical values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of petrol has an octane number of 88. Explain how this number is determined. Compare RON, MON, and AKI for:  
- Regular petrol (RON = 91, MON = 81)  
- Premium petrol (RON = 95, MON = 85)  
Calculate the AKI for each and comment on which fuel is more suitable for a high-compression-ratio SI engine. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Definition of SIT:**  
Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT), or auto-ignition temperature, is the **minimum temperature** to which a fuel-air mixture must be raised for combustion to occur **spontaneously**, without any external ignition source like a spark or flame.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Typical Values:**

| Fuel | SIT (°C) |
|---|---|
| Methane | 540 |
| Iso-octane | 415 |
| Gasoline | 280 |
| Diesel | 210 |
| n-Cetane | 205 |

**Step 3 (3 marks):** **Significance:**

- **SI Engines:** The mixture is ignited by a spark plug. The fuel must have a **HIGH SIT** so that it does NOT self-ignite prematurely during the compression stroke. Premature ignition causes **knock**, which reduces efficiency and damages the engine. Hence, **Octane Number** is the key property (resistance to knock).

- **CI Engines:** There is no spark plug. Combustion must occur solely due to compression heating. The fuel must have a **LOW SIT** so that it self-ignites reliably when compressed to high pressure/temperature. Hence, **Cetane Number** is the key property (ignition quality).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:**  
> SI engine → **High SIT fuel** → **High Octane Number**  
> CI engine → **Low SIT fuel** → **High Cetane Number**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Octane Number Determination:**

The octane number of a fuel is determined using a **CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine** under standard test conditions. The fuel's knock tendency is compared to a blend of:
- **Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane):** ON = 100
- **n-Heptane:** ON = 0

A fuel with ON = 88 has the same knock intensity as a mixture of 88% iso-octane and 12% n-heptane. Two standards are used:
- **RON (Research Octane Number):** Mild conditions, 600 rpm, 52 °C intake.
- **MON (Motor Octane Number):** Severe conditions, 900 rpm, 149 °C intake, advanced ignition timing.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **AKI Calculations:**

For Regular Petrol:
$$AKI_{reg} = \frac{RON + MON}{2} = \frac{91 + 81}{2} = \frac{172}{2} = 86$$

For Premium Petrol:
$$AKI_{prem} = \frac{95 + 85}{2} = \frac{180}{2} = 90$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** **Sensitivity Comparison:**

- Regular: Sensitivity = $91 - 81 = 10$
- Premium: Sensitivity = $95 - 85 = 10$

Both have equal sensitivity, indicating similar behaviour across operating conditions.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** **Commentary for High-CR SI Engine:**

- A high-compression-ratio SI engine operates at higher cylinder pressures and temperatures, making it **more prone to knock**.
- The **Premium petrol (RON = 95, AKI = 90)** is **more suitable** because higher RON means greater resistance to knock under the high-compression, severe operating conditions.
- Regular petrol (RON = 91) may cause audible knock in such engines, leading to power loss, increased emissions, and possible piston damage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Atomic masses confusion:** Students often use H = 2 instead of H = 1 in fuel mass calculation. Always use **H = 1 kg/kmol** (atomic mass of hydrogen).
> 
> 2. **Air composition trap:** If the problem says "21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by **volume**," use 3.76 as the N₂/O₂ ratio. If it says "by **mass**," use a different ratio (typically 3.31). Read the question carefully.
> 
> 3. **A/F ≠ F/A confusion:** Equivalence ratio φ = (F/A)actual / (F/A)stoich, NOT (A/F)actual / (A/F)stoich. Easy to lose 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Octane vs Cetane engine mix-up:** A common mistake is recommending high-cetane fuel for a petrol engine or high-octane fuel for a diesel. Always match fuel property to engine type.
> 
> 5. **Unitless numbers:** Octane and Cetane numbers are **dimensionless**. Don't write "ON = 88 units" or "CN = 50 kg."
> 
> 6. **RON vs MON confusion:** RON measures **mild** conditions; MON measures **severe** conditions. RON is always ≥ MON for the same fuel.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Combustion Fundamentals
- Combustion is rapid oxidation of fuel-air mixture releasing heat.
- Requires correct **mixture preparation** and **ignition source**.

### 🎯 Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)
- **Stoichiometric A/F for petrol:** 14.7 : 1; for pure octane: 15.05 : 1
- **Lean mixture:** A/F > stoichiometric (excess air)
- **Rich mixture:** A/F < stoichiometric (excess fuel)
- **Lambda (λ) = 1:** Stoichiometric; **λ > 1:** Lean; **λ < 1:** Rich
- **Equivalence ratio φ = 1/λ** — indicates rich/lean directly.

### 🎯 Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT)
- Minimum temperature for spontaneous ignition.
- **Gasoline SIT (~280°C) > Diesel SIT (~210°C)**
- **High SIT** is required for SI engine fuels (to resist knock).
- **Low SIT** is required for CI engine fuels (to ignite under compression).

### 🎯 Octane Number (ON)
- Measure of **knock resistance** in SI engines.
- Reference: **Iso-octane (100)** and **n-Heptane (0)**.
- **RON** (mild test) ≥ **MON** (severe test).
- **AKI = (RON + MON)/2** is the posted octane rating.
- Higher ON = safer for high-compression SI engines.

### 🎯 Cetane Number (CN)
- Measure of **ignition quality** in CI engines.
- Reference: **n-Cetane (100)** and **α-Methylnaphthalene (0)**.
- **Higher CN = Shorter ignition delay = Smoother CI combustion**.
- Typical automotive diesel: **CN = 45–55**.

### 🎯 Quick-Recall Trade-offs
| If Engine is... | Fuel Property | Key Number |
|---|---|---|
| SI (Petrol) | High resistance to knock | High **Octane** (≥ 91) |
| CI (Diesel) | Easy compression ignition | High **Cetane** (≥ 45) |

### 🎯 Common Numerical Values to Memorise
- $(A/F)_{stoich}$ for octane = **15.05**
- $(A/F)_{stoich}$ for methane = **17.16**
- $(A/F)_{stoich}$ for cetane = **14.88**
- $(A/F)_{stoich}$ for gasoline = **14.7**
- N₂/O₂ molar ratio in air = **3.76**
- Molar mass of air = **28.97 kg/kmol**
- Molar mass of O₂ = **32 kg/kmol**
- Molar mass of N₂ = **28 kg/kmol**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuel, Air and Ignition Systems — The "Engine Breathing Trilogy"

An internal combustion engine is essentially an **air pump that burns fuel**. To run smoothly, it requires three perfectly synchronized sub-systems working in harmony:

1. **Fuel System** → Delivers the correct quantity of fuel at the right pressure and time.
2. **Air Intake (Induction) System** → Supplies the engine with the right mass of air.
3. **Ignition System** → Ignites the air-fuel mixture at the optimal moment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEMET613, Module 2):**
> The module covers the evolution from mechanical **carburettors** to electronic **fuel injection (MPFI, CRDI, GDI)**, the principles of **naturally aspirated** and **forced induction** (turbochargers and superchargers), and the **spark ignition system** with its components and **ignition timing** strategies.

---

## 1.2 The Carburettor (Simple Carburettor)

### 📌 Formal Definition
A **carburettor** is a mechanical device that mixes air with fuel in the correct proportion for combustion in a **Spark Ignition (SI) engine**. It uses the **Venturi effect** to draw fuel from a float-controlled bowl into the air stream using atmospheric pressure differential.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
> Imagine you are spraying perfume into a fast-moving wind. The faster the air rushes past the nozzle, the more perfume is sucked out. A carburettor does the same thing — a narrow throat (Venturi) speeds up incoming air, creating a low-pressure zone that "sucks" fuel out of a small reservoir.

### Key Components
- **Float Chamber** — Maintains constant fuel level.
- **Float + Needle Valve** — Acts as a mechanical level sensor and shut-off.
- **Venturi** — A narrow throat that accelerates airflow (creating low pressure).
- **Main Jet** — Calibrated orifice controlling fuel flow rate.
- **Main Discharge Nozzle** — Sprays fuel into the air stream.
- **Throttle Valve (Butterfly Valve)** — Controls engine power output.
- **Choke** — Enriches mixture during cold start.

> [!NOTE]
> The theoretical **air-fuel ratio (AFR)** for complete combustion of gasoline is **14.7 : 1** (also called the **stoichiometric ratio**). Carburettors are tuned to deliver this under ideal cruising conditions.

---

## 1.3 Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)

### 📌 Formal Definition
**MPFI** is an electronic fuel injection system in which **each cylinder has its own injector** positioned near the **intake port** (just before the intake valve). Fuel is sprayed onto the back of the intake valve, ensuring precise metering under all operating conditions.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
> Instead of one common kitchen tap dispensing water to all sinks (like a carburettor), MPFI installs **one dedicated faucet at every sink**. A central computer (ECU) tells each faucet exactly how much water to release, when, and for how long — far more efficient and fair.

### Key Components
- **Fuel Tank**, **Electric Fuel Pump** (in-tank), **Fuel Filter**.
- **Fuel Rail** — A common pressurized tube feeding all injectors.
- **Pressure Regulator** — Maintains constant pressure differential across the injectors (~**2.5 – 3.5 bar**).
- **Fuel Injectors** (one per cylinder).
- **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** + sensors (MAP, MAF, TPS, O₂, Coolant, Crank).

---

## 1.4 Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)

### 📌 Formal Definition
**CRDI** is a high-pressure direct injection system used predominantly in **diesel engines**, where a **common accumulator (rail)** stores fuel at very high pressure (**up to 2000 bar / 200 MPa**) and electronically controlled injectors deliver it directly into the combustion chamber.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
> Picture a city overhead water tank connected to every home. The tank is always full and pressurized. When any tap is opened, water gushes out instantly at the same high pressure. The **common rail** is the overhead tank; **injectors** are the taps; and the **ECU** decides which tap opens, when, and for how long.

### Key Sub-Components
- **Supply Pump (Feed Pump)** — Low-pressure transfer from tank.
- **High-Pressure Pump** — Pressurizes fuel (driven by camshaft).
- **Common Rail** — Accumulator with pressure sensor.
- **Solenoid or Piezo Injectors** — Actuated by ECU pulses.
- **ECU + Pressure Control Valve (PCV) + Pressure Limiter Valve**.

---

## 1.5 Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)

### 📌 Formal Definition
**GDI** is a direct injection system for **spark-ignition engines** in which fuel is injected **directly into the combustion chamber** at high pressure (**~100 – 350 bar**), enabling **stratified charge**, **homogeneous charge**, or **mixed-mode** operation for improved efficiency and power.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
> In MPFI, fuel is sprayed onto the intake valve, like misting water onto a hot pan *before* placing it on the stove. In GDI, fuel is sprayed *directly into the burning chamber* — like pouring water directly onto a sizzling pan. The reaction is faster, more controlled, and more efficient.

### Operational Modes
- **Homogeneous Mode (High Load):** Uniform mixture, stoichiometric AFR.
- **Stratified Mode (Light Load):** Rich mixture around spark plug, lean elsewhere → **ultra-lean overall** (AFR up to **40:1**).
- **Mixed Mode (Transitional):** Combination of both.

---

## 1.6 Solenoid vs. Piezo Injectors

### Solenoid Injector
- A **magnetic solenoid** lifts a needle valve when energized.
- Response time: **~0.2 – 0.5 ms**.
- Used in conventional MPFI and older CRDI systems.

### Piezo Injector
- Uses a **piezoelectric stack** (lead zirconate titanate) that expands by a few **micrometres** when voltage is applied.
- Response time: **~0.05 – 0.1 ms** (much faster).
- Enables **multiple injections per cycle** (pilot, main, post).
- Used in modern CRDI systems (Bosch, Denso, Continental).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Piezo injectors can perform **up to 7–9 injections per combustion cycle**, drastically reducing **noise, vibration, harshness (NVH)** and emissions.

---

## 1.7 Naturally Aspirated vs. Forced Induction

### Naturally Aspirated (NA) Engine
- Air enters the cylinder only at **atmospheric pressure** (≈ **1.013 bar**).
- Power is limited by volumetric efficiency (typically **80 – 90 %**).
- No parasitic loss, linear throttle response.

### Forced Induction
A **supercharging device** compresses intake air, forcing **more mass of air** into the cylinder. This allows more fuel to be burned, producing **more power from the same displacement**.

| Type | Energy Source | Lag | Efficiency |
|------|---------------|-----|------------|
| **Turbocharger** | Exhaust gas (turbine) | Yes (turbo lag) | High (uses waste energy) |
| **Supercharger** | Belt from crankshaft | No (instant) | Lower (parasitic loss) |

### Turbocharger
- **Exhaust gas → Turbine → Compressor** on a common shaft.
- Operates at speeds up to **~200,000 rpm**.
- Produces **boost pressure** (typically **0.5 – 2.5 bar** above atmospheric).
- Components: Turbine, Compressor, Bearing housing, Wastegate, Intercooler.

### Supercharger
- Mechanically driven (Roots, Twin-screw, Centrifugal).
- Centrifugal type is most common in modern cars.
- Operates at speeds up to **~50,000 rpm**.
- Immediate response, but saps engine power.

---

## 1.8 Spark Ignition System

### 📌 Formal Definition
A **spark ignition (SI) system** generates a high-voltage spark (**15,000 – 40,000 V**) at the correct instant to ignite the compressed air-fuel mixture in the cylinder.

### Main Components
- **Battery** (12 V) → **Ignition Coil** (steps up to high voltage) → **Distributor** (in older systems) → **Spark Plug** (creates arc).
- Modern systems are **distributorless (DIS / COP)** and controlled by the **ECU**.

### Ignition Timing
The **ignition timing** is the crank angle (BTDC — Before Top Dead Centre) at which the spark fires. It is **advanced** at higher RPM and under low loads to give the flame time to fully develop, maximizing power and efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical values:** Idle: **5° – 15° BTDC**; Cruising: **20° – 35° BTDC**; High RPM: **30° – 45° BTDC**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Carburettor — Operating Principles in Detail

The carburettor functions on three fundamental physics principles:

1. **Bernoulli's Principle:** In a Venturi, velocity increases → pressure decreases.
2. **Atmospheric Pressure Differential:** Atmospheric pressure pushes fuel up the discharge nozzle.
3. **Venturi Suction:** The low-pressure zone at the throat draws fuel into the air stream.

### Step-by-Step Operating Sequence
- **Step 1:** Piston moves down → air rushes in through the venturi.
- **Step 2:** Air accelerates at the throat → static pressure drops (Venturi effect).
- **Step 3:** The pressure differential (atmospheric at float chamber − low pressure at throat) pushes fuel up the main discharge nozzle.
- **Step 4:** Fuel atomizes into the air stream → combustible mixture formed.
- **Step 5:** Throttle valve (butterfly) opens/closes to control mixture quantity → RPM control.

### Supplementary Systems
- **Idle System:** Operates at closed throttle via idle port and idle adjustment screw.
- **Main Metering System:** Engages at mid-to-high throttle via main jet.
- **Power Valve (Economiser):** Enriches mixture at WOT (Wide Open Throttle).
- **Choke:** Restricts air intake → enriches mixture for cold starting.

---

## 2.2 MPFI — Detailed Functional Analysis

### Working Principle
1. The **electric fuel pump** pressurizes fuel to **~3.5 bar**.
2. Fuel reaches the **fuel rail** and is available at every injector at equal pressure.
3. The **ECU** computes injection timing and duration from sensor inputs (MAF, MAP, TPS, O₂, crank angle).
4. Injectors are pulsed **sequentially** (one at a time, synchronized with each cylinder's intake stroke) or in **batch-fire** mode (older systems).

### ECU Sensor Inputs
- **MAF (Mass Air Flow)** — Measures incoming air mass.
- **MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure)** — Indirectly infers air mass.
- **TPS (Throttle Position Sensor)** — Driver demand.
- **O₂ Sensor (Lambda)** — Closed-loop feedback.
- **CKP / CMP** — Crank and cam position (for precise timing).
- **Coolant & Air Temperature** — Mixture compensation.

---

## 2.3 CRDI — Detailed Functional Analysis

### High-Pressure Generation
- The **high-pressure pump** (radial piston type, driven by camshaft) generates pressure up to **2000 bar** in stages.
- The **common rail** is a thick-walled accumulator that maintains near-constant pressure.

### Injection Phases (Modern Piezo CRDI)
- **Pilot Injection(s):** Small amounts before main injection → reduce combustion noise.
- **Main Injection:** Primary combustion event.
- **After Injection:** Burns soot, regenerates DPF.
- **Post Injection:** For EGR, emission control.

---

## 2.4 GDI — Detailed Functional Analysis

### Stratified Charge Operation
- Injection occurs late in compression stroke.
- Fuel forms a **rich cloud around the spark plug**.
- Surrounding air remains lean.
- Effective overall AFR can be **25:1 to 40:1**.
- Improves fuel economy by up to **15 %** in light-load cruising.

### Homogeneous Charge Operation
- Injection occurs during intake stroke.
- Uniform mixture throughout the chamber.
- Used at high load for maximum power (stoichiometric AFR = 14.7:1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> GDI engines require **high-pressure fuel pumps**, **multi-hole injectors** (up to **10 holes**), and are prone to **carbon deposits** on intake valves (no fuel wash to clean them).

---

## 2.5 Forced Induction — Deep Analysis

### Turbocharger Working
- **Exhaust gas** drives the turbine wheel.
- The **turbine** and **compressor** share a common shaft supported by fluid bearings or ball bearings.
- **Compressor** draws in and pressurizes fresh air → **boost**.
- **Wastegate** (or variable geometry turbo) controls maximum boost.
- **Intercooler** (air-to-air or air-to-water) cools compressed air → increases density (ΔT reduction).

### Supercharger Working (Centrifugal Type)
- Driven via **belt** from the crankshaft.
- Centrifugal compressor impeller accelerates air radially outward.
- Pressure rise proportional to **impeller speed²**.

### Boost Pressure Equation
Boost is often expressed as:
$$P_{boost} = P_{manifold} - P_{atmospheric}$$

> The **boost ratio** is defined as the pressure ratio across the compressor:
> $$\Pi = \frac{P_{manifold}}{P_{atmospheric}}$$
> For a naturally aspirated engine, $\Pi = 1.0$. With **0.5 bar boost**, $P_{manifold} = 1.5 \text{ bar}$, so $\Pi = 1.5$.

---

## 2.6 Spark Ignition System — Detailed Operation

### Coil-Induction Process
The ignition coil is essentially a **step-up transformer**:
- **Primary winding:** ~100 – 200 turns of thick wire (low resistance).
- **Secondary winding:** ~15,000 – 30,000 turns of fine wire.
- **Turns ratio (n)** typically around **1:100**.

When the **primary circuit is broken** (contact breaker opens or transistor switches off), the collapsing magnetic field induces a high voltage in the secondary, following Faraday's Law:
$$V_s = -N_s \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

### Distributorless Ignition System (DIS) / Coil-on-Plug (COP)
- One coil per cylinder (or one coil per pair of cylinders).
- ECU directly fires each coil at the correct moment.
- Eliminates distributor, rotor, and cap → higher reliability.

---

## 2.7 Ignition Timing — Deep Theory

### Why Timing Matters
- Flame propagation speed ≈ **15 – 25 m/s** in SI engines.
- If spark fires at TDC, peak pressure occurs *after* TDC → power is lost.
- If spark fires too early → knock (detonation), engine damage.
- **Optimum timing:** peak cylinder pressure occurs at **~15° – 20° ATDC**.

### Advance Mechanisms (Conventional System)
- **Initial Timing:** Set statically during manufacture (typically **5° – 10° BTDC**).
- **Centrifugal Advance:** Increases with RPM (flyweights move outward).
- **Vacuum Advance:** Increases as manifold vacuum rises (light load).

### Modern Electronic Advance
$$\theta_{total} = \theta_{initial} + \theta_{centrifugal\,(electronic)} + \theta_{vacuum\,(MAP-based)} - \theta_{knock\,correction}$$

ECU computes timing from a **3D map** of RPM × Load and adjusts using **knock sensor feedback**.

---

## 📊 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) | $AFR = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Gasoline stoichiometric = 14.7 |
| 2 | Relative AFR (λ) | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | λ < 1 = rich; λ > 1 = lean |
| 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{\rho_{air} \cdot V_d}$ | NA: 80 – 90 %; Forced: >100 % |
| 4 | Air Mass per Cycle (Ideal Gas) | $m_{air} = \dfrac{P_{man} \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_v}{R_{air} \cdot T_{man}}$ | $R_{air}$ = 287 J/(kg·K) |
| 5 | Power Increase with Boost | $P_{boost} \approx P_{NA} \cdot \dfrac{P_{man}}{P_{atm}}$ | Approximately linear |
| 6 | Boost Ratio | $\Pi = \dfrac{P_{man}}{P_{atm}}$ | NA: Π = 1.0 |
| 7 | Compressor Work (Ideal) | $W_c = c_p \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | γ = 1.4 for air |
| 8 | Turns Ratio of Ignition Coil | $n = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | Typically ~100 |
| 9 | Secondary Voltage (Faraday) | $V_s = -N_s \cdot \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | dΦ/dt from collapsing field |
| 10 | Total Ignition Advance | $\theta_{total} = \theta_{initial} + \theta_{centrifugal} + \theta_{vacuum} - \theta_{knock}$ | All in crank degrees |
| 11 | Injector Flow Rate (Volumetric) | $\dot{V}_f = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$ | $C_d$ ≈ 0.7 – 0.9 |
| 12 | Stoichiometric AFR (Gasoline) | $C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O$ | Yields 14.7 |
| 13 | Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel, $C_{12}H_{23}$) | $C_{12}H_{23} + 17.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 12\,CO_2 + 11.5\,H_2O$ | Yields ≈ 14.5 |
| 14 | BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{brake}}$ | Units: g/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note for KTU Exam Tables:** All absolute values, magnitudes, and conditional expressions in formulas use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never the raw pipe symbol $\vert$ in tables.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Carburettors** → Still used in low-cost two-wheelers, lawn mowers, and small engines (cost-effective, no ECU needed).
- **MPFI** → Standard in mid-range Indian cars (Maruti, Hyundai, Honda) for balance of cost, performance, and emissions compliance with **BS-VI norms**.
- **CRDI** → Standard in modern diesel cars (Tata, Mahindra, BMW) for high torque and fuel economy.
- **GDI** → Used in premium performance cars (VW TSI, Ford EcoBoost, Toyota D-4S) for stratified-charge efficiency.
- **Turbochargers** → Mandatory in modern downsized engines (1.0 L 3-cylinder producing 120+ HP).
- **Spark Ignition** → Still the dominant technology in petrol cars; ignites **~50 – 80 times per second per cylinder** at highway speeds.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass of Air Inducted per Cycle

We derive the **mass of air drawn into one cylinder per cycle** — the fundamental equation used in fuel injection quantity calculation.

### Step 1: Start with the Ideal Gas Law

For the air charge inside the cylinder (assumed ideal):
$$P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$$

Solving for the mass $m$:
$$m = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T}$$

**Textual conversion:** $P$ is the absolute pressure of the inducted air, $V$ is the effective volume occupied, $R$ is the specific gas constant of air, and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

### Step 2: Apply to Engine Induction

The actual mass inducted depends on **volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ (which accounts for pressure drop, heat transfer, residual gases, etc.):
$$m_{air} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{P_{man} \cdot V_d}{R_{air} \cdot T_{man}}$$

**Textual conversion:** $P_{man}$ is the **manifold absolute pressure** (gauge + atmospheric), $V_d$ is the **displacement volume** of one cylinder, $R_{air}$ is the gas constant for air (**287 J/(kg·K)**), and $T_{man}$ is the **manifold absolute temperature** in Kelvin.

### Step 3: Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** A 4-cylinder 1.2 L engine at idle: $V_d = \frac{1200 \text{ cm}^3}{4} = 300 \text{ cm}^3 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$, $P_{man} = 0.4 \text{ bar} = 40{,}000 \text{ Pa}$, $T_{man} = 320 \text{ K}$, $\eta_v = 0.55$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air} &= 0.55 \cdot \frac{(40{,}000 \text{ Pa}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3)}{(287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}) \cdot (320 \text{ K})} \\[6pt]
&= 0.55 \cdot \frac{12}{91{,}840} \\[6pt]
&= 0.55 \cdot 1.307 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} \\[6pt]
&= 7.19 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg per cylinder per cycle} \\[6pt]
&\approx 7.19 \times 10^{-2} \text{ g per cylinder per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual conversion:** At idle, the engine induces only about **0.072 grams of air per cylinder per cycle** — a remarkably small mass that nonetheless supports combustion when mixed with ~5 mg of fuel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fuel Mass per Injection Pulse

The ECU must command the injector to deliver a specific fuel mass $m_f$ for a desired AFR.

### Step 1: Stoichiometric Requirement

For complete combustion of gasoline at $\lambda = 1$:
$$m_f = \frac{m_{air}}{14.7}$$

### Step 2: Real Engine with Lambda Adjustment

For a target $\lambda$ (e.g., $\lambda = 0.85$ for WOT enrichment):
$$m_f = \frac{m_{air}}{14.7 \cdot \lambda}$$

### Step 3: Numerically

Using the previous $m_{air} = 7.19 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}$ at $\lambda = 1$:
$$m_f = \frac{7.19 \times 10^{-5}}{14.7} = 4.89 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg} = 4.89 \text{ mg}$$

At WOT ($\lambda = 0.85$):
$$m_f = \frac{7.19 \times 10^{-5}}{14.7 \cdot 0.85} = 5.76 \text{ mg}$$

**Textual conversion:** The ECU must command the injector to deliver approximately **4.9 mg of fuel at idle** and **5.8 mg at full load** — a 20 % enrichment. The injector pulse width (typically **2 – 12 ms**) is precisely calibrated to deliver this.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Theoretical Power Gain from Forced Induction

### Step 1: Power Proportional to Air Mass

For an engine burning stoichiometric mixture, **brake power is proportional to air mass flow** (since fuel mass follows from AFR):
$$P_{brake} \propto m_{air}$$

### Step 2: Air Mass Proportional to Manifold Pressure

From the ideal gas law (Step 1, Section 3.1), at constant temperature and displacement:
$$m_{air} \propto P_{man}$$

### Step 3: Power Ratio

Therefore:
$$\frac{P_{boost}}{P_{NA}} = \frac{P_{man,boost}}{P_{man,NA}} = \frac{P_{man,boost}}{P_{atm}}$$

(Assuming NA engine reaches atmospheric manifold pressure at WOT.)

### Step 4: Numerical Example

NA engine power: $P_{NA} = 100 \text{ kW}$. With turbo delivering manifold pressure of **1.6 bar (absolute)**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{boost} &= 100 \text{ kW} \cdot \frac{1.6 \text{ bar}}{1.013 \text{ bar}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 158 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual conversion:** A modest **0.6 bar of boost** (~9 psi) yields a theoretical **58 % power increase** — explaining why turbocharging has become standard in modern downsized engines.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Ignition Coil Secondary Voltage

The ignition coil behaves as a transformer. We derive the maximum theoretical secondary voltage.

### Step 1: Voltage Transformation

$$\frac{V_s}{V_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} = n$$

Solving for $V_s$:
$$V_s = n \cdot V_p$$

### Step 2: With Mutual Inductance

When the primary current $I_p$ is interrupted in time $\Delta t$, the induced secondary voltage (Faraday's Law) is:
$$V_s = \frac{M \cdot \frac{dI_p}{dt}}{1}$$

A more practical engineer's formula using stored primary energy:
$$V_s = I_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_p}{C_s}}$$

where $C_s$ is the secondary (and load) capacitance.

### Step 3: Real-World Values

A typical coil: $L_p = 5 \text{ mH}$, primary current at breaking = **5 A**, secondary capacitance ~ **80 pF**.
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= 5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{80 \times 10^{-12}}} \\[6pt]
&= 5 \cdot \sqrt{6.25 \times 10^{7}} \\[6pt]
&= 5 \cdot 7906 \\[6pt]
&\approx 39{,}530 \text{ V} = 39.5 \text{ kV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual conversion:** A typical ignition coil generates a peak of about **40 kV** — well above the **15 – 25 kV** needed to jump the spark plug gap under cylinder pressure. The excess margin ensures reliable firing even with fouled or worn plugs.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Carburettor vs MPFI vs CRDI vs GDI

| Parameter | Carburettor | MPFI | CRDI | GDI |
|-----------|-------------|------|------|-----|
| **Injection Location** | Air intake (Venturi) | Intake port | Combustion chamber (diesel) | Combustion chamber (gasoline) |
| **Pressure** | Atmospheric | 2.5 – 3.5 bar | 200 – 2000 bar | 100 – 350 bar |
| **Metering Accuracy** | Poor (mechanical) | High (ECU) | Very High | Very High |
| **Cold Start** | Choke required | ECU enrichment | Glow plugs | ECU enrichment |
| **AFR Flexibility** | Fixed mapping | Wide lambda | Lambda + smoke limit | Stratified possible (AFR > 25) |
| **Fuel Economy** | Baseline | +10 – 15 % | +20 – 30 % | +15 – 20 % |
| **Power Output** | Baseline | +5 – 10 % | +30 – 50 % | +10 – 20 % |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | High | High |
| **Response** | Slow (mechanical lag) | Fast | Fast (some lag) | Fastest |
| **Modern Use** | 2-wheelers, small engines | Most petrol cars | All modern diesels | Premium/performance cars |

### Component Description (Matching the Diagram)
- **VENTURI THROAT:** Constricted section that accelerates incoming air.
- **MAIN DISCHARGE NOZZLE:** Final fuel outlet into the air stream.
- **FLOAT CHAMBER:** Reservoir with constant fuel level.
- **NEEDLE VALVE:** Mechanically opens/closes with float position.
- **CHOKE VALVE:** Pulls extra fuel-rich mixture for cold starting.
- **THROTTLE VALVE (BUTTERFLY):** Driver-controlled power gate.

### Operating Logic
- **Exhaust gas** spins the **turbine** → drives the **compressor** on the same shaft.
- **Compressor** pressurizes **fresh air** → cooled by the **intercooler** → enters the **intake manifold** at boosted pressure.
- **Wastegate** bleeds excess exhaust to limit boost pressure.
- **ECU** modulates the wastegate (or variable geometry vanes) to achieve target boost.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**List the main components of a simple carburettor and explain the function of the float chamber and needle valve.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)
A simple carburettor consists of the following main components: **float chamber, float and needle valve, venturi, main jet, main discharge nozzle, throttle valve, and choke**.

- **Float Chamber:** A fuel reservoir that maintains a **constant fuel level slightly below the venturi throat**. This ensures a steady, predictable fuel head for the main discharge nozzle.
- **Needle Valve:** A precision-tapered valve controlled by the float. As fuel level rises, the float pushes the needle into its seat, shutting off inflow. As fuel is consumed, the float drops, opening the needle. This creates a **self-regulating mechanical feedback loop** that holds the fuel level within ±1 mm.

> The float + needle assembly is essentially a **mechanical level sensor and regulator**, equivalent in function to a modern ECU-controlled fuel pressure regulator.

**Distribution:** (1) List of components, (1) Float chamber function, (1) Needle valve function.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between naturally aspirated and forced induction engines with examples.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Feature | Naturally Aspirated (NA) | Forced Induction |
|---------|-------------------------|------------------|
| **Intake Air Source** | Atmospheric pressure only | Pressurized by compressor/turbo |
| **Manifold Pressure** | ≤ 1.0 bar absolute | 1.2 – 3.0 bar absolute |
| **Volumetric Efficiency** | 80 – 90 % | 100 – 150 % (or more) |
| **Power Density** | Baseline | 30 – 80 % higher from same displacement |
| **Examples** | Maruti Alto K10 1.0 L (68 PS) | Hyundai Venue 1.0 L Turbo (120 PS) |

**Key Examples (1 mark each side):**
- **NA:** Maruti Swift 1.2 L petrol (83 PS) — uses only atmospheric pressure.
- **Forced Induction:** Maruti Grand Vitara 1.5 L Turbo Petrol (103 PS) — uses a turbocharger.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **turbo lag** (delay in turbocharger spool-up) with **supercharger lag** (which is minimal). Do not write "both have lag."

### **🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat line diagram, explain the **working of a simple carburettor**. Discuss the role of the **venturi** and **float chamber** in mixture formation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **components and working of a Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) system** with a line diagram. Compare it with a carburettor-based system. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question A(a): Simple Carburettor (7 Marks)

**Line Diagram Description (1.5 Marks):**
The diagram should show: Air filter → Choke → Venturi throat with main discharge nozzle projecting into it → Throttle valve downstream → Float chamber with float and needle valve connected to fuel line from tank. Label all parts clearly.

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**

1. **Piston Intake Stroke:** As the piston moves down, low pressure is created in the intake manifold.
2. **Air Acceleration:** Atmospheric air rushes in, passes through the **choke** (open during normal operation) and accelerates through the **venturi throat**.
3. **Venturi Effect (Bernoulli's Principle):** Air velocity at the throat increases, causing static pressure to drop below atmospheric pressure.
4. **Fuel Discharge:** The float chamber is at atmospheric pressure. The pressure difference ($\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{throat}$) pushes fuel up through the **main jet** and out of the **discharge nozzle**.
5. **Atomization & Mixing:** Fuel emerges as a fine spray, atomizes in the high-velocity air stream, and forms a combustible mixture.
6. **Throttle Control:** The butterfly throttle valve regulates the total air-fuel mixture flow, controlling engine speed and load.

**Role of Venturi (1.5 Marks):**
The venturi is a **carefully profiled constriction** that converts pressure energy of incoming air into kinetic energy (velocity). The throat's cross-sectional area determines the magnitude of the pressure drop, which directly controls the fuel flow rate. A **smaller throat = greater velocity = greater pressure drop = more fuel drawn**.

**Role of Float Chamber (1 Mark):**
Maintains a **constant fuel head** at the main jet. Without it, fuel delivery would vary erratically with vehicle tilt, acceleration, or fuel slosh, causing an unstable mixture.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question A(b): MPFI System (7 Marks)

**Line Diagram Description (1.5 Marks):**
The diagram should depict: Fuel tank → Electric fuel pump → Fuel filter → Fuel rail (with pressure regulator and return line) → Four injectors (one per cylinder, near intake valves) → ECU receiving signals from MAF, MAP, TPS, O₂, and CKP sensors.

**Components and Their Functions (2.5 Marks):**
- **Electric Fuel Pump:** Delivers pressurized fuel (~3.5 bar) from the tank.
- **Fuel Filter:** Removes particulates; protects injectors from clogging.
- **Fuel Rail:** Distributes fuel at uniform pressure to all injectors.
- **Pressure Regulator:** Maintains constant ΔP across injectors; returns excess fuel to the tank.
- **Injectors:** Solenoid-operated valves that spray atomized fuel near the intake valve, controlled by ECU pulse width.
- **ECU:** Brain of the system — computes injection duration and timing from multiple sensor inputs.

**Working (2 Marks):**
The ECU reads **MAF/MAP** for air mass, **TPS** for driver demand, **O₂ sensor** for closed-loop AFR correction, and **CKP** for cylinder identification. For each intake stroke, the ECU commands an injector pulse whose **width (typically 2 – 12 ms)** is precisely proportional to the required fuel mass. Injection is **sequential** (one injector at a time, in firing order) for maximum accuracy.

**Comparison with Carburettor (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | Carburettor | MPFI |
|--------|-------------|------|
| Metering | Mechanical, AFR fixed | Electronic, AFR adaptive |
| Fuel Distribution | Single point | One injector per cylinder |
| Cold Start | Manual choke | Automatic ECU enrichment |
| Emission Compliance | Difficult (Euro 1/2) | Easy (BS-VI / Euro 6) |
| Fuel Economy | Baseline | +10 – 15 % |
| Cost | Low | Higher (ECU + sensors) |

---

### **🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI) system** with a neat block diagram. Describe the working of **solenoid and piezo injectors**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a **turbocharger** and a **supercharger** with diagrams. Compare their performance characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question B(a): CRDI System (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram Description (1.5 Marks):**
The diagram should show: Fuel tank → Supply pump → Fuel filter → High-pressure pump (radial piston) → Common rail with rail pressure sensor and pressure control valve → 4 piezo injectors → Combustion chambers. ECU receives inputs from rail pressure sensor, crank position sensor, coolant sensor, and accelerator pedal sensor; outputs PWM signals to injectors and PCV.

**CRDI System Working (3 Marks):**
1. The **supply pump** (low pressure, ~5 bar) transfers fuel from the tank through the filter to the **high-pressure pump**.
2. The **high-pressure pump** (3 radial pistons, driven by camshaft at half engine speed) compresses fuel to **up to 2000 bar** in three pulses per camshaft revolution.
3. The **common rail** (a thick-walled steel tube of volume ~20 – 30 cm³) acts as a pressure accumulator. The **rail pressure sensor** continuously monitors pressure and feeds back to the ECU.
4. The **pressure control valve (PCV)** on the rail bleeds excess fuel to maintain target pressure.
5. On each command from the ECU, an **injector** opens for a few hundred microseconds, delivering a precisely metered quantity of fuel directly into the combustion chamber at extreme pressure, producing fine atomization.

**Solenoid Injector (1.25 Marks):**
A **solenoid** (electromagnetic coil) is wound around a magnetic core attached to a **needle valve**. When the ECU energizes the coil with a 12 V pulse, the magnetic field lifts the needle against spring force, opening a precision orifice. Fuel sprays through multiple micro-holes. Response time ~0.2 – 0.5 ms. Used in conventional MPFI and entry-level CRDI.

**Piezo Injector (1.25 Marks):**
A stack of **piezoelectric crystals** (lead zirconate titanate, ~25 mm long) is mechanically coupled to a **needle valve** via a hydraulic coupler. When the ECU applies ~200 V across the stack, it **elongates by ~30 – 50 μm** within microseconds. The hydraulic coupler amplifies this stroke to ~100 μm, lifting the needle. Response time **~0.05 – 0.1 ms** — about 4× faster than solenoid. Enables **multiple injections per cycle** (pilot + main + after), drastically reducing diesel knock and emissions.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question B(b): Turbocharger and Supercharger (7 Marks)

**Turbocharger — Construction & Working (3 Marks):**
A turbocharger consists of a **turbine wheel** and a **compressor wheel** mounted on a **common shaft** supported by fluid bearings (or ball bearings in modern units) inside a bearing housing.
- **Exhaust gas** from the engine's exhaust manifold flows into the turbine housing, striking the turbine blades and causing it to spin at speeds up to **~200,000 rpm**.
- The same shaft drives the **compressor wheel**, which draws in fresh air and compresses it.
- The pressurized air passes through an **intercooler** (charge air cooler) to reduce its temperature, increasing density.
- Cooled, dense air enters the intake manifold at **boost pressure** (typically 0.5 – 2.0 bar gauge).
- A **wastegate** (a bypass valve controlled by the ECU) limits maximum boost by diverting exhaust away from the turbine.

**Supercharger (Centrifugal) — Construction & Working (2 Marks):**
A centrifugal supercharger is **belt-driven** from the engine's crankshaft (typically via a serpentine or dedicated drive belt). It contains a high-speed **impeller** inside a compressor housing with a volute-shaped diffuser.
- As the impeller spins at up to ~50,000 rpm, air is drawn in axially and flung outward centrifugally.
- The diffuser converts kinetic energy into pressure energy.
- Boost pressure is proportional to (impeller speed)².
- A **bypass valve** prevents compressor surge at low engine speeds.

**Comparison (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Turbocharger | Supercharger |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| **Energy Source** | Waste exhaust gas | Mechanical (crankshaft) |
| **Response Time** | Slower (turbo lag ~1 – 2 s) | Instantaneous |
| **Parasitic Loss** | None (uses waste energy) | Yes (5 – 15 % of engine power) |
| **Boost at Idle** | Minimal | Available |
| **Thermal Load** | High (needs intercooler) | Lower |
| **Service Life** | Long (no belt) | Belt replacement needed |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high | High |
| **Applications** | Most modern petrol/diesel (Tata Nexon, Hyundai Creta) | Performance cars (Mercedes Kompressor, older Jaguar) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON MISTAKE**
> 1. **In CRDI questions:** Do NOT confuse the **supply pump** (low pressure) with the **high-pressure pump**. Mark specifically for the **three-radial-piston high-pressure pump** and the **role of the common rail as a pressure accumulator**.
> 2. **In injector questions:** Clearly distinguish **actuation mechanism** (solenoid = magnetic; piezo = crystal deformation). Marks are lost for stating "both use electromagnets."
> 3. **In turbocharger questions:** Many students omit the **wastegate** and the **intercooler**. These are **mandatory** elements for full marks.
> 4. **In ignition timing questions:** Always state the timing in **degrees BTDC** (Before Top Dead Centre), not just "advance angle." Numerical value is expected (e.g., 30° BTDC at 3000 rpm).
> 5. **In MPFI questions:** Do not write "fuel is injected into the cylinder." It is injected at the **intake port**, before the intake valve. This is the key distinction from GDI/CRDI.

---

## 🧠 5.1 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### ✅ Carburettor Fundamentals
- Carburettor = mechanical fuel-air mixer using **Venturi effect**.
- Key parts: **Float chamber, Venturi, Main jet, Throttle, Choke**.
- AFR determined by **venturi vacuum** vs. **atmospheric pressure** in float chamber.
- Pros: cheap, simple. Cons: poor accuracy, hard to meet emission norms.
- Still used in **2-wheelers and small engines**.

### ✅ MPFI (Multi-Point Fuel Injection)
- **One injector per cylinder**, mounted at the **intake port**.
- Pressure: **2.5 – 3.5 bar**.
- **ECU-controlled** with sensor feedback (MAF, MAP, O₂, TPS, CKP).
- Sequence: Pump → Filter → Rail → Injector → Back to tank (via pressure regulator).
- Standard in most **BS-VI petrol cars**.

### ✅ CRDI (Common Rail Direct Injection)
- **Common rail accumulator** at very high pressure (up to **2000 bar**).
- **Direct injection** into combustion chamber (diesel).
- **Piezo injectors** enable multiple injections (pilot, main, after, post).
- Reduces **NVH and emissions** dramatically.

### ✅ GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection)
- Direct injection for **gasoline** engines at **100 – 350 bar**.
- Two modes: **Homogeneous** (high load) and **Stratified** (light load, AFR up to 40:1).
- Multi-hole injectors (up to 10 holes) for fine spray.
- Vulnerable to **intake valve carbon deposits**.

### ✅ Injector Working
- **Solenoid:** Magnetic field lifts needle (~0.2 – 0.5 ms response).
- **Piezo:** Piezoelectric stack expansion lifts needle (~0.05 – 0.1 ms response, faster, multi-injection capable).

### ✅ Forced Induction
- **NA engine:** Atmospheric pressure only, $\eta_v$ ≤ 90 %.
- **Turbocharger:** Driven by exhaust gas; no parasitic loss; has turbo lag; uses **wastegate** and **intercooler**.
- **Supercharger:** Belt-driven from crank; instant response; parasitic loss; bypass valve to prevent surge.
- Power gain ≈ $P_{boost} \approx P_{NA} \times (P_{man}/P_{atm})$.

### ✅ Spark Ignition System
- Components: **Battery → Ignition switch → Coil → Distributor (older) / ECU trigger (newer) → Spark plug**.
- Coil acts as step-up transformer; secondary voltage **15 – 40 kV**.
- Modern systems: **Coil-on-Plug (COP)**, **Distributorless Ignition System (DIS)**.
- ECU computes firing from **CKP** and **CMP** sensors.

### ✅ Ignition Timing
- Expressed in **degrees BTDC**.
- **Advance increases with RPM** (flame needs more time) but **decreases with load** (less time available).
- Three components: **Initial + Centrifugal + Vacuum** (mechanical) or **ECU 3D map** (electronic).
- **Knock sensor** provides feedback to retard timing if detonation detected.
- Typical values: idle 5° – 15° BTDC; WOT 30° – 45° BTDC.

### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize
| Constant | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) | **14.7 : 1** |
| Stoichiometric AFR (diesel) | **≈ 14.5 : 1** |
| MPFI rail pressure | **2.5 – 3.5 bar** |
| GDI rail pressure | **100 – 350 bar** |
| CRDI rail pressure | **up to 2000 bar** |
| Turbocharger shaft speed | **up to 200,000 rpm** |
| Supercharger speed | **up to 50,000 rpm** |
| Secondary coil voltage | **15 – 40 kV** |
| Typical idle advance | **5° – 15° BTDC** |
| Typical WOT advance | **30° – 45° BTDC** |
| Atmospheric pressure | **1.013 bar** |
| Volumetric efficiency (NA) | **80 – 90 %** |
| $R_{air}$ | **287 J/(kg·K)** |

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — OEMET613 (Automotive Technology)**
> *Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework. Aligned with Revised Bloom's Taxonomy: Remembering (L1), Understanding (L2), Applying (L3), Analyzing (L4), Evaluating (L5), Creating (L6).*
