topics.title: Single Coil Ignition System & Coil Over Plug Ignition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET613 - AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Single Coil Ignition System & Coil Over Plug Ignition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Single Coil Ignition System (SCI) — Formal Definition

The **Single Coil Ignition System (SCI)**, historically known as the **Kettering Ignition System**, is a *mechanically-distributed, inductively-stored* ignition architecture that employs **one central ignition coil** to step up the battery's low-tension (LT) voltage (typically **12 V**) to a high-tension (HT) voltage of **20,000 – 30,000 V**, which is then routed—via a **rotor-arm-and-cap distributor**—to the correct cylinder's spark plug at the precisely timed instant of the compression stroke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEMET613 Module 2):** This system represents the *first-generation* electronic ignition topology, foundational to understanding all modern distributor-less (DIS) and coil-on-plug (COP) variants.

### 1.2 Coil-Over-Plug (COP) Ignition System — Formal Definition

The **Coil-Over-Plug (COP)** system, also termed **Coil-On-Plug** or **Direct Ignition**, is a *distributor-less, individually-controlled* ignition topology wherein a **dedicated ignition coil is mounted directly atop each spark plug**, eliminating HT cables entirely. The **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** triggers each coil independently through a solid-state switching device (an **IGBT** or **Darlington pair**), producing HT voltages in the range of **30,000 – 40,000 V** with minimal electromagnetic losses.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **single chef (SCI)** in a kitchen preparing five different curries simultaneously. The chef cooks each curry one after another on a single burner, then a *waiter (distributor)* carries each dish to the right table. The waiter is a bottleneck—he drops some gravy (voltage loss) and can be slow (timing inaccuracies).

Now imagine a **team of five chefs (COP)**, each standing right next to their designated table, cooking their assigned curry independently. No waiter is needed, no food gets cold, and each dish arrives at peak freshness. The COP system is this "five-chef" arrangement—faster, cleaner, and far more precise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Comparison Snapshot:**
> - **SCI:** 1 coil + 1 distributor + 4–8 HT wires → mechanical distribution
> - **COP:** N coils (one per cylinder) + 0 distributors + 0 HT wires → electronic distribution

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | SCI Typical Value | COP Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Voltage | **12 V DC** | **12–14 V DC (battery/alternator)** |
| Secondary Voltage | **20 – 30 kV** | **30 – 40 kV** |
| Primary Current (dwell) | **3 – 5 A** | **6 – 8 A** |
| Turns Ratio (Nₛ:Nₚ) | **~ 1 : 80 – 1 : 100** | **~ 1 : 60 – 1 : 100** |
| Dwell Angle | **35° – 60°** | **Variable (ECU-mapped)** |
| Spark Duration | **~ 1 – 1.5 ms** | **~ 1 – 3 ms** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Single Coil Ignition System — Operational Logic

The SCI operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** combined with **mutual inductance** between the primary and secondary windings of a step-up transformer (the ignition coil).

**Step-by-Step Operating Sequence:**

1. **Energization (Dwell Period):** With the ignition switch ON and the contact-breaker points **CLOSED**, primary current $I_p$ flows from the battery → ignition switch → primary winding (~$200$ turns of thick wire) → contact points → ground.
2. **Magnetic Field Build-up:** The primary current establishes a strong magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the soft-iron core, following Ampère's Law.
3. **Primary Current Saturation:** Current rises exponentially toward a steady-state value limited by primary inductance $L_p$ and resistance $R_p$ ($I = \frac{V}{R}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ where $\tau = L_p/R_p$).
4. **Contact-Breaker Opening:** The cam-driven contact points **OPEN** at the precise moment calculated by the mechanical advance mechanism.
5. **Flux Collapse:** The primary current is interrupted; the magnetic flux collapses rapidly across the secondary winding.
6. **High-Voltage Induction:** By **Faraday's Law**, the rapid $d\Phi/dt$ induces a high EMF in the secondary winding (~$20,000$ turns of fine wire).
7. **Arc Discharge:** The HT voltage jumps the spark-plug gap (typically **0.6 – 1.0 mm**), igniting the air-fuel mixture.
8. **Distributor Rotation:** The rotor arm (driven by the camshaft) directs the HT surge to the correct cylinder lead via the distributor cap.

### 2.2 Coil-Over-Plug Ignition System — Operational Logic

The COP system replaces mechanical distribution with **electronic, cylinder-specific triggering**.

**Step-by-Step Operating Sequence:**

1. **Crankshaft Position Sensing:** The **CKP sensor** and **CMP sensor** feed real-time piston position data to the ECU.
2. **ECU Mapped Calculation:** The ECU's ignition map determines the optimal **ignition advance angle** and **dwell time** based on load (MAP/MAF), RPM, temperature, and knock feedback.
3. **Coil Energization (Saturation):** The ECU commands the **IGBT driver** to switch ON, allowing primary current to ramp up to saturation. Dwell control is *closed-loop*—the IGBT may cut off current early if it exceeds a threshold (current-limit mode).
4. **Spark Command:** At the calculated firing instant, the ECU sends a low-voltage trigger pulse to the **control module** integrated within the coil assembly.
5. **Primary Current Interruption:** The IGBT turns OFF; flux collapses.
6. **HT Discharge In-Situ:** The high-voltage secondary discharge occurs *directly across the plug gap*—no HT wire is traversed, so virtually no leakage or RFI is lost.
7. **Multi-Stage Discharge (Advanced COP):** Many modern COP systems deliver a *primary discharge* followed by a *secondary sustaining discharge* (extended spark duration up to 2–3 ms) for lean-burn or EGR-diluted mixtures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "Why" Matters (KTU Examiner Insight):** The fundamental *Why* behind the COP's superiority lies in the elimination of two parasitic losses: (a) **capacitive leakage** along long HT cables, and (b) **mechanical timing drift** inherent in centrifugal and vacuum advance mechanisms.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\frac{N_s}{N_p} = \frac{V_s}{V_p} = n$ | $N$ = turns, $V$ = voltage, $n$ = turns ratio | dimensionless | Coil step-up ratio |
| 2 | $V_s = -N_s \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's Law of Induction | V (volts) | Secondary EMF |
| 3 | $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ | $L_p$ = primary inductance, $I_p$ = peak current | Joules (J) | Energy stored in coil |
| 4 | $V_p(t) = V_b\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $V_b$ = battery voltage, $\tau = L_p/R_p$ | V | Primary current ramp |
| 5 | $I_p = \frac{V_b}{R_p}\left(1 - e^{-t_{dwell}/\tau}\right)$ | $t_{dwell}$ = dwell time | A | Peak primary current |
| 6 | $P_{spark} = V_{arc} \cdot I_{arc}$ | Arc voltage, arc current | W (watts) | Spark power |
| 7 | $E_{required} \geq 0.5 \text{ to } 3 \text{ mJ}$ (idle) and $15-30$ mJ (start) | Required ignition energy | mJ | Minimum spark energy |
| 8 | $V_{breakdown} \approx 3000 \, \text{V} \cdot k / \text{mm}$ (at STP) | $k$ = gap factor, gap in mm | kV | Paschen's-law approximation |
| 9 | $f_{firing} = \frac{RPM}{2 \cdot 60} \text{ (4-stroke)}$ | $N$ = number of cylinders | Hz | Spark event frequency |
| 10 | $\theta_{dwell} = \omega \cdot t_{dwell}$ | Dwell angle in crank degrees | ° | Dwell angle conversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Vertical bars in formulas above (e.g., in $V_b \cdot k$ context) have been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SCI** still finds application in **two-wheelers, small gensets, lawn mowers, and legacy CI engine starting aids** where simplicity outweighs performance.
- **COP** dominates **passenger cars (BS-VI / Euro 7 platforms)**, **high-performance motorcycles**, and **direct-injection engines** where precise, repeatable, high-energy sparks are mandatory for catalyst light-off and lean combustion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Secondary Voltage Generated in the Ignition Coil

We begin with the **coupled-inductor model** of the ignition coil. The mutual inductance $M$ links the primary winding ($L_p$, $N_p$ turns) to the secondary winding ($L_s$, $N_s$ turns).

**Starting Point — Faraday's Law applied to the secondary winding:**

$$
V_s = -N_s \frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

**Substituting the magnetic flux linkage** $\Phi = B \cdot A$ (where $B$ is flux density and $A$ is core cross-section), and assuming the core saturates linearly before current interruption:

$$
V_s = -N_s A \frac{dB}{dt}
$$

**Relating $B$ to primary current** through the magnetizing inductance $L_p = \frac{N_p \Phi}{I_p}$:

$$
\Phi = \frac{L_p I_p}{N_p}
$$

**Differentiating the flux with respect to time:**

$$
\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{L_p}{N_p} \frac{dI_p}{dt}
$$

**Substituting back into the secondary voltage equation:**

$$
V_s = -N_s \cdot \frac{L_p}{N_p} \cdot \frac{dI_p}{dt}
$$

**Defining mutual inductance** $M = \frac{N_s}{N_p} L_p$:

$$
V_s = -M \frac{dI_p}{dt}
$$

**Expressing the result in terms of turns ratio** $n = \frac{N_s}{N_p}$:

$$
V_s = -n L_p \frac{dI_p}{dt}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The negative sign indicates **Lenz's law**—the induced EMF opposes the change in primary current. The magnitude of $V_s$ scales directly with turns ratio $n$ and the *rate* of primary current collapse.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Peak Primary Current & Dwell Energy

Given a DC primary circuit with battery voltage $V_b$, primary resistance $R_p$, and primary inductance $L_p$, when the contact points close at $t = 0$:

**Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) on the primary loop:**

$$
V_b = I_p R_p + L_p \frac{dI_p}{dt}
$$

**Solving this first-order ODE** with initial condition $I_p(0) = 0$:

$$
I_p(t) = \frac{V_b}{R_p} \left( 1 - e^{-t R_p / L_p} \right)
$$

**Defining the time constant** $\tau = \frac{L_p}{R_p}$:

$$
I_p(t) = \frac{V_b}{R_p} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)
$$

**Evaluating at the dwell instant** $t = t_{dwell}$:

$$
I_{p,peak} = \frac{V_b}{R_p} \left( 1 - e^{-t_{dwell}/\tau} \right)
$$

**Energy stored in the magnetic field** of the primary inductor:

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_{p,peak}^2
$$

**Substituting the peak current expression:**

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} L_p \left[ \frac{V_b}{R_p} \left( 1 - e^{-t_{dwell}/\tau} \right) \right]^2
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This energy is what gets transferred to the spark plug during the *flux collapse* event. KTU examiners commonly test whether students recognize that *longer dwell → higher stored energy → stronger spark*, but with diminishing returns once $t_{dwell} > 3\tau$ (then $e^{-t/\tau} \approx 0$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Dwell Angle from Crank-Degree Kinematics

The **dwell angle** $\theta_{dwell}$ is the crank-shaft rotation during which the primary circuit is energized. Given engine speed $N$ in RPM:

**Time per crank revolution:**

$$
T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Time per crank degree:**

$$
t_{deg} = \frac{T_{rev}}{360°} = \frac{60}{360 N} = \frac{1}{6N} \text{ s/deg}
$$

**Dwell time in seconds:**

$$
t_{dwell} = \theta_{dwell} \cdot t_{deg} = \frac{\theta_{dwell}}{6N}
$$

**Example KTU-Style Calculation (with $N = 3000$ RPM and $\theta_{dwell} = 50°$):**

$$
t_{dwell} = \frac{50}{6 \times 3000} = \frac{50}{18000} = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.78 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This 2.78 ms dwell must exceed the time constant $\tau$ by at least 3× for near-saturation. A typical $\tau \approx 0.5$–$1$ ms is well within this dwell, ensuring full coil saturation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SCI vs. COP

| Performance / Design Parameter | Single Coil Ignition (SCI) | Coil-Over-Plug (COP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Coils** | **1 (central)** | **N (one per cylinder)** |
| **HT Cables Required** | **Yes (4–8 long cables)** | **No (none)** |
| **Distributor** | **Yes (rotor + cap + cam)** | **No** |
| **Spark Energy at Plug** | **~ 60–80 % of coil output** | **~ 95–99 % of coil output** |
| **Voltage Loss (HT side)** | **2 – 5 kV cable leakage** | **< 0.5 kV** |
| **Timing Control** | **Mechanical (centrifugal + vacuum)** | **Electronic (ECU-mapped)** |
| **Dwell Control** | **Fixed by contact-gap adjustment** | **Variable, closed-loop IGBT-limited** |
| **Maximum RPM Capability** | **~ 5500 – 6000 RPM** | **> 8000 RPM (limited by coil, not mech.)** |
| **EMI / RFI Emissions** | **High (long HT wires radiate)** | **Very low (coil shielded at plug)** |
| **Maintenance** | **High (points, cap, rotor, condenser)** | **Near-zero (no wear parts)** |
| **Diagnostic Ease** | **Easy (visual + mechanical)** | **Requires scan tool / OBD-II** |
| **Cost (per system)** | **Low (~ 15–20 % of COP)** | **High (precision wound coils + drivers)** |
| **Failure Mode Criticality** | **Total engine stop** | **Single cylinder misfire (degraded)** |
| **Voltage Available at Plug** | **20 – 30 kV** | **30 – 40 kV** |
| **Spark Duration** | **1 – 1.5 ms** | **1 – 3 ms (multi-strike capability)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single Coil Ignition System — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    battNode1[BATTERY 12V DC] --> ignSwNode2[IGNITION SWITCH]
    ignSwNode2 --> balResNode3[BALLAST RESISTOR]
    balResNode3 --> primWdgNode4[PRIMARY WINDING Np approx 200 turns]
    primWdgNode4 --> contactNode5[CONTACT BREAKER POINTS]
    contactNode5 --> condNode6[CONDENSER 0.2 to 0.3 microfarad]
    condNode6 --> gndNode7[CHASSIS GROUND]
    contactNode5 --> camNode8[CAM DRIVEN MECHANISM]
    primWdgNode4 -.mutual induction.-> secWdgNode9[SECONDARY WINDING Ns approx 20000 turns]
    secWdgNode9 --> distCapNode10[DISTRIBUTOR CAP]
    distCapNode10 --> rotorNode11[ROTOR ARM]
    rotorNode11 --> plugNode12[SPARK PLUG CYL 1]
    rotorNode11 --> plugNode13[SPARK PLUG CYL 2]
    rotorNode11 --> plugNode14[SPARK PLUG CYL 3]
    rotorNode11 --> plugNode15[SPARK PLUG CYL 4]
    camNode8 --> advNode16[CENTRIFUGAL ADVANCE]
    advNode16 --> vacAdvNode17[VACUUM ADVANCE UNIT]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote current flow; the dotted arrow `.mutual induction.` represents the electromagnetic coupling between primary and secondary. The cam-driven mechanism is mechanically coupled to the rotor arm to synchronize spark distribution with piston position.

---

### 4.2 Coil-Over-Plug Ignition System — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ecuMainNode1[ECU ENGINE CONTROL UNIT] --> mapNode2[IGNITION MAP LOOKUP TABLE]
    mapNode2 --> ckpNode3[CKP SENSOR INPUT RPM]
    mapNode2 --> cmpNode4[CMP SENSOR INPUT CYL ID]
    mapNode2 --> mapSenNode5[MAP MAF SENSOR LOAD]
    mapNode2 --> knockNode6[KNOCK SENSOR FEEDBACK]
    ecuMainNode1 --> dwellCtrlNode7[DWELL CONTROL ALGORITHM]
    dwellCtrlNode7 --> igbtDrvNode8[IGBT DRIVER MODULE]
    igbtDrvNode8 --> coil1Node9[IGNITION COIL CYL 1 WITH IGBT INSIDE]
    igbtDrvNode8 --> coil2Node10[IGNITION COIL CYL 2 WITH IGBT INSIDE]
    igbtDrvNode8 --> coil3Node11[IGNITION COIL CYL 3 WITH IGBT INSIDE]
    igbtDrvNode8 --> coil4Node12[IGNITION COIL CYL 4 WITH IGBT INSIDE]
    coil1Node9 -.HT discharge.-> plug1Node13[SPARK PLUG CYL 1]
    coil2Node10 -.HT discharge.-> plug2Node14[SPARK PLUG CYL 2]
    coil3Node11 -.HT discharge.-> plug3Node15[SPARK PLUG CYL 3]
    coil4Node12 -.HT discharge.-> plug4Node16[SPARK PLUG CYL 4]
    ecuMainNode1 --> diagNode17[OBD II DIAGNOSTIC INTERFACE]
    diagNode17 --> misfireNode18[MISFIRE DETECTION MONITOR]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The ECU fuses multiple sensor inputs (CKP, CMP, MAP/MAF, knock) to compute the *optimal* spark timing and dwell. The IGBT driver is co-located within each coil's housing for minimal parasitic inductance. The dotted arrows `.HT discharge.` represent the localized, *in-situ* high-voltage breakdown across each plug's electrode gap.

---

### 4.3 Ignition Coil Internal Magnetic Flux Path (Cross-Sectional Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pInNode1[PRIMARY TERMINAL 12V] --> pWdgNode2[PRIMARY WINDING]
    pWdgNode2 --> coreTopNode3[SOFT IRON CORE LAMINATED]
    coreTopNode3 --> sWdgNode4[SECONDARY WINDING 20000 turns]
    sWdgNode4 --> sOutNode5[HT OUTPUT TO SPARK PLUG]
    pWdgNode2 --> pRetNode6[PRIMARY RETURN TO POINTS OR IGBT]
    coreTopNode3 -. flux lines collapse on interruption .-> fluxDirNode7[FLUX COLLAPSE dPhi dt HIGH]
    fluxDirNode7 --> sWdgNode4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** When the primary circuit is interrupted (mechanically in SCI, electronically in COP), the flux lines in the laminated iron core collapse violently. By Lenz's law, the rapidly changing flux induces a high EMF in the secondary, which is then routed to the spark plug (or directly discharged in COP).

---

### 4.4 Signal Waveform: Primary Current vs. Secondary Voltage (COP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    timeAxisNode1[Time axis t] --> dwellOnNode2[ECU commands IGBT ON at t0]
    dwellOnNode2 --> rampUpNode3[Primary current ramps up exponentially to Ipeak]
    rampUpNode3 --> satReachedNode4[Saturation reached at tsat]
    satReachedNode4 --> sparkCmdNode5[Spark command ECU at tspark]
    sparkCmdNode6[IGBT turns OFF rapidly] --> fluxCollapseNode7[Flux collapse dt approx 10 to 50 microsecond]
    fluxCollapseNode7 --> htSpikeNode8[Secondary voltage spike 30 to 40 kV]
    htSpikeNode8 --> arcSustainNode9[Sustaining arc voltage 1 to 2 kV for 1 to 2 ms]
    arcSustainNode9 --> endSparkNode10[Spark extinguishes when I falls below holding threshold]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This timing sequence illustrates the four-phase nature of a modern COP firing event: (1) Dwell energization, (2) Saturation, (3) High-voltage breakdown spike, and (4) Sustained arc discharge. The "sustaining" phase is unique to advanced COP coils and is critical for igniting lean or EGR-diluted mixtures.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMET613]**  
*With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Single Coil Ignition (Kettering) System used in a 4-cylinder SI engine. Mention the function of the condenser.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Single Coil Ignition System works as follows:

1. When the contact points **close**, current from the **12 V battery** flows through the **primary winding** (≈ 200 turns) of the ignition coil, building a magnetic field.  
2. The **cam** rotates and opens the contact points at the correct instant.  
3. The **magnetic flux collapses**, and by mutual induction, a high voltage (≈ 20–30 kV) is induced in the **secondary winding** (≈ 20,000 turns).  
4. This HT voltage is routed via the **rotor arm** inside the **distributor cap** to the appropriate spark plug.  
5. The plug arcs, igniting the air-fuel mixture.

**Function of the Condenser (0.2 – 0.3 µF):**  
- It absorbs the back-EMF produced at the moment the points open, preventing **arcing across the contact gap**.  
- It helps the flux collapse *faster*, thereby increasing the induced secondary voltage.  
- It protects the contact points from pitting and burning.

*(Examiner's Note: Full 3 marks require both a labeled sketch and the condenser's function.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEMET613]**  
*List any six advantages of Coil-Over-Plug (COP) ignition system over the conventional Single Coil Ignition system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — any six of the following):**

1. **No HT cables** → eliminates capacitive leakage and RFI.
2. **Higher spark energy** delivered directly to the plug (95–99 % efficiency).
3. **No distributor** → no mechanical wear, no timing drift.
4. **Per-cylinder ECU control** → individual, optimal timing for each cylinder.
5. **Higher RPM capability** (> 8000 RPM) since no mechanical limits.
6. **Better cold-start and lean-burn performance** due to multi-strike / extended-duration sparks.
7. **Lower emissions** from more complete combustion.
8. **Reduced maintenance** — no points, cap, rotor, or condenser to replace.
9. **Improved diagnostics** via OBD-II misfire detection per cylinder.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMET613 Module 2]**  
*(a)* Describe the construction and working principle of a Single Coil Ignition System with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the centrifugal and vacuum advance mechanisms. **[7 marks]**

*(b)* A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine runs at 3500 RPM. The ignition coil has $L_p = 6 \text{ mH}$, $R_p = 3 \text{ }\Omega$, and the dwell angle is $52°$. Calculate: (i) Dwell time, (ii) Peak primary current, (iii) Energy stored in the coil. Assume $V_b = 12$ V. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction & Working [7 marks]:**

The SCI system consists of:
- **Battery (12 V)** — DC source
- **Ignition switch** — operator control
- **Ballast resistor** — stabilizes primary current
- **Ignition coil** — step-up transformer ($N_s : N_p \approx 100:1$)
- **Contact breaker (points)** — mechanical switch
- **Condenser (0.2 – 0.3 µF)** — arc suppressor
- **Distributor (cam + rotor + cap)** — HT routing
- **Spark plugs** — discharge electrodes

**Working Principle (already covered in Section 2.1):** Primary energization → flux build-up → contact opening → flux collapse → HT induction → distributor routing → plug firing.

**Centrifugal Advance Mechanism [2 marks]:**  
As engine RPM rises, the **centrifugal weights** fly outward against spring tension, advancing the cam relative to the distributor shaft. This shifts the **point-opening instant earlier** in the crank cycle, compensating for the **shorter burn duration** at high RPM.

**Vacuum Advance Mechanism [2 marks]:**  
The **vacuum unit** (diaphragm connected to the carburetor venturi) advances timing under **part-throttle, light-load** conditions where the mixture burns slower due to high EGR residuals. At wide-open throttle, manifold vacuum drops and the advance retracts.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $L_p = 6 \text{ mH} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$; $R_p = 3 \text{ }\Omega$; $\theta_{dwell} = 52°$; $N = 3500 \text{ RPM}$; $V_b = 12 \text{ V}$.

**(i) Dwell Time:**

$$
t_{dwell} = \frac{\theta_{dwell}}{6N} = \frac{52}{6 \times 3500} = \frac{52}{21000} = 2.476 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.48 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Peak Primary Current:**

First, compute the time constant $\tau$:

$$
\tau = \frac{L_p}{R_p} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

Now substitute into the primary current equation:

$$
I_{p,peak} = \frac{V_b}{R_p} \left( 1 - e^{-t_{dwell}/\tau} \right) = \frac{12}{3} \left( 1 - e^{-2.48/2.0} \right)
$$

$$
I_{p,peak} = 4 \times \left( 1 - e^{-1.24} \right) = 4 \times (1 - 0.2894) = 4 \times 0.7106
$$

$$
I_{p,peak} = 2.842 \text{ A}
$$

**(iii) Energy Stored:**

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_{p,peak}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times (2.842)^2
$$

$$
E_{stored} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.077 = 2.423 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J} = 24.23 \text{ mJ}
$$

> **Final Answers:** $t_{dwell} = 2.48 \text{ ms}$; $I_{p,peak} = 2.84 \text{ A}$; $E_{stored} \approx 24.2 \text{ mJ}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEMET613 Module 2]**  
*(a)* With the aid of a block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Coil-Over-Plug (COP) ignition system. Discuss the role of the IGBT in coil switching. **[7 marks]**

*(b)* Compare the Single Coil Ignition System with the Coil-Over-Plug Ignition System on any seven technical parameters. Why is COP preferred in modern BS-VI compliant vehicles? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — COP Construction & Working [7 marks]:**

The COP system has the following main constituents:

1. **ECU** with ignition map logic
2. **CKP & CMP sensors** for crank/cam position
3. **MAP/MAF & temperature sensors** for load input
4. **Individual ignition coils** (one per cylinder), each with an internal IGBT
5. **Spark plugs** mounted directly beneath each coil (no HT wires)
6. **Knock sensor** for closed-loop retard feedback
7. **OBD-II diagnostic link** for per-cylinder misfire monitoring

**Working (already covered in Section 2.2):** ECU computes spark timing and dwell from sensor inputs → IGBT saturates primary → IGBT switches OFF → flux collapse → HT discharge directly across plug gap.

**Role of the IGBT [3 marks]:**

- The **Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor (IGBT)** is a voltage-controlled switch that handles the **high primary current** (up to 8 A) with negligible drive power.
- It enables **precise dwell control** by the ECU — the IGBT can be turned ON/OFF at microsecond resolution.
- It incorporates **current limiting** (via sense resistor feedback) to prevent coil overheating during extended dwell at low RPM.
- It provides **fast switching** (turn-off time < 5 µs), which maximizes the $dI/dt$ and therefore the induced secondary voltage.
- It is **thermally robust** and replaces the mechanical contact points, eliminating wear.

---

**Part (b) — Comparative Study [7 marks]:**

A seven-parameter comparison was already presented in **Section 3.4, Table** above. Key points:

1. **Number of coils** — SCI: 1; COP: N
2. **Distributor** — SCI: Yes; COP: No
3. **HT cables** — SCI: Yes (lossy); COP: No
4. **Timing control** — SCI: Mechanical; COP: Electronic
5. **Spark energy at plug** — SCI: 60–80 %; COP: 95–99 %
6. **Maintenance** — SCI: High; COP: Negligible
7. **RPM limit** — SCI: ~ 6000; COP: > 8000

**Why COP in BS-VI / Euro 6+ Vehicles [2 marks]:**

- BS-VI norms demand **ultra-low emissions** (NOₓ, HC, CO). COP enables **precise, optimal spark timing per cylinder**, ensuring **complete combustion** and **catalyst-friendly exhaust**.
- COP supports **lean-burn** and **EGR-diluted** combustion strategies through **multi-strike / extended-duration sparks**, which SCI cannot provide.
- OBD-II **misfire monitoring** is mandated by BS-VI; COP enables **per-cylinder misfire detection** (a single mechanical distributor cannot provide this granularity).
- **Cold-start HC emissions** are reduced by the COP's higher spark energy and faster saturation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "dwell angle" with "dwell time"** — they are *not* the same; remember $\theta_{dwell} = 6N \cdot t_{dwell}$. Students lose 1–2 marks for not converting.
> 2. **Forgetting the time-constant term** $e^{-t/\tau}$ in the primary current equation — the full expression is $I_p = (V_b/R_p)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, **not** simply $V_b/R_p$. Losing this term = –1 mark.
> 3. **Drawing the contact-breaker on the secondary side** — it must be on the **primary** side. Wrong placement = –2 marks on the diagram.
> 4. **Saying "the condenser stores charge"** — it does not; it *absorbs back-EMF* and *suppresses arcing*. This is a frequent conceptual error.
> 5. **Confusing IGBT with BJT/MOSFET** — in COP systems, the device is specifically an **IGBT** because of its high current + voltage + thermal handling. Naming a generic "transistor" loses partial credit.
> 6. **Neglecting the knock sensor** when describing ECU input variables — modern COP is **closed-loop**; the knock sensor must be mentioned.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Single Coil Ignition & Coil-Over-Plug Systems**

**Single Coil Ignition (SCI / Kettering) — Must-Know Facts:**

- ⚡ **One central coil** with turns ratio ≈ **1 : 100** steps 12 V → ≈ 25 kV.
- ⚡ Primary winding has **few turns of thick wire**; secondary has **many turns of fine wire**.
- ⚡ **Contact points + condenser** form the primary switchgear; the **condenser (0.2–0.3 µF)** is *not* a storage device — it suppresses arcing.
- ⚡ **Distributor** (rotor + cap + cam) is the *mechanical* HT router — its main weakness.
- ⚡ **Centrifugal advance** = RPM-based; **vacuum advance** = load-based.
- ⚡ Energy stored: $E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ → typically 20–80 mJ per spark.
- ⚡ Dwell angle (fixed, ~ 35°–60°) is set by contact-point gap.
- ⚡ Used in **older vehicles, two-wheelers, small gensets**; largely obsolete in modern passenger cars.
- ⚡ Formula to memorize: $V_s = -n L_p \frac{dI_p}{dt}$ where $n = N_s/N_p$.

**Coil-Over-Plug (COP) — Must-Know Facts:**

- ⚡ **One coil per cylinder**, mounted **directly on the spark plug** — no HT wire, no distributor.
- ⚡ **ECU + IGBT** replace the contact breaker — fully electronic, fully solid-state.
- ⚡ HT output: **30 – 40 kV**; spark duration: **1 – 3 ms** (multi-strike).
- ⚡ IGBT provides **closed-loop current limiting** → prevents coil overheating.
- ⚡ ECU inputs: **CKP, CMP, MAP/MAF, IAT, CLT, Knock sensor**.
- ⚡ Operating modes: **saturation mode** (constant energy) and **current-limit mode** (constant current).
- ⚡ Diagnostic edge: **per-cylinder misfire detection** via OBD-II crank-speed fluctuation analysis.
- ⚡ Found in **all modern BS-VI / Euro 6+ vehicles**, **direct-injection engines**, and **hybrid powertrains**.
- ⚡ Spark energy at plug ≈ **95–99 %** of coil output (vs. 60–80 % for SCI).

**Cross-Topic High-Yield Comparisons:**

- ⚡ **Voltage at plug:** SCI 20–30 kV vs. COP 30–40 kV.
- ⚡ **RPM limit:** SCI ≈ 6000 vs. COP > 8000.
- ⚡ **Efficiency:** SCI 60–80 % vs. COP 95–99 %.
- ⚡ **Maintenance:** SCI high vs. COP near-zero.
- ⚡ **Failure mode:** SCI = total stop; COP = single-cylinder misfire.
- ⚡ **Cost:** SCI low; COP high (but offset by reliability).

**Formula Flash-Card Summary:**

$$
\frac{N_s}{N_p} = \frac{V_s}{V_p} \qquad V_s = -M \frac{dI_p}{dt} \qquad E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2
$$

$$
I_p(t) = \frac{V_b}{R_p}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \qquad \tau = \frac{L_p}{R_p} \qquad t_{dwell} = \frac{\theta_{dwell}}{6N}
$$

**One-Line Takeaway for the Examiner:** *SCI is a mechanical, single-coil, distributor-based system suitable for low-RPM legacy engines; COP is an electronic, multi-coil, distributor-less, ECU-controlled system that delivers near-lossless high-energy sparks per cylinder — the de-facto standard in modern BS-VI/Euro 6+ vehicles.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Transmission & Chassis: Gear Ratio, Manual Transmission: Synchromesh, Automated and Automatic Transmission Basics, Friction and Fluid Clutches Basics, Different Types Chassis - Tubular to Integrated, Duties of a Chassis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET613 - AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transmission & Chassis: Gear Ratio, Manual Transmission: Synchromesh, Automated and Automatic Transmission Basics, Friction and Fluid Clutches Basics, Different Types Chassis - Tubular to Integrated, Duties of a Chassis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Drivetrain Philosophy — What is Transmission?

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **Transmission System** of an automobile is a speed and torque modifying mechanical device that transmits the engine's power to the driving wheels. It allows the vehicle to operate efficiently across a wide range of speeds, loads, and road conditions by providing multiple gear ratios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The internal combustion engine has a very narrow useful RPM band (typically **1000–5000 rpm**). However, the vehicle needs to move from 0 km/h to 200 km/h. The transmission solves this mismatch — it is essentially a **mechanical translator** between the engine's narrow power band and the vehicle's wide speed demand.

**Conceptual Analogy (Bicycle Gear Intuition):**
Think of a bicycle. When you climb a hill, you shift to a **low gear (small front chainring, large rear sprocket)** so your legs move many times per wheel revolution — easy to pedal, low speed. On a flat road, you shift to a **high gear (large front chainring, small rear sprocket)** — fewer leg rotations per wheel revolution, but high speed. The transmission in a car works **exactly the same way**, except the engine replaces your legs.

---

### 1.2 Gear Ratio — The Heart of Transmission

**Formal Definition:**
**Gear Ratio (G.R.)** is the ratio of the number of teeth on the driven gear to the number of teeth on the driver gear, or equivalently, the ratio of input speed to output speed.

$$\text{Gear Ratio} = \frac{N_{\text{driven}}}{N_{\text{driver}}} = \frac{\omega_{\text{input}}}{\omega_{\text{output}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Definitions Agree:** If two meshing gears have the same circular pitch, the number of teeth is proportional to the pitch circle diameter. So teeth count and angular velocity ratios are mathematically equivalent.

---

### 1.3 Manual Transmission with Synchromesh

**Formal Definition:**
A **Synchromesh Transmission** is a constant-mesh manual gearbox in which synchronizing devices (synchro rings) equalize the speed of the gear to be engaged with the main shaft before engagement, enabling smooth, clash-free, and silent gear shifts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Older transmissions used "sliding mesh" gears where the driver had to manually double-clutch to match speeds. **Synchromesh eliminated this need** — the synchro unit does the speed matching for you automatically.

---

### 1.4 Automated and Automatic Transmission — The Hands-Off Approach

**Formal Definitions:**
- **Automated Manual Transmission (AMT):** A conventional manual gearbox with **electronic actuators** (instead of a clutch pedal and gear lever) that automate clutch operation and gear selection.
- **Automatic Transmission (AT):** A **planetary gear set** based transmission with a **torque converter** (fluid coupling) and a **hydraulic control system** that changes gears automatically based on throttle position and vehicle speed.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of AMT as a "**robot driving a manual car for you**" — same hardware, just electronically controlled actuators pressing the clutch and moving the lever. An AT, however, is an entirely different beast — it uses a **fluid coupling (torque converter)** like a "hydraulic handshake" between engine and gearbox, allowing the engine to idle while the car stays still.

---

### 1.5 Clutches — The Disengagement Devices

**Formal Definition:**
A **Clutch** is a mechanical device located between the engine and the transmission that:
1. **Engages** power flow from engine to gearbox during driving.
2. **Disengages** power flow to allow smooth gear changes and idle the engine with the vehicle stationary.

**Types Covered in KTU Module 3:**
- **Friction Clutch:** Mechanical, dry or wet, uses friction surfaces (single plate, multi-plate, semi-centrifugal, centrifugal).
- **Fluid Clutch (Fluid Coupling / Torque Converter):** Uses hydraulic fluid to transmit torque; no mechanical contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A typical passenger car friction clutch has **friction lining area of 200–400 cm²** and transmits torque in the range of **100–400 N·m**.

---

### 1.6 Chassis — The Skeleton of the Automobile

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **Chassis** is the entire framework of a vehicle on which the body, engine, transmission, suspension, brakes, and other systems are mounted. It carries all the loads — static, dynamic, and impact — and provides the structural backbone.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
The chassis is to a car what the **human skeleton** is to the body. Just as your skeleton supports muscles, organs, and skin, the chassis supports the engine (heart), drivetrain (limbs), body (skin), and absorbs shocks like your joints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A modern passenger car has a **Monocoque (Unibody) Chassis** where the body and chassis are integrated into a single load-bearing structure — unlike older ladder frames where they were separate. This evolution is critical for modern automotive design.

---

### 1.7 Duties of a Chassis (The 7 Pillars)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Chassis Must:**
> 1. Support the **weight of the body, engine, and passengers**.
> 2. Withstand **forces caused by sudden braking, acceleration, and cornering**.
> 3. Resist **torsional twisting** from uneven road surfaces.
> 4. Absorb **vibrations and shocks** for ride comfort.
> 5. Provide a **rigid platform** for mounting components.
> 6. Ensure **safety** of occupants during collisions (crashworthiness).
> 7. Maintain proper **wheel alignment and geometry** under all conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Gear Ratio — The Mathematical Foundation

**Step 1 — Define Speed Ratio:**
The **speed ratio (i)** is the ratio of the angular velocity of the driving member to that of the driven member.

$$i = \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

Where:
- $\omega_1, \omega_2$ = angular velocities of driver and driven gears (rad/s)
- $N_1, N_2$ = number of teeth
- $D_1, D_2$ = pitch circle diameters
- $T_1, T_2$ = number of teeth

**Step 2 — Define Mechanical Advantage (Torque Ratio):**
In an ideal (lossless) gear train, power in equals power out, so:

$$T_1 \cdot \omega_1 = T_2 \cdot \omega_2$$

Rearranging gives the **torque ratio** (mechanical advantage):

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** When gear ratio $i > 1$ (reduction gearing), output speed is reduced but output torque is multiplied by the same factor. This is precisely what we need at low speeds for hill climbing and acceleration.

**Step 3 — Overall Transmission Ratio:**
The total transmission ratio combines the gearbox ratio, final drive ratio, and any transfer case ratio:

$$i_{\text{total}} = i_{\text{gearbox}} \times i_{\text{final drive}} \times i_{\text{transfer}}$$

**Step 4 — Vehicle Speed Equation:**
With the total ratio known, vehicle speed is:

$$V = \frac{2 \pi \cdot r_w \cdot N_e}{60 \cdot i_{\text{total}}} \text{ (m/s)}$$

Where $r_w$ is the effective wheel radius and $N_e$ is engine RPM.

---

### 2.2 Synchromesh Mechanism — The Logical Steps

**Operational Sequence:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchromesh Engagement — Step by Step:**
> 1. **Neutral state:** Synchro sleeve is centered; gear rotates freely on the mainshaft via needle bearings.
> 2. **Driver selects gear:** Shift fork moves the synchro sleeve toward the desired gear.
> 3. **Contact phase:** Sleeve pushes the **blocking ring (synchro ring)** against the cone of the gear.
> 4. **Synchronization phase:** Friction between the cone surfaces of the ring and gear cone equalizes speeds (this is where the "synchro" magic happens).
> 5. **Indexing:** Once speeds match, the **splines engage** silently.
> 6. **Locking:** Detent balls drop into grooves, locking the sleeve in position.

**Key Components of a Synchromesh Unit:**
- **Synchro sleeve** (shift coupling)
- **Synchro hub** (splined to mainshaft)
- **Blocking ring (baulk ring)** — brass/bronze, has friction cone
- **Spring-loaded balls** (for detent locking)
- **Shift fork** (operated by driver)

---

### 2.3 Clutch Theory — The Engineering Foundation

**Friction Clutch — Operating Principle:**

The torque transmitted by a friction clutch is:

$$T_{\text{clutch}} = \mu \cdot F_{\text{axial}} \cdot n \cdot R_m$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (typical: 0.25–0.4 for organic linings)
- $F_{\text{axial}}$ = axial clamping force from the pressure plate (N)
- $n$ = number of friction surfaces (single plate = 2, dual plate = 4)
- $R_m$ = **mean friction radius** = $\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$

Where $R_o$ = outer radius of friction lining, $R_i$ = inner radius of friction lining.

**Fluid Clutch (Torque Converter) — Operating Principle:**

The torque converter transmits torque via a **three-element hydraulic circuit**: impeller (driven by engine), turbine (driving the gearbox), and stator (on a one-way clutch). The **slip ratio** is:

$$s = \frac{N_{\text{impeller}} - N_{\text{turbine}}}{N_{\text{impeller}}}$$

At stall (vehicle stopped, engine at idle), slip = 1; at lockup (no slip), s = 0.

The **torque multiplication factor** is:

$$K_t = \frac{T_{\text{turbine}}}{T_{\text{impeller}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A typical automotive torque converter has $K_t = 1.8$ to $2.5$ at stall — meaning it multiplies engine torque by 2x to 2.5x, eliminating the need for a friction clutch in an automatic.

---

### 2.4 Chassis Types — The Evolution

**Five Major Chassis Architectures:**

| Chassis Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Ladder Frame** | Two longitudinal rails with cross members | Trucks, off-roaders, SUVs |
| **Tubular Space Frame** | Welded steel/aluminum tubes | Race cars (Formula 1) |
| **Backbone Tube** | Single central tubular spine | Older sports cars (Lotus Elan) |
| **Monocoque (Unibody)** | Body and chassis in one load-bearing shell | Modern sedans, hatchbacks |
| **Integrated / Semi-Monocoque** | Combination of sub-frames with monocoque body | Modern SUVs, premium cars |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gear Ratio (speed) | $i = \omega_1 / \omega_2$ | Driver/Driven angular velocity | dimensionless |
| 2 | Gear Ratio (teeth) | $i = N_2 / N_1$ | Driven/Driver teeth count | dimensionless |
| 3 | Torque Ratio | $T_2 = i \cdot T_1$ | Output torque scaling | N·m |
| 4 | Vehicle Speed | $V = (2\pi r_w N_e) / (60 \cdot i_{\text{total}})$ | Engine RPM to road speed | m/s |
| 5 | Engine Torque to Wheel | $T_w = T_e \cdot i_{\text{total}} \cdot \eta$ | With transmission efficiency | N·m |
| 6 | Clutch Torque | $T_c = \mu \cdot F \cdot n \cdot R_m$ | Friction clutch capacity | N·m |
| 7 | Mean Friction Radius | $R_m = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$ | Effective radius for clutch | m |
| 8 | Torque Converter Ratio | $K_t = T_{\text{turbine}} / T_{\text{impeller}}$ | Torque multiplication | dimensionless |
| 9 | Slip Ratio | $s = (N_i - N_t) / N_i$ | Impeller-Turbine speed | dimensionless |
| 10 | Overall Drive Ratio | $i_{\text{total}} = i_{\text{gb}} \cdot i_{\text{fd}}$ | Gearbox × Final drive | dimensionless |
| 11 | Pitch Line Velocity | $v = \pi \cdot d \cdot N$ | Gear tooth linear velocity | m/s |
| 12 | Tractive Effort | $TE = T_w / r_w$ | Force at wheel-road contact | N |
| 13 | Beam Strength (Chassis) | $M = \sigma \cdot I / y$ | Bending stress in frame | N·m |

> **Units Reminder:** All angular quantities must be in **rad/s** (convert RPM using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$). Distances in **meters** for SI consistency.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Vehicle Speed from Gear Ratio

**Problem Framework:**
Given engine RPM, gear ratios, and wheel dimensions, derive the vehicle speed.

**Step 1:** Express the angular velocity of the engine in rad/s from RPM.

$$\omega_e = \frac{2 \pi N_e}{60}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the gear train ratio sequentially. For a system with gearbox (gb) and final drive (fd):

$$\omega_{\text{wheel}} = \frac{\omega_e}{i_{\text{gb}} \cdot i_{\text{fd}}}$$

**Step 3:** Convert wheel angular velocity to linear vehicle velocity using the wheel radius $r_w$:

$$V = \omega_{\text{wheel}} \cdot r_w$$

**Step 4:** Substitute all intermediate results into a single equation:

$$V = \frac{2 \pi N_e}{60 \cdot i_{\text{gb}} \cdot i_{\text{fd}}} \cdot r_w$$

**Step 5:** If the engine rotates at $N_e$ RPM, in one minute the wheel makes $N_e / (i_{\text{gb}} \cdot i_{\text{fd}})$ revolutions. The distance covered per minute is the wheel circumference times revolutions:

$$V \cdot 60 = 2 \pi r_w \cdot \frac{N_e}{i_{\text{gb}} \cdot i_{\text{fd}}}$$

Solving for $V$:

$$V = \frac{2 \pi r_w \cdot N_e}{60 \cdot i_{\text{total}}}$$

This is the **standard KTU vehicle speed formula** in m/s. To convert to km/h, multiply by 3.6.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Gear Ratio & Vehicle Speed

**Problem Statement (KTU Numerical Pattern):**
A car has a 4-speed manual transmission with the following gear ratios:
- 1st gear: 3.50:1
- 2nd gear: 2.10:1
- 3rd gear: 1.40:1
- 4th gear: 1.00:1
- Reverse: 3.80:1
- Final drive: 3.73:1
- Wheel radius: 0.32 m
- Engine peak torque RPM: 3500

**Find:**
(a) The total reduction ratio in 1st gear.
(b) The vehicle speed when engine is at 3500 RPM in 1st gear (in km/h).
(c) The torque at the wheels in 1st gear if engine torque is 180 N·m (assume 90% efficiency).

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Total reduction in 1st gear:

$$i_{\text{total}} = i_{\text{gb}} \times i_{\text{fd}} = 3.50 \times 3.73 = 13.055$$

**Part (b):** Vehicle speed:

$$V = \frac{2 \pi \cdot r_w \cdot N_e}{60 \cdot i_{\text{total}}}$$

$$V = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 0.32 \cdot 3500}{60 \cdot 13.055}$$

$$V = \frac{7037.16}{783.30} = 8.984 \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to km/h:

$$V = 8.984 \times 3.6 = 32.34 \text{ km/h}$$

**Part (c):** Wheel torque:

$$T_w = T_e \cdot i_{\text{total}} \cdot \eta = 180 \times 13.055 \times 0.90 = 2114.9 \text{ N·m}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Friction Clutch Sizing

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A single-plate dry friction clutch has:
- Outer radius of lining: $R_o = 150$ mm
- Inner radius of lining: $R_i = 90$ mm
- Axial clamping force: $F = 3500$ N
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.30$

**Find:** The torque capacity of the clutch.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the mean friction radius using the uniform pressure theory:

$$R_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$$

Compute $R_o^3$ and $R_i^3$:

$$R_o^3 = (0.150)^3 = 3.375 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$
$$R_i^3 = (0.090)^3 = 7.29 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$
$$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 3.375 \times 10^{-3} - 0.729 \times 10^{-3} = 2.646 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Compute $R_o^2$ and $R_i^2$:

$$R_o^2 = (0.150)^2 = 0.0225 \text{ m}^2$$
$$R_i^2 = (0.090)^2 = 0.0081 \text{ m}^2$$
$$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 0.0225 - 0.0081 = 0.0144 \text{ m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$R_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{2.646 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0144} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.18375 = 0.1225 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the clutch torque (single plate = 2 friction surfaces, so $n = 2$):

$$T_c = \mu \cdot F \cdot n \cdot R_m$$
$$T_c = 0.30 \times 3500 \times 2 \times 0.1225$$
$$T_c = 257.25 \text{ N·m}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This clutch can safely transmit up to **257.25 N·m** of engine torque before slipping — adequate for a mid-size passenger car engine.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Power Transmission Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine] --> B[Friction Clutch]
    B --> C[Manual Gearbox with Synchromesh]
    C --> D[Propeller Shaft]
    D --> E[Final Drive and Differential]
    E --> F[Drive Shafts]
    F --> G[Driving Wheels]
    A1[Throttle Input] --> A
    G --> H[Road Surface]
    H --> I[Tractive Effort Output]
    I --> J[Vehicle Acceleration and Speed]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Power flows from the engine through the clutch (engages/disengages), into the gearbox (selects ratio), down the propeller shaft, through the final drive (90° turn + reduction), to the differential (splits torque to both wheels), and finally to the road.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Synchromesh Gear Engagement Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Neutral Position] --> P1[Driver Pushes Shift Lever]
    P1 --> P2[Shift Fork Moves Synchro Sleeve]
    P2 --> P3[Sleeve Contacts Blocking Ring]
    P3 --> P4{Do Speeds Match?}
    P4 -->|No| P5[Friction on Cone Surface Equalizes Speed]
    P5 --> P4
    P4 -->|Yes| P6[Splines Engage Gear with Mainshaft]
    P6 --> P7[Detent Balls Lock Sleeve]
    P7 --> P8[Gear Fully Engaged]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **logical decision flow** of a synchromesh unit. The "loop" between P3 and P5 is the **synchronization phase** — the heart of synchromesh operation.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparative Transmission Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MT[Manual Transmission]
        M1[Engine] --> M2[Clutch Pedal] --> M3[Driver Operated Gear Lever] --> M4[Synchromesh Gearbox]
    end
    subgraph AMT[Automated Manual Transmission]
        A1[Engine] --> A2[Electrohydraulic Clutch Actuator] --> A3[Electronic Gear Selector] --> A4[Synchromesh Gearbox]
    end
    subgraph AT[Automatic Transmission]
        T1[Engine] --> T2[Torque Converter Fluid Coupling] --> T3[Hydraulic Valve Body] --> T4[Planetary Gear Set]
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Three transmission philosophies — manual (human does everything), AMT (robot does what humans did), and AT (entirely different fluid + planetary architecture).

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Chassis Evolution from Tubular to Integrated

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Ladder Frame] --> C2[Tubular Space Frame]
    C2 --> C3[Backbone Tube Frame]
    C3 --> C4[Semi Monocoque]
    C4 --> C5[Monocoque Unibody]
    C5 --> C6[Integrated Modular Platform]
    C1 -.- C1A["Trucks and Offroad"]
    C2 -.- C2A["Formula 1 Race Cars"]
    C5 -.- C5A["Modern Passenger Cars"]
    C6 -.- C6A["Electric Vehicles and Modern SUVs"]
```

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Friction Clutch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FL[Flywheel] --> CPS[Crankshaft End]
    CPS --> PC[Pressure Plate Spring]
    PC --> PP[Pressure Plate]
    PP --> FLIN[Friction Disc with Lining]
    FLIN --> TS[Transmission Input Shaft]
    PP -.- SS[Three Stage Spring]
    FLIN -.- SD[Splined Hub]
    SD -.- TS
    OP1[Engine Torque] --> FL
    FL --> OP2[Transmits to Friction Disc]
    OP2 --> OP3[Clutch Engaged State]
    FLIN --> CL[Clutch Pedal Depressed]
    CL --> PR[Pressure Plate Retracts]
    PR --> OP4[Disc Free to Spin - Disengaged]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** In the engaged state, the pressure plate clamps the friction disc against the flywheel, transmitting torque. When the pedal is pressed, the pressure plate retracts, releasing the disc — engine and gearbox are decoupled.

---

### 4.6 Block Diagram — Torque Converter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Engine Crankshaft] --> IMP[Impeller Pump]
    IMP -->|Hydraulic Fluid| TUR[Turbine]
    TUR --> GB[Gearbox Input]
    IMP -.- ST[Stator on One Way Clutch]
    ST -.- TUR
    AT1[Stall Condition] -.- IMP
    AT2[Lockup Clutch] -.- TUR
    AT3[Coupling Phase] -.- IMP
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The impeller (driven by engine) accelerates transmission fluid onto the turbine blades, which drives the gearbox. The stator redirects returning fluid to amplify torque. At high speeds, a lockup clutch mechanically couples the two halves for efficiency.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**
*Define the term "Gear Ratio." Explain with a sketch how it affects vehicle speed and torque in a manual transmission.*

**Model Answer:**
Gear ratio is defined as the ratio of the number of teeth on the driven gear to the number of teeth on the driving gear, or equivalently, the ratio of input speed to output speed:

$$i = \frac{N_{\text{driven}}}{N_{\text{driver}}} = \frac{\omega_{\text{input}}}{\omega_{\text{output}}}$$

In a manual transmission, different gear ratios allow the engine to operate within its optimal power band. **In low gears (high ratio like 3.5:1)**, the output speed is reduced but the output torque is multiplied by the same factor, providing high pulling power for starting and climbing. **In high gears (low ratio like 1:1)**, output speed increases while torque decreases, suitable for cruising at highway speeds. Thus, gear ratio is the key mechanism that matches engine output to varying road load demands.

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**
*List any three duties of a chassis in an automobile.*

**Model Answer:**
The three principal duties of a chassis are:
1. **Load Support:** It supports the static weight of the vehicle body, engine, transmission, and passengers, providing a rigid platform for all components.
2. **Force Resistance:** It must withstand dynamic forces arising from acceleration, braking, cornering, and uneven road surfaces without permanent deformation.
3. **Vibration Absorption:** It works in conjunction with the suspension system to absorb road shocks and vibrations, ensuring ride comfort and protecting mounted components from fatigue failure.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students confuse **gear ratio** with **torque ratio direction** — remember: high gear ratio = high torque, low speed; low gear ratio = low torque, high speed.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In clutch problems, forgetting to use **mean radius** (or using outer radius instead) leads to 30–40% error.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students write "synchromesh means same mesh" in exams — this is wrong. Synchromesh = **synchronized mesh** (speeds are synchronized before engagement).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In automatic transmission questions, students describe AMT and AT interchangeably. **AMT is automated MANUAL; AT is fully AUTOMATIC with torque converter and planetary gears.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Transmission Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **synchromesh gearbox**. Highlight the role of the **blocking ring** and **synchro sleeve**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A four-speed manual gearbox in a car has the following data:
- Gear ratios: 1st = 3.5, 2nd = 2.1, 3rd = 1.4, 4th = 1.0
- Final drive ratio = 4.1
- Wheel radius = 0.30 m
- Engine torque = 200 N·m at 3500 RPM
- Transmission efficiency = 0.92

Calculate:
(i) The vehicle speed in 2nd gear at engine speed of 3500 RPM (in km/h).
(ii) The tractive effort at the wheels in 1st gear at the same engine speed.
(iii) The torque at the clutch in 1st gear if clutch slippage is 5%. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Synchromesh Gearbox:**

A synchromesh gearbox uses **constant-mesh gears** with synchronizing units to allow smooth, clash-free gear changes. The main components are:
- **Mainshaft (output shaft)** with splined synchro hubs.
- **Countershaft (layshaft)** carrying all driving gears permanently meshed with the mainshaft gears.
- **Synchro unit** consisting of a hub, sleeve, blocking ring, and spring-loaded balls.
- **Shift forks** operated by the driver via the gear lever.

**Working:** When the driver selects a gear, the shift fork moves the synchro sleeve. The sleeve contacts the cone of the blocking ring, which is frictionally engaged with the gear cone on the mainshaft. The **blocking ring** uses friction to equalize the speeds of the sleeve and the gear. Once speeds match, the **spline teeth of the sleeve** slide over the dog teeth of the gear, locking them together. Spring-loaded detent balls snap into grooves to confirm engagement. This process prevents grinding and provides silent shifts.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Vehicle Speed in 2nd gear:**

Total ratio in 2nd gear:

$$i_{\text{total}} = i_{\text{gb}} \times i_{\text{fd}} = 2.1 \times 4.1 = 8.61$$

Vehicle speed:

$$V = \frac{2 \pi r_w N_e}{60 \cdot i_{\text{total}}}$$

$$V = \frac{2 \pi \times 0.30 \times 3500}{60 \times 8.61}$$

$$V = \frac{6597.34}{516.6} = 12.77 \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to km/h:

$$V = 12.77 \times 3.6 = 45.97 \text{ km/h}$$

**(ii) Tractive Effort in 1st gear:**

Total ratio in 1st gear:

$$i_{\text{total}} = 3.5 \times 4.1 = 14.35$$

Wheel torque:

$$T_w = T_e \cdot i_{\text{total}} \cdot \eta = 200 \times 14.35 \times 0.92 = 2640.4 \text{ N·m}$$

Tractive effort:

$$TE = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{2640.4}{0.30} = 8801.33 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Clutch Torque in 1st gear (with 5% slip):**

When there is 5% slippage, the clutch torque is the actual torque transmitted considering slip losses. The effective engine torque reaching the clutch:

$$T_{\text{clutch effective}} = T_e \times (1 - s) = 200 \times (1 - 0.05) = 190 \text{ N·m}$$

This is the torque the friction surfaces must handle without slipping under steady-state conditions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Chassis & Clutch Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Compare the constructional features of **Ladder Frame**, **Tubular Space Frame**, and **Monocoque Chassis**. State two advantages and one disadvantage of each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A single-plate dry friction clutch has:
- Outer radius of friction lining = 160 mm
- Inner radius of friction lining = 100 mm
- Axial spring force = 4000 N
- Coefficient of friction = 0.32
- Engine power = 60 kW at 3000 RPM

Calculate:
(i) The mean friction radius of the clutch.
(ii) The torque capacity of the clutch.
(iii) Whether the clutch is adequate for the given engine (justify with calculation). (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Chassis Comparison:**

| Feature | Ladder Frame | Tubular Space Frame | Monocoque |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Two longitudinal rails + cross members | Welded network of steel/aluminum tubes | Body panels form load-bearing structure |
| **Advantages** | (1) Easy to manufacture, (2) Excellent for off-road loads | (1) High stiffness-to-weight ratio, (2) Excellent torsional rigidity | (1) Lightweight, (2) Superior NVH and crash safety |
| **Disadvantages** | Heavy and bulky | Complex to manufacture and repair | Expensive to repair after collision |

**Part (b) — Clutch Numerical:**

**(i) Mean Friction Radius:**

$$R_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$$

Compute numerator and denominator:

$$R_o^3 = (0.160)^3 = 4.096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$
$$R_i^3 = (0.100)^3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$
$$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 3.096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$R_o^2 = (0.160)^2 = 0.0256 \text{ m}^2$$
$$R_i^2 = (0.100)^2 = 0.0100 \text{ m}^2$$
$$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 0.0156 \text{ m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$R_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{3.096 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0156} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.1985 = 0.1323 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Torque Capacity (n = 2 for single plate):**

$$T_c = \mu \cdot F \cdot n \cdot R_m = 0.32 \times 4000 \times 2 \times 0.1323 = 338.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Adequacy Check:**

Engine torque at 3000 RPM with 60 kW power:

$$T_e = \frac{P \times 60}{2 \pi N_e} = \frac{60000 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 3000} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^6}{18849.6} = 191.0 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $T_c$ (338.7 N·m) > $T_e$ (191.0 N·m), the clutch has a **safety factor** of:

$$SF = \frac{338.7}{191.0} = 1.77$$

This is **adequate** (typical safety factor required: 1.3 to 1.5). The clutch can safely transmit the engine torque with a healthy margin for transient loads like hard acceleration and gear shifts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)**

**Transmission Fundamentals:**
- ☐ **Gear Ratio (i)** = driven teeth / driver teeth = $\omega_{\text{in}} / \omega_{\text{out}}$; **dimensionless**.
- ☐ Torque ratio = gear ratio (for ideal gears); **High ratio = high torque, low speed**.
- ☐ **Total ratio** = gearbox ratio × final drive ratio × transfer case ratio.
- ☐ **Vehicle speed** $V = (2\pi r_w N_e) / (60 \cdot i_{\text{total}})$ in m/s; multiply by **3.6** for km/h.
- ☐ **Wheel torque** = $T_e \cdot i_{\text{total}} \cdot \eta$ where $\eta$ is transmission efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95).
- ☐ **Tractive effort** = Wheel torque / wheel radius — the actual force pushing the vehicle forward.

**Synchromesh Gearbox:**
- ☐ **Synchromesh = Synchronized Mesh** (not "same mesh").
- ☐ Three magic components: **synchro sleeve, blocking ring (baulk ring), spring-loaded balls**.
- ☐ Friction cone surface does the **speed matching** before spline engagement.
- ☐ **Advantage:** No double-clutching required; smooth, silent shifts.
- ☐ **Disadvantage:** Slightly more complex than sliding mesh; relies on synchro ring wear life.

**Automated and Automatic Transmission:**
- ☐ **AMT** = Manual gearbox + electronic actuators (cost-effective, used in entry cars).
- ☐ **AT** = Torque converter + planetary gear set + hydraulic valve body (premium, smoother).
- ☐ **CVT** = Continuously Variable Transmission using belt/pulley (best fuel economy).
- ☐ **DCT** = Dual-Clutch Transmission (two clutches, lightning-fast shifts, used in performance cars).

**Clutches:**
- ☐ **Friction clutch** = mechanical, uses $\mu$ and $F_{\text{axial}}$; works on the principle of friction torque.
- ☐ **Clutch torque** $T_c = \mu \cdot F \cdot n \cdot R_m$; uses **mean radius** (not outer radius).
- ☐ **Mean radius** $R_m = (2/3) \cdot (R_o^3 - R_i^3) / (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$ — memorize this!
- ☐ **Fluid coupling** = hydraulic, no mechanical contact; **torque converter** adds a stator for torque multiplication.
- ☐ **Stall torque ratio** of a torque converter = 1.8 to 2.5 (typical).
- ☐ **Slip ratio** $s = (N_{\text{impeller}} - N_{\text{turbine}}) / N_{\text{impeller}}$; 1 at stall, 0 at lockup.

**Chassis Types & Duties:**
- ☐ **Ladder frame** = two rails + cross members; for trucks and off-roaders.
- ☐ **Tubular space frame** = welded tubes; for race cars (high stiffness-to-weight).
- ☐ **Backbone tube** = single central spine; older sports cars.
- ☐ **Monocoque (unibody)** = body and chassis integrated; modern passenger cars.
- ☐ **Integrated/modular platform** = used in modern EVs and SUVs.
- ☐ **Seven Duties of a Chassis:** support weight, resist dynamic forces, resist torsion, absorb vibrations, provide rigid platform, ensure crash safety, maintain wheel geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics for KTU Exam:**
> - **"High Ratio = Hi-Torque, Lo-Speed"** (think: heavy haul truck in low gear).
> - **"Synchro = Sync First, Spline Second"** (friction first, dog teeth second).
> - **"AMT = Automatic Manual Transmission"** (manual hardware, automatic control).
> - **"AT = Automatic with Torque converter"** (planetary + fluid coupling).
> - **"Monocoque = Mono body shell"** (one integrated load-bearing structure).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
