topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET613 - AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** E & E Architecture - ECUs, Sensors and Actuators Other Than ECM, Distributed and Zonal Electrical Architecture, Basics of Communication Protocols - CAN, LIN and Ethernet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 E&E (Electrical & Electronic) Architecture — Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Automotive Technology*, **E&E Architecture** is defined as the **structured, top-down design framework** that specifies the functional decomposition, logical interconnection, physical placement, and communication topology of all electrical and electronic components within a vehicle. It is the master blueprint that defines **what does what, where it sits, how it talks, and on which wire**.

Mathematically, an E&E Architecture can be represented as a tuple:

$$\mathcal{A} = \{F, H, C, P\}$$

Where:
- $F$ = Set of vehicle functions (powertrain, ADAS, body, chassis, infotainment)
- $H$ = Set of ECUs (Hardware/Compute nodes)
- $C$ = Set of communication channels (CAN, LIN, Ethernet, FlexRay)
- $P$ = Set of power distribution networks (12V, 48V, 400V/800V HV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** E&E Architecture is the *single most important* differentiator between vehicles of the same mechanical platform — it defines the "nervous system" of the modern automobile.

## 1.2 ECU (Electronic Control Unit)

An **ECU** is a ruggedized, automotive-grade embedded computer built around a microcontroller or microprocessor, equipped with dedicated sensor signal-conditioning circuitry, actuator driver stages, non-volatile memory, and one or more vehicle communication transceivers.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of an ECU as the **"specialist doctor"** in a hospital. The Engine Control Module (ECM) is like a cardiologist, the TCM is an orthopedist, the BCM is the general physician, and the ADAS ECU is the neurologist. Each has specialized sensors (stethoscope, X-ray) and actuators (medications, surgical tools), and they communicate with each other through a structured hospital information system (the in-vehicle network).

> [!NOTE]
> **Other than ECM**, the major ECUs studied in Module 4 include:
> - **TCM** — Transmission Control Module
> - **BCM** — Body Control Module
> - **ABS/ESC ECU** — Anti-lock Braking / Electronic Stability Control
> - **SRS / RCM** — Supplemental Restraint System / Restraint Control Module
> - **HVAC ECU** — Heating, Ventilation, Air-Conditioning
> - **ADAS ECU** — Advanced Driver Assistance Systems
> - **Cluster / IC** — Instrument Cluster
> - **IVI / HU** — In-Vehicle Infotainment / Head Unit
> - **Gateway ECU** — Cross-domain bridge

## 1.3 Sensors and Actuators (Other Than Engine Sensors)

**Sensors** are transducers that convert physical quantities (position, speed, temperature, pressure, light, acceleration, yaw) into standardized electrical signals (analog voltage, PWM, SENT, or digital).

**Actuators** are electro-mechanical devices that convert electrical commands into physical motion, force, light, or sound.

**Real-world analogy:** A sensor is the *human eye, ear, or skin* (input) while an actuator is the *human muscle or vocal cord* (output). The ECU is the *brain*.

| Category | Common Sensors (Non-Engine) | Common Actuators (Non-Engine) |
|---|---|---|
| Chassis | Wheel-speed, Yaw-rate, Steering-angle, Accelerometer | ABS Solenoid, Brake-by-wire motor, EPB actuator |
| Body | Rain/Light, Ambient T, Seat-occupancy, Door-pin | Door lock motor, Window lift, Mirror adjust, Wiper motor |
| ADAS | Camera, Radar, LiDAR, Ultrasonic | EPS motor, Active damper, Brake actuator |
| HVAC | Cabin T, Solar sensor, Humidity | Blower motor, Blend door actuator, Compressor clutch |
| Safety | Crash (G-sensor), Seatbelt buckle, Pedal position | Airbag squib, Pretensioner, Seatbelt retractor |

## 1.4 Distributed E&E Architecture

In a **Distributed Architecture**, each subsystem has its own dedicated ECU physically located near its sensors/actuators, and these ECUs communicate over multi-drop serial buses (CAN, LIN, FlexRay). It is the **classical automotive approach** used in vehicles from the 1990s to early 2020s.

**Analogy:** This is like an **old-fashioned village** where every house (ECU) has its own well (sensor) and stove (actuator), and villagers talk by shouting across the street (CAN bus). Works fine for small populations, but gets chaotic and noisy as the village grows.

## 1.5 Zonal E&E Architecture

In a **Zonal Architecture**, ECUs are reorganized by **physical zone of the car** (Front-Left, Front-Right, Rear-Left, Rear-Right, Central/Cockpit) rather than by function. Each zone contains a **Zonal Controller** (a powerful, multi-functional ECU) that aggregates all sensors/actuators in that zone and connects to a **centralized High-Performance Computer (HPC)** via high-speed Ethernet.

**Analogy:** This is like a **modern smart apartment building**. Each floor (zone) has a single floor manager (zonal controller) who handles all lights, doors, and AC for that floor, and reports up to the building's central command center (HPC). Less wiring, faster decisions, easier maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favorite:** Tesla Model 3, Volkswagen SSP platform, and Volvo SPA2 use **zonal architecture**. Traditional OEMs (Toyota, Hyundai, Tata, Maruti) are still on **distributed** for mass-market models.

## 1.6 Communication Protocols — Overview

| Protocol | Speed | Topology | Cost | Primary Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LIN** | up to **20 kbps** | Single-wire, Master-Slave | Very Low | Window, mirror, seat, wiper, sunroof |
| **CAN 2.0** | up to **1 Mbps** | Two-wire differential, Multi-master | Medium | Powertrain, Chassis, Body |
| **CAN FD** | up to **5 Mbps** | Two-wire differential, Multi-master | Medium | Modern powertrain, ADAS sensors |
| **CAN XL** | up to **10 Mbps** | Two-wire differential | Medium-High | Backbone & high-speed body |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** | **100 Mbps** | Single twisted pair, full-duplex | High | ADAS, backbone, infotainment |
| **Automotive Ethernet (1000BASE-T1)** | **1 Gbps** | Single twisted pair, full-duplex | Very High | HPC backbone, camera data |
| **FlexRay** | up to **10 Mbps** | Two channels, TDMA | High | Safety-critical chassis (legacy) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage levels (electrical standards):**
> - CAN recessive level: **≈ 2.5 V** (both CAN_H and CAN_L)
> - CAN dominant level: CAN_H ≈ **3.5 V**, CAN_L ≈ **1.5 V** → differential **≈ 2 V**
> - LIN bus high level: **> 0.8 × V_bat** (typically ≈ 12 V)
> - Automotive Ethernet uses **PAM-3** (100/1000BASE-T1) over a single twisted pair at ±1 V swing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ECU Internal Architecture (Layered View)

A modern automotive ECU (non-ECM) follows the **AUTOSAR Classic** 4-layer model:

1. **Application Layer (ASW)** — Software components, control algorithms (PID, lookup tables, state machines).
2. **Runtime Environment (RTE)** — Middleware that abstracts the hardware.
3. **Basic Software (BSW)** Layer 4:
   - **Services Layer** — OS, diagnostics (UDS), NVRAM, communication.
   - **ECU Abstraction Layer (MCAL above)** — Drivers for I/O, memory, communication.
   - **Microcontroller Abstraction Layer (MCAL)** — Register-level drivers.
   - **Complex Drivers (CDD)** — For sensors/actuators not standardized by AUTOSAR.
4. **Hardware (MCu)** — Microcontroller (Infineon Aurix TC3xx, NXP S32K3, Renesas RH850).

### Why Multi-layer?
The layered architecture enables **hardware-independent application software**, allowing OEMs to migrate code from one MCU to another with minimal change — a critical requirement for the 15+ year lifecycle of a vehicle.

## 2.2 Sensor Classification (Other Than Engine Sensors)

### A. By Output Signal Type

| Type | Working Principle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Analog** | Variable resistance / voltage | NTC thermistor, LDR |
| **Active Analog** | Generates own voltage (piezo) | Piezo knock, accelerometer |
| **Digital (Frequency/PWM)** | Variable frequency or duty cycle | Hall wheel-speed, SENT |
| **Digital (Bus)** | Serial protocol | Camera, Radar via Ethernet |

### B. Working Principles (Detailed)

**1. Hall-Effect Wheel Speed Sensor (ABS)**
A rotating toothed reluctor wheel modulates the magnetic flux through a Hall IC. Each tooth edge produces a digital pulse.

$$f = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi} \quad \text{[Hz]}$$

Where $N$ = number of teeth (e.g., 48), $\omega$ = angular velocity in rad/s.

**2. Yaw Rate Sensor (MEMS Vibratory Gyroscope)**
A proof mass driven at resonance along the drive axis; Coriolis force due to rotation produces a displacement proportional to angular rate.

$$\omega_z = \frac{F_{Coriolis}}{2 m v_d}$$

Where $m$ = proof mass, $v_d$ = drive velocity.

**3. Steering Angle Sensor (Optical Encoder)**
Multi-track Gray-code disc; absolute position over 360° with ±0.1° resolution.

**4. ADAS Camera (Image Sensor)**
CMOS active-pixel sensor; raw image output at 30–60 fps over MIPI-CSI2 or Ethernet.

## 2.3 Actuator Classification (Other Than Engine Actuators)

| Actuator | Energy Domain | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Solenoid** | Magnetic → Linear | ABS modulator valve, transmission shift |
| **DC Motor (Brushed)** | Electrical → Rotary | Window lift, seat adjust, blower |
| **BLDC Motor** | Electrical → Rotary (commutated) | EPS, HV compressor, water pump |
| **Relay** | Electromagnetic switch | Headlamp, horn, fan |
| **Piezo** | Electrical → Displacement | (Engine) — listed for completeness |
| **Stepper Motor** | Electrical → Precise rotation | HVAC blend/flap door |
| **LED Driver** | Electrical → Light | DRL, interior lighting |
| **Squib** | Pyrotechnic | Airbag inflator |

### Solenoid Force Equation (Actuator Analysis)

$$F = \frac{N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot \mu_0 \cdot A}{2 g^2}$$

Where $N$ = turns, $I$ = current, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $A$ = pole face area, $g$ = air gap.

## 2.4 Distributed vs. Zonal Architecture — Deep Comparison

| Parameter | Distributed | Zonal |
|---|---|---|
| ECU count | 80–150 | 15–40 |
| Harness length | 3–5 km | 1.5–2.5 km |
| Harness weight | 50–70 kg | 30–45 kg |
| Bus type | CAN / LIN heavy | Ethernet backbone |
| Software update | Module-by-module | OTA via central HPC |
| Feature add-on | Hardware change | Software change |
| Failure domain | Localized (per ECU) | Contained per zone |
| Cost (entry) | Low | High (initially) |
| Latency | Multi-hop | Single-hop to HPC |
| Example | Maruti Baleno | Tesla Model Y |

## 2.5 CAN Protocol — Deep Technical Analysis

**Controller Area Network (CAN)** was developed by **Bosch in 1986** and standardized as **ISO 11898**.

### 2.5.1 Physical Layer

- Two wires: **CAN_H (yellow)** and **CAN_L (green)**.
- Terminating resistors of **120 Ω** at each end (total ≈ 60 Ω between lines).
- Differential voltage $V_{diff} = V_{CAN\_H} - V_{CAN\_L}$
- Dominant bit ($V_{diff} \approx 2V$) represents logic **0**.
- Recessive bit ($V_{diff} \approx 0V$) represents logic **1**.
- Wired-AND logic: dominant overrides recessive.

### 2.5.2 Data Link Layer

- **CSMA/CR** — Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Resolution.
- **Non-destructive bitwise arbitration** — Node with lowest ID wins.
- **Bit stuffing** — After 5 consecutive identical bits, a stuff bit of opposite polarity is inserted.

### 2.5.3 CAN Bit Time Segments

The **Nominal Bit Time** is divided into four non-overlapping segments:

$$t_{Bit} = t_{Sync} + t_{Prop} + t_{Phase1} + t_{Phase2}$$

$$t_{Bit} = t_q \cdot (SYNC\_SEG + PROP\_SEG + PHASE\_SEG1 + PHASE\_SEG2)$$

Where $t_q$ = **time quantum** = $\frac{1}{f_{clk} / BRP}$ and $BRP$ = Baud Rate Prescaler.

- **SYNC_SEG** = 1 t_q (synchronization)
- **PROP_SEG** = 1–8 t_q (physical bus delay compensation)
- **PHASE_SEG1** = 1–8 t_q (edge phase shift)
- **PHASE_SEG2** = 1–8 t_q (max of PHASE_SEG2 and SJW)
- **SJW** (Synchronization Jump Width) = 1–4 t_q

### 2.5.4 Maximum Bus Length vs Bit Rate

Theoretical maximum CAN bus length (per ISO 11898):

$$L_{max} \approx \frac{40 \text{ m} \cdot 1 \text{ Mbps}}{f_{Bit}}$$

| Bit Rate | Max Length |
|---|---|
| **1 Mbps** | 40 m |
| **500 kbps** | 100 m |
| **250 kbps** | 250 m |
| **125 kbps** | 500 m |

### 2.5.5 Number of Nodes (CAN 2.0A)

$$N_{max} = 2^{11} = 2048 \quad (\text{11-bit ID})$$

$$N_{max} = 2^{29} \quad (\text{29-bit ID, theoretical, CAN 2.0B})$$

Practical limit due to bus loading: **~30 nodes** at 1 Mbps.

### 2.5.6 Frame Efficiency (Key KTU Formula)

$$\eta_{frame} = \frac{N_{data} \cdot t_{Bit}}{t_{Frame}}$$

For a standard CAN 2.0 frame with 8-byte data and worst-case bit stuffing (≈ 23 stuff bits in 108 raw bits):

$$t_{Frame} \approx 108 \cdot t_{Bit} = 108 \cdot \frac{1}{500\,000} = 216 \,\mu s$$

$$\eta = \frac{64}{108} \approx 59.3\%$$

### 2.5.7 Bus Load (Critical Real-World Metric)

$$L_{bus} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{Frame,i} \cdot f_{msg,i})}{t_{window}} \times 100\%$$

Recommended: keep **< 65%** for headroom; **< 50%** for safety-critical buses.

## 2.6 LIN Protocol — Deep Technical Analysis

**Local Interconnect Network (LIN)**, standardized as **ISO 17987**, was developed by the **LIN Consortium** (1999) as a low-cost sub-bus for non-critical body electronics.

### 2.6.1 Key Characteristics
- **Single-wire**, bus level follows battery voltage (**12 V nominal**).
- **Master-Slave**: 1 master + up to **15 slaves** (16 nodes total).
- **Bit rate**: 1 kbps to **20 kbps** (typically 19.2 kbps).
- **Synchronization**: master sends SYNC break + SYNC byte; slaves lock their baud rate to within ±2%.
- **Frame-based**: header from master, response from slave (or vice versa for slave-to-slave).

### 2.6.2 LIN Frame Format

$$t_{Frame,LIN} = t_{Header} + t_{Response}$$

Header = Break (≥ 13 bit) + Sync (0x55) + ID byte (6 ID bits + 2 parity bits).
Response = 2, 4, or 8 data bytes + 1 checksum byte.

### 2.6.3 LIN Bus Load Calculation

$$L_{LIN} = \frac{t_{Frame} \cdot f_{msg}}{1} \times 100\%$$

Strict limit: LIN typically **< 40%** to ensure deterministic latency.

## 2.7 Automotive Ethernet — Deep Technical Analysis

**Automotive Ethernet** uses **100BASE-T1** (IEEE 802.3bw, 100 Mbps) and **1000BASE-T1** (IEEE 802.3bp, 1 Gbps) over a **single twisted pair** with full-duplex, simultaneous transmit/receive.

### 2.7.1 Key Innovations
- **PAM-3 signaling**: 3 voltage levels (–1, 0, +1) carry **1.585 bits per symbol**.
- **Full-duplex** on single pair using echo cancellation (no CSMA/CD needed).
- **Latency**: 5–10 µs end-to-end (vs. 100+ µs on CAN due to arbitration).
- **Bandwidth efficiency**:

$$\eta_{Ethernet} = \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{overhead}}$$

For 1500-byte Ethernet frame: $\eta = 1500/1538 \approx 97.5\%$.

### 2.7.2 Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) — Subset for Real-Time

TSN (IEEE 802.1Qbv, 802.1Qbu) provides **deterministic latency** critical for ADAS:

- **Time-Aware Shaper (TAS)** reserves time slots for critical traffic.
- **Frame Preemption** allows high-priority frames to interrupt low-priority ones.

Maximum reserved latency with TSN: **< 100 µs** across 7 hops (validated in AVB/TSN profiles).

## 2.8 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{Bit, CAN} = t_q \cdot (1 + PROP + PH1 + PH2)$ | Nominal CAN bit time | seconds |
| 2 | $f_{Bit, CAN} = 1 / t_{Bit}$ | CAN bit rate | bps |
| 3 | $L_{max, CAN} \approx 40 / f_{Mbps}$ | Max CAN bus length | meters |
| 4 | $N_{max, CAN} = 2^{11}$ (Std) or $2^{29}$ (Ext) | Max nodes (theoretical) | nodes |
| 5 | $t_{Frame, CAN} \approx 108 \cdot t_{Bit}$ (Std, 8B data) | Frame transmission time | seconds |
| 6 | $\eta_{CAN} = N_{data} / N_{total}$ | Frame efficiency | % |
| 7 | $L_{bus} = \sum(t_{Frame,i} \cdot f_i) / t_{window}$ | Bus utilization | % |
| 8 | $t_{prop} = L \cdot 5 \text{ ns/m}$ | Propagation delay (twisted pair) | seconds |
| 9 | $f_{wheel} = N_{teeth} \cdot \omega / 2\pi$ | Wheel speed sensor frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $F_{solenoid} = \frac{N^2 I^2 \mu_0 A}{2 g^2}$ | Solenoid pull force | Newtons |
| 11 | $\eta_{Ethernet} = L_{payload} / L_{frame}$ | Ethernet frame efficiency | % |
| 12 | $t_{Bit, LIN} = 1 / 19\,200$ | LIN nominal bit time (≈ 52 µs) | seconds |
| 13 | $\Delta L_{harness} = \frac{L_{trad} - L_{zonal}}{L_{trad}} \times 100$ | Wiring harness weight reduction | % |
| 14 | $L_{max, LIN} \leq 40$ m | LIN bus length limit | meters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily by OEMs and Tier-1 suppliers (Bosch, Continental, Wipro, Tata Elxsi) for **bus load budgeting**, **ECU pin budgeting**, and **wiring harness optimization** during the early design phase — *before a single prototype is built*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CAN Bit Rate from Time Quanta

**Given:**
- Oscillator frequency $f_{clk} = 20$ MHz
- Baud Rate Prescaler $BRP = 4$
- Segments: $SYNC = 1$, $PROP = 2$, $PH1 = 3$, $PH2 = 3$ (in $t_q$)

**Find:** Nominal CAN bit rate.

### Step 1: Compute Time Quantum
The Time Quantum $t_q$ is the smallest unit of time in CAN bit timing, derived by dividing the system clock period by the Baud Rate Prescaler.

$$t_q = \frac{BRP}{f_{clk}}$$

Substituting values:

$$t_q = \frac{4}{20 \times 10^6} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

$$t_q = 200 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 2: Compute Nominal Bit Time
The Nominal Bit Time is the sum of all four bit time segments, each expressed as an integer multiple of $t_q$.

$$t_{Bit} = t_q \cdot (SYNC\_SEG + PROP\_SEG + PHASE\_SEG1 + PHASE\_SEG2)$$

Substituting the segment values:

$$t_{Bit} = 200 \text{ ns} \cdot (1 + 2 + 3 + 3)$$

$$t_{Bit} = 200 \text{ ns} \cdot 9$$

$$t_{Bit} = 1800 \text{ ns} = 1.8 \,\mu s$$

### Step 3: Compute Bit Rate
The bit rate is the reciprocal of the nominal bit time.

$$f_{Bit} = \frac{1}{t_{Bit}} = \frac{1}{1.8 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{Bit} \approx 555\,556 \text{ bps} \approx 555.56 \text{ kbps}$$

**Interpretation:** This configuration is commonly chosen to obtain a **near-500 kbps** bus (within the ±0.5% tolerance of the CAN standard for proper arbitration), used in most body and chassis CAN buses of modern passenger cars.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CAN Bus Load (Real-World Vehicle Scenario)

**Scenario:** A body CAN bus has three periodic messages from BCM and HVAC ECUs:

| Message | ID | DLC (bytes) | Period (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1: Door status | 0x120 | 2 | 20 |
| M2: Headlamp cmd | 0x230 | 3 | 50 |
| M3: HVAC setpoint | 0x310 | 5 | 100 |

Bus bit rate $f_{Bit} = 500$ kbps. Each frame has approximately **$47 + DLC \cdot 8 + 23$** raw bits (worst-case stuffing).

### Step 1: Bit Time

$$t_{Bit} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^3} = 2 \,\mu s$$

### Step 2: Frame Time for Each Message

**M1 (DLC = 2):**
Bits per frame = 47 + 2 × 8 + 23 = 47 + 16 + 23 = 86 bits
$$t_{Frame,M1} = 86 \cdot 2 \,\mu s = 172 \,\mu s$$

**M2 (DLC = 3):**
Bits per frame = 47 + 3 × 8 + 23 = 47 + 24 + 23 = 94 bits
$$t_{Frame,M2} = 94 \cdot 2 \,\mu s = 188 \,\mu s$$

**M3 (DLC = 5):**
Bits per frame = 47 + 5 × 8 + 23 = 47 + 40 + 23 = 110 bits
$$t_{Frame,M3} = 110 \cdot 2 \,\mu s = 220 \,\mu s$$

### Step 3: Frame Rates

$$f_{M1} = 1/0.020 = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{M2} = 1/0.050 = 20 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{M3} = 1/0.100 = 10 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 4: Aggregate Bus Load

$$L_{bus} = \sum_i (t_{Frame,i} \cdot f_i)$$

$$L_{bus} = (172 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50) + (188 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 20) + (220 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10)$$

$$L_{bus} = 8.6 \times 10^{-3} + 3.76 \times 10^{-3} + 2.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$L_{bus} = 14.56 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s/s}$$

$$L_{bus} = 1.456\%$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Bus load is **very low** (1.46%) → there is **plenty of headroom** for event-triggered messages (e.g., crash signal, anti-theft). This is well below the **65% rule-of-thumb** safety margin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Frame Efficiency for CAN FD

**CAN FD** (Flexible Data-rate, ISO 11898-1:2015) increases data payload from 8 bytes to **64 bytes** while raising the bit rate during the data phase.

**Given:** Arbitration phase = 500 kbps, Data phase = 2 Mbps, Payload = 64 bytes.

### Step 1: Arbitrate + Header Bits (fixed at 500 kbps)
Arbitration + Control + CRC fixed portion = approximately 27 bits at 500 kbps.

$$t_{arb} = \frac{27}{500 \times 10^3} = 54 \,\mu s$$

### Step 2: Data Bits at 2 Mbps
64 bytes × 8 = 512 bits + ~32 stuffing bits (worst case):

$$t_{data} = \frac{544}{2 \times 10^6} = 272 \,\mu s$$

### Step 3: CRC + Ack at 2 Mbps
~24 bits:
$$t_{CRC+Ack} = \frac{24}{2 \times 10^6} = 12 \,\mu s$$

### Step 4: Total Frame Time

$$t_{Frame,FD} = 54 + 272 + 12 = 338 \,\mu s$$

### Step 5: Efficiency

$$\eta_{FD} = \frac{512}{27 + 544 + 24} = \frac{512}{595} \approx 86.05\%$$

**Comparison:** vs. classic CAN at ~59% → **CAN FD is ~1.45× more efficient** in frame utilization, plus 8× more payload capacity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Ethernet Effective Throughput vs CAN

**Given:** 100BASE-T1 Automotive Ethernet, 1500-byte payload per frame.

### Step 1: Total Frame Size
Frame: Preamble (7) + SFD (1) + Header (14) + Payload (1500) + CRC (4) + IFG (12) = 1538 bytes
Total bits: 1538 × 8 = **12,304 bits**

### Step 2: Time to Transmit One Frame

$$t_{Frame,ETH} = \frac{12\,304}{100 \times 10^6} = 123.04 \,\mu s$$

### Step 3: Max Frame Rate

$$f_{Frame,ETH} = \frac{1}{123.04 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 8127 \text{ frames/s}$$

### Step 4: Throughput

$$\Theta = 8127 \cdot 1500 \cdot 8 = 97.5 \text{ Mbps}$$

This matches the nominal line rate — **Automotive Ethernet is ~98% efficient** compared to CAN's ~59%.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor → ECU → Actuator Mapping (Typical Modern Sedan)

| Function | Sensor | ECU | Actuator | Bus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS | Wheel-speed (Hall) | ABS ECU | Hydraulic modulator solenoid | CAN-FD Chassis |
| ESC | Yaw rate + Steering angle | ABS/ESC ECU | Brake + Engine torque request | CAN-FD Chassis |
| EPS | Torque sensor + Steering angle | EPS ECU | BLDC motor | CAN-FD Chassis |
| Airbag | Crash G-sensor (front/side) | RCM | Squib + Pretensioner | CAN-FD Chassis |
| HVAC | Cabin T + Solar | HVAC ECU | Blower + Blend door stepper | LIN → Body-CAN |
| Window | Hall position switch | Door Module | DC motor | LIN → Body-CAN |
| Mirror | Joystick switch | Mirror Module | DC motors (X/Y) | LIN → Body-CAN |
| Wiper | Rain sensor | BCM | DC motor + Park switch | LIN → Body-CAN |
| Camera (ADAS) | CMOS image sensor | ADAS ECU | — (visualization only) | Automotive Ethernet |
| Radar (ADAS) | 77 GHz MMIC | Radar ECU | — | Automotive Ethernet |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Distributed E&E Architecture (Classical Approach)

```mermaid
graph TB
    Power[12V Battery + DC-DC]:::pow

```

## 4.2 Zonal E&E Architecture (Modern / Future Approach)


## 4.3 CAN Standard Frame Structure (11-bit ID)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SOF[SOF 1 bit Dominant]:::start
    ARB[Arbitration Field 11 bit ID + RTR 1 bit]:::arbit
    CTRL[Control Field IDE r0 DLC 4 bits]:::ctrl

    CRC[CRC Field 15 bits + Delimiter 1]:::crc

    SOF --> ARB
    ARB --> CTRL
    CTRL --> DATA
    DATA --> CRC
    CRC --> ACK
    ACK --> EOF
    EOF --> IFS
```
